verses.text: and the Horites in their Mount Seir, to El Paran, which is by the wilderness.
verses.explainer: They defeat the Horites in their hill country of Seir (later Edom) and press as far as El-paran by the wilderness. The eastern kings sweep through the entire region.

verses.text: They returned, and came to En Mishpat (also called Kadesh), and struck all the country of the Amalekites, and also the Amorites, that lived in Hazazon Tamar.
verses.explainer: They turn back and conquer En-mishpat (Kadesh) and all the territory of the Amalekites and Amorites. The campaign is comprehensive.

verses.text: The king of Sodom, and the king of Gomorrah, and the king of Admah, and the king of Zeboiim, and the king of Bela (also called Zoar) went out; and they set the battle in array against them in the valley of Siddim;
verses.explainer: The five kings of Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboiim, and Zoar march out and draw up battle lines in the Valley of Siddim.

verses.text: against Chedorlaomer king of Elam, and Tidal king of Goiim, and Amraphel king of Shinar, and Arioch king of Ellasar; four kings against the five.
verses.explainer: Four kings against five -- but the five are outmatched.

verses.text: Now the valley of Siddim was full of tar pits; and the kings of Sodom and Gomorrah fled, and some fell there, and those who remained fled to the hills.
verses.explainer: The Valley of Siddim is full of bitumen (tar) pits. The kings of Sodom and Gomorrah flee and fall into them; the rest flee to the mountains.

verses.text: They took all the goods of Sodom and Gomorrah, and all their food, and went their way.
verses.explainer: The eastern kings seize all the goods and food of Sodom and Gomorrah and depart.

verses.text: They took Lot, Abram’s brother’s son, who lived in Sodom, and his goods, and departed.
verses.explainer: They also take Lot and his possessions. Lot's choice to live near Sodom has consequences -- when Sodom falls, Lot falls with it.

verses.text: One who had escaped came and told Abram, the Hebrew. At that time, he lived by the oaks of Mamre, the Amorite, brother of Eshcol, and brother of Aner; and they were allies of Abram.
verses.explainer: An escapee tells Abram the Hebrew. This is the first time the word 'Hebrew' is used in the Bible. It may derive from Eber (10:21) or from a word meaning 'one who crosses over.'

verses.text: When Abram heard that his relative was taken captive, he led out his trained men, born in his house, three hundred and eighteen, and pursued as far as Dan.
verses.explainer: When Abram hears that Lot has been captured, he leads 318 trained men born in his household on a rescue mission. This tells us Abram's household is enormous -- more like a small tribe than a family. He pursues the kings as far as Dan.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': '318 trained men', 'explanation': "Abram had a personal militia of trained warriors born in his household -- not hired soldiers but retainers raised from birth. This reveals Abram as a powerful chieftain, not a simple nomad. The number 318 has been noted: in later Jewish numerology, 318 is the numerical value of Eliezer, Abram's chief servant."}

verses.text: He divided himself against them by night, he and his servants, and struck them, and pursued them to Hobah, which is on the left hand of Damascus.
verses.explainer: Abram divides his forces and attacks at night, routing the eastern kings and pursuing them north of Damascus. Military genius: night attacks with divided forces create confusion and the impression of a larger army.

verses.text: He brought back all the goods, and also brought back his relative, Lot, and his goods, and the women also, and the other people.
verses.explainer: Abram recovers all the goods, his kinsman Lot, Lot's possessions, and all the people. The rescue is complete.

verses.text: The king of Sodom went out to meet him after his return from the slaughter of Chedorlaomer and the kings who were with him, at the valley of Shaveh (that is, the King’s Valley).
verses.explainer: The king of Sodom comes out to meet Abram after his return from the victory. The stage is set for two encounters that define two approaches to life.

verses.text: Melchizedek king of Salem brought out bread and wine: and he was priest of God Most High.
verses.explainer: Melchizedek, king of Salem, brings out bread and wine. He is priest of God Most High. This figure appears without introduction, without genealogy, without explanation. He is simultaneously king and priest -- a combination forbidden in later Israelite law.

thenVsNow.then: Salem is traditionally identified with Jerusalem. If so, Melchizedek is the king of the city where David will later reign and Solomon will build the Temple. Abram pays homage to the king of the future holy city.
thenVsNow.now: Melchizedek has fascinated theologians for millennia. He represents a priesthood that predates and transcends the Levitical system. For Christians, he points to Christ's priesthood -- based not on lineage but on the power of an indestructible life.

verses.text: He blessed him, and said, “Blessed be Abram of God Most High, possessor of heaven and earth:
verses.explainer: Melchizedek blesses Abram: 'Blessed be Abram by God Most High, Possessor of heaven and earth.' The blessing recognizes Abram's God as the supreme deity.

verses.text:
and blessed be God Most High, who has delivered your enemies into your hand.”
Abram gave him a tenth of all.
verses.explainer: Melchizedek blesses God Most High for delivering Abram's enemies. Abram gives Melchizedek a tenth of everything. The first tithe in the Bible is given by the covenant patriarch to a mysterious priest-king.

verses.text: The king of Sodom said to Abram, “Give me the people, and take the goods for yourself.”
verses.explainer: The king of Sodom says to Abram: 'Give me the people, and take the goods for yourself.' He offers Abram the plunder of war -- a normal and expected arrangement.

verses.text: Abram said to the king of Sodom, “I have lifted up my hand to Yahweh, God Most High, possessor of heaven and earth,
verses.explainer: Abram tells the king of Sodom: 'I have lifted my hand to the LORD, God Most High, Possessor of heaven and earth.' Abram has made a vow.

verses.text: that I will not take a thread nor a sandal strap nor anything that is yours, lest you should say, ‘I have made Abram rich.’
verses.explainer: Abram refuses to take even a thread or a sandal strap from the king of Sodom, so that Sodom cannot say 'I have made Abram rich.' Abram's wealth will come from God, not from Sodom. He accepts blessing from Melchizedek (God's priest) but refuses enrichment from Sodom (the corrupt city).

verses.text: I will accept nothing from you except that which the young men have eaten, and the portion of the men who went with me: Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre. Let them take their portion.”
verses.explainer: Abram says his young men may take their share, and his allies Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre may take their portion. He will not bind others to his vow, but he himself will take nothing from Sodom.

explainer.summary: This is one of the most theologically significant chapters in the Bible. God appears to Abram in a vision, promising to be his shield and reward. Abram voices his deepest fear: he has no heir. God takes him outside, shows him the stars, and promises offspring as numerous as the stars. Abram believes, and God credits it to him as righteousness. Then God makes a formal covenant, passing between cut animals in a ceremony that places the entire covenant obligation on himself.
themes: God bearing the covenant curse alone
The prophecy of Egyptian slavery and exodus
explainer.contextInBook: After Abram's military victory, God addresses his deepest anxiety: childlessness. This chapter contains the two pillars of Abrahamic theology: justification by faith (v. 6) and the unconditional covenant (vv. 9-21).

wordStudies.translatedAs: To consider firm, reliable, trustworthy
wordStudies.meaning: To consider firm, reliable, trustworthy. From the root aman, from which we get 'Amen.' Abram did not merely agree with information; he put his full weight on God's promise. He trusted God's character.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Right standing, being in correct relationship
wordStudies.meaning: Right standing, being in correct relationship. When God credited Abram's faith as righteousness, he declared that Abram was in right relationship with him -- not because of works but because of trust. This verse becomes the foundation of Paul's theology of justification.

verses.text: After these things Yahweh’s word came to Abram in a vision, saying, “Don’t be afraid, Abram. I am your shield, your exceedingly great reward.”
verses.explainer: After these things, the word of the LORD comes to Abram in a vision: 'Fear not, Abram, I am your shield; your reward shall be very great.' After the battle with the kings and the refusal of Sodom's wealth, God assures Abram that he has not lost out -- God himself is the reward.

verses.text: Abram said, “Lord Yahweh, what will you give me, since I go childless, and he who will inherit my estate is Eliezer of Damascus?”
verses.explainer: Abram responds with raw honesty: 'O Lord GOD, what will you give me, for I continue childless, and the heir of my house is Eliezer of Damascus?' The promise of a great nation means nothing without an heir. Abram names the problem that haunts him.

verses.text: Abram said, “Behold, to me you have given no children: and, behold, one born in my house is my heir.”
verses.explainer: Abram says: 'You have given me no offspring, and a servant born in my house will be my heir.' He assumes God's promise will be fulfilled through adoption rather than biology. His expectations are too small.

verses.text: Behold, Yahweh’s word came to him, saying, “This man will not be your heir, but he who will come out of your own body will be your heir.”
verses.explainer: The word of the LORD comes: 'This man shall not be your heir; your very own son shall be your heir.' God corrects Abram's assumption. The promise is for a biological son.

verses.text: Yahweh brought him outside, and said, “Look now toward the sky, and count the stars, if you are able to count them.” He said to Abram, “So will your offspring be.”
verses.explainer: God takes Abram outside and says: 'Look toward heaven and number the stars, if you are able to number them. So shall your offspring be.' Under the vast ancient sky, unpolluted by light, the stars would have been staggering. God asks an old, childless man to believe that his descendants will be uncountable.

verses.text: He believed in Yahweh, who credited it to him for righteousness.
verses.explainer: And Abram believed the LORD, and he counted it to him as righteousness. This is one of the most important verses in the Bible. Paul builds his entire theology of justification by faith on this sentence. Abram did not earn right standing with God through works, rituals, or obedience -- he simply believed God's promise.

thenVsNow.then: In the ancient world, right standing with the gods was achieved through ritual performance, sacrificial offerings, and moral behavior. The idea that simply trusting God's word could establish right relationship was revolutionary.
thenVsNow.now: The Protestant Reformation was largely a debate about this verse. Luther and the Reformers argued that Genesis 15:6 establishes the principle of sola fide -- faith alone as the basis of right standing with God.

verses.text: He said to Abram, “I am Yahweh who brought you out of Ur of the Chaldees, to give you this land to inherit it.”
verses.explainer: God identifies himself: 'I am the LORD who brought you out of Ur of the Chaldeans to give you this land to possess it.' The self-identification echoes the later formula 'I am the LORD who brought you out of Egypt.'

verses.text: He said, “Lord Yahweh, how will I know that I will inherit it?”
verses.explainer: Abram asks: 'O Lord GOD, how am I to know that I shall possess it?' This is not doubt but a request for assurance. Abram believed about the offspring; now he asks for confirmation about the land.

verses.text: He said to him, “Bring me a heifer three years old, a female goat three years old, a ram three years old, a turtledove, and a young pigeon.”
verses.explainer: God instructs Abram to bring a heifer, a goat, a ram, a turtledove, and a pigeon. These are the animals later used in Israel's sacrificial system. God is preparing a covenant ceremony.

verses.text: He brought him all these, and divided them in the middle, and laid each half opposite the other; but he didn’t divide the birds.
verses.explainer: Abram cuts the animals in half and arranges the halves opposite each other, but he does not cut the birds. This is a covenant-making ritual well-attested in the ancient Near East.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Cutting a covenant', 'explanation': "The Hebrew phrase for making a covenant is literally 'cutting a covenant' (karat berit). The ceremony involved cutting animals in half and walking between the pieces. The meaning was self-imprecatory: 'May what happened to these animals happen to me if I break this covenant.' It was the most solemn commitment possible."}

verses.text: The birds of prey came down on the carcasses, and Abram drove them away.
verses.explainer: Birds of prey come down on the carcasses, and Abram drives them away. He guards the covenant sacrifice against defilement.

verses.text: When the sun was going down, a deep sleep fell on Abram. Now terror and great darkness fell on him.
verses.explainer: As the sun is going down, a deep sleep (tardemah) falls on Abram, along with a great dreadful darkness. The same divine sleep that fell on Adam (2:21). God is about to do something that requires Abram to be passive.

verses.text: He said to Abram, “Know for sure that your offspring will live as foreigners in a land that is not theirs, and will serve them. They will afflict them four hundred years.
verses.explainer: God tells Abram that his offspring will be sojourners in a land not their own, where they will be enslaved and oppressed for 400 years. This is the prophecy of the Egyptian slavery that will not be fulfilled for centuries.

verses.text: I will also judge that nation, whom they will serve. Afterward they will come out with great wealth,
verses.explainer: God will judge the nation that enslaves them, and afterward they will come out with great possessions. The Exodus is promised here, centuries before it happens.

verses.text: but you will go to your fathers in peace. You will be buried at a good old age.
verses.explainer: Abram himself will die in peace at a good old age. He will not see the promise fulfilled, but he will be at peace.

verses.text: In the fourth generation they will come here again, for the iniquity of the Amorite is not yet full.”
verses.explainer: In the fourth generation, his descendants will return, for 'the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet complete.' God delays giving Israel the land because the current inhabitants have not yet exhausted his patience. Even judgment on Canaan is measured and just.

verses.text: It came to pass that, when the sun went down, and it was dark, behold, a smoking furnace, and a flaming torch passed between these pieces.
verses.explainer: After sunset, a smoking fire pot and a flaming torch pass between the pieces. These represent God's presence. In a normal covenant ceremony, both parties would walk between the pieces. Here, only God passes through -- he takes the entire obligation on himself. If the covenant is broken, God will pay the price.

thenVsNow.then: Both parties passing between cut animals meant both were bound by the covenant. God passing through alone was unprecedented: it meant the covenant's success depended entirely on God, not on Abram. Abram could not break it because Abram was not a party to the self-curse.
thenVsNow.now: Christians see in this passage a foreshadowing of the cross: God ultimately did bear the curse of the broken covenant in Christ. The one who passed between the pieces eventually passed through death itself.

verses.text: In that day Yahweh made a covenant with Abram, saying, “I have given this land to your offspring, from the river of Egypt to the great river, the river Euphrates:
verses.explainer: On that day, the LORD makes a covenant with Abram: 'To your offspring I give this land, from the river of Egypt to the great river, the Euphrates.' The land promise is given its fullest geographic extent.

verses.text: the Kenites, the Kenizzites, the Kadmonites,
verses.explainer: The land of the Kenites, Kenizzites, and Kadmonites.

verses.text: the Hittites, the Perizzites, the Rephaim,
verses.explainer: The Hittites, Perizzites, and Rephaim.

verses.text: the Amorites, the Canaanites, the Girgashites, and the Jebusites.”
verses.explainer: The Amorites, Canaanites, Girgashites, and Jebusites. Ten nations are named -- their land will become Israel's inheritance.

explainer.summary: Ten years in Canaan and still no child. Sarai proposes that Abram have a child through her Egyptian servant Hagar -- a culturally acceptable surrogate arrangement. Abram agrees, Hagar conceives, and the household explodes. Hagar despises Sarai, Sarai blames Abram, Abram deflects, and Sarai mistreats Hagar until she flees. God finds Hagar in the wilderness, commands her to return, and promises that her son Ishmael will father a great nation. Hagar names God 'the God who sees me.'
themes: Surrogate motherhood in the ancient world
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter shows the consequences of trying to fulfill God's promise through human engineering. The Hagar-Sarah conflict will echo through the ages in the rivalry between Isaac and Ishmael.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The God who sees
wordStudies.meaning: The God who sees. Hagar's name for God, born from her experience of being seen and heard when she was invisible and forgotten. She is the only person in the Bible who gives God a new name.

verses.text: Now Sarai, Abram’s wife, bore him no children. She had a servant, an Egyptian, whose name was Hagar.
verses.explainer: Sarai has borne no children. She has an Egyptian servant named Hagar. The detail that Hagar is Egyptian may connect her to Abram's time in Egypt (12:16) -- perhaps she was acquired there.

verses.text: Sarai said to Abram, “See now, Yahweh has restrained me from bearing. Please go in to my servant. It may be that I will obtain children by her.” Abram listened to the voice of Sarai.
verses.explainer: Sarai says to Abram: 'The LORD has prevented me from having children. Go in to my servant; perhaps I shall obtain children through her.' This is not adultery in the ancient context -- it was a legal and culturally accepted practice for obtaining an heir through a servant.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Surrogate motherhood', 'explanation': "Marriage contracts from ancient Nuzi (northern Mesopotamia) explicitly required barren wives to provide a servant as surrogate. The resulting child was legally the wife's. Sarai is following established custom, not inventing a solution. But custom and faith are not the same thing."}

verses.text: Sarai, Abram’s wife, took Hagar the Egyptian, her servant, after Abram had lived ten years in the land of Canaan, and gave her to Abram her husband to be his wife.
verses.explainer: Abram listens to Sarai's voice. The phrase echoes Adam 'listening to the voice' of Eve in 3:17. Both times, listening to a wife's practical suggestion rather than waiting for God's plan leads to complications.

verses.text: He went in to Hagar, and she conceived. When she saw that she had conceived, her mistress was despised in her eyes.
verses.explainer: Abram goes in to Hagar, and she conceives. When Hagar realizes she is pregnant, she looks with contempt on Sarai. The power dynamic shifts: the fertile servant now has what the barren mistress does not.

verses.text: Sarai said to Abram, “This wrong is your fault. I gave my servant into your bosom, and when she saw that she had conceived, I was despised in her eyes. Yahweh judge between me and you.”
verses.explainer: Sarai blames Abram: 'May the wrong done to me be on you!' She proposed the arrangement, but now that it has backfired, she holds Abram responsible. The blame-shifting echoes Genesis 3.

verses.text: But Abram said to Sarai, “Behold, your maid is in your hand. Do to her whatever is good in your eyes.” Sarai dealt harshly with her, and she fled from her face.
verses.explainer: Abram tells Sarai to do whatever she wants with Hagar. He refuses to protect either woman. Sarai mistreats Hagar, and Hagar flees. The patriarch fails both women.

verses.text: Yahweh’s angel found her by a fountain of water in the wilderness, by the fountain on the way to Shur.
verses.explainer: The angel of the LORD finds Hagar by a spring of water in the wilderness, on the way to Shur (toward Egypt). She is heading home. This is the first appearance of the 'angel of the LORD' in the Bible, and remarkably, it is to an Egyptian slave woman, not to a patriarch.

verses.text:
He said, “Hagar, Sarai’s servant, where did you come from? Where are you going?”
She said, “I am fleeing from the face of my mistress Sarai.”
verses.explainer: The angel asks: 'Hagar, servant of Sarai, where have you come from and where are you going?' He knows her name and her status. The question invites her to name her pain.

verses.text: Yahweh’s angel said to her, “Return to your mistress, and submit yourself under her hands.”
verses.explainer: The angel tells Hagar to return and submit to Sarai. This is a hard command. Returning to suffering is not a fairy-tale resolution. But God has plans for Hagar that require her to be in Abraham's household.

verses.text: Yahweh’s angel said to her, “I will greatly multiply your offspring, that they will not be counted for multitude.”
verses.explainer: The angel promises Hagar offspring so numerous they cannot be counted. This echoes the promise made to Abram in 13:16 and 15:5. Hagar receives her own version of the Abrahamic blessing.

verses.text: Yahweh’s angel said to her, “Behold, you are with child, and will bear a son. You shall call his name Ishmael, because Yahweh has heard your affliction.
verses.explainer: The angel tells Hagar she is pregnant with a son and shall call him Ishmael -- 'God hears' -- because the LORD has listened to her affliction. God names the child after his response to a suffering woman's cries.

verses.text: He will be like a wild donkey among men. His hand will be against every man, and every man’s hand against him. He will live opposite all of his brothers.”
verses.explainer: Ishmael will be a 'wild donkey of a man' -- free, untamed, independent. He will live in conflict with his brothers. This is not a curse but a description of a nomadic warrior people.

thenVsNow.then: The wild donkey (pere) was admired in the ancient Near East for its freedom and endurance in the desert. Calling Ishmael a wild donkey was describing a fierce, independent spirit.
thenVsNow.now: Ishmael is honored as an ancestor in Islam, and the Ishmaelite peoples became the Arab nations. The 'conflict with brothers' has been read -- sometimes problematically -- as a prophecy of the ongoing tension between Isaac's and Ishmael's descendants.

verses.text: She called the name of Yahweh who spoke to her, “You are a God who sees,” for she said, “Have I even stayed alive after seeing him?”
verses.explainer: Hagar gives God a name: El Roi, 'the God who sees.' She says 'Have I really seen God and remained alive after seeing him?' An Egyptian slave woman names God. She is the only person in the Bible to do so.

thenVsNow.then: In the ancient world, slaves were invisible -- property, not persons. That God sought out, spoke to, and made promises to a runaway slave woman was culturally shocking. Hagar is the first person in the Bible to receive a divine messenger.
thenVsNow.now: Hagar's story resonates with anyone who has felt invisible, abandoned, or trapped by circumstances beyond their control. The God who sees the outcast remains a powerful theological claim.

verses.text: Therefore the well was called Beer Lahai Roi. Behold, it is between Kadesh and Bered.
verses.explainer: The well is called Beer-lahai-roi -- 'the well of the Living One who sees me.' The place is named for the encounter. Hagar marks the map.

verses.text: Hagar bore a son for Abram. Abram called the name of his son, whom Hagar bore, Ishmael.
verses.explainer: Hagar bears Abram a son, and Abram names him Ishmael, as the angel instructed. Abram accepts the name Hagar was given, acknowledging God's encounter with her.

verses.text: Abram was eighty-six years old when Hagar bore Ishmael to Abram.
verses.explainer: Abram is 86 years old when Hagar bears Ishmael. Thirteen years will pass before God speaks again.

explainer.summary: Thirteen years of silence end when God appears to 99-year-old Abram and expands the covenant. God changes Abram's name to Abraham ('father of a multitude') and Sarai's to Sarah ('princess'). He establishes circumcision as the covenant sign -- a permanent mark in the flesh of every male. God specifically promises that Sarah will bear a son named Isaac, through whom the covenant will continue. Abraham laughs at the impossibility and asks God to bless Ishmael instead. God promises to bless Ishmael but insists the covenant goes through Isaac.
themes: Laughter as a response to God's promises
explainer.contextInBook: After the detour through Hagar and Ishmael, God clarifies: the covenant heir will come through Sarah. The name changes signal that the promise has entered a new phase. Abraham and Sarah are being prepared for the impossible.

wordStudies.translatedAs: God Almighty
wordStudies.meaning: God Almighty. The meaning of Shaddai is debated: it may come from a word for mountain (the God of the mountain), from a word for breast (the God who nourishes), or from a word meaning 'the one who is sufficient.' It emphasizes God's power to do what seems impossible.

verses.text: When Abram was ninety-nine years old, Yahweh appeared to Abram and said to him, “I am God Almighty. Walk before me, and be blameless.
verses.explainer: When Abram is 99, the LORD appears and says: 'I am God Almighty (El Shaddai). Walk before me and be blameless.' After thirteen years of silence, God returns with both a name and a command. The name reveals power; the command requires integrity.

verses.text: I will make my covenant between me and you, and will multiply you exceedingly.”
verses.explainer: God promises to make his covenant between himself and Abraham and to multiply him greatly. The covenant is being formalized.

verses.text: Abram fell on his face. God talked with him, saying,
verses.explainer: Abram falls facedown. The physical posture reflects the gravity of the encounter.

verses.text: “As for me, behold, my covenant is with you. You will be the father of a multitude of nations.
verses.explainer: God says: 'As for me, my covenant is with you, and you shall be the father of a multitude of nations.' God states his obligation first. The covenant begins with what God commits to do.

verses.text: Your name will no more be called Abram, but your name will be Abraham; for I have made you the father of a multitude of nations.
verses.explainer: Abram's name is changed to Abraham. Abram means 'exalted father'; Abraham means 'father of a multitude.' God gives a childless 99-year-old a name that means 'father of many nations.' Every time anyone says his name, the promise is spoken.

verses.text: I will make you exceedingly fruitful, and I will make nations of you. Kings will come out of you.
verses.explainer: God will make Abraham exceedingly fruitful. Nations and kings will come from him.

verses.text: I will establish my covenant between me and you and your offspring after you throughout their generations for an everlasting covenant, to be a God to you and to your offspring after you.
verses.explainer: God establishes an everlasting covenant between himself and Abraham and his descendants throughout their generations. The covenant is permanent and multigenerational.

verses.text: I will give to you, and to your offspring after you, the land where you are traveling, all the land of Canaan, for an everlasting possession. I will be their God.”
verses.explainer: God will give the entire land of Canaan as an everlasting possession. The land promise is now tied to the everlasting covenant.

verses.text: God said to Abraham, “As for you, you will keep my covenant, you and your offspring after you throughout their generations.
verses.explainer: God tells Abraham to keep the covenant -- he and his descendants throughout their generations. The human obligation begins.

verses.text: This is my covenant, which you shall keep, between me and you and your offspring after you. Every male among you shall be circumcised.
verses.explainer: Every male shall be circumcised. This is the sign of the covenant between God and Abraham.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Circumcision', 'explanation': 'Circumcision was practiced in the ancient Near East, particularly in Egypt, but usually as a puberty rite or priestly requirement. God transforms it into a covenant sign applied to infants on the eighth day. The mark in the flesh of the reproductive organ connects the covenant to the promise of descendants.'}

verses.text: You shall be circumcised in the flesh of your foreskin. It will be a token of the covenant between me and you.
verses.explainer: The flesh of the foreskin shall be circumcised as a sign of the covenant. A physical mark that cannot be undone, hidden from public view, affecting the organ of reproduction. The sign is intimate, permanent, and connected to the promise of offspring.

verses.text: He who is eight days old will be circumcised among you, every male throughout your generations, he who is born in the house, or bought with money from any foreigner who is not of your offspring.
verses.explainer: Every male eight days old shall be circumcised, including those born in the household and those bought with money from foreigners. The covenant community includes non-Israelites -- it is not purely ethnic.

verses.text: He who is born in your house, and he who is bought with your money, must be circumcised. My covenant will be in your flesh for an everlasting covenant.
verses.explainer: Both those born in the house and those bought with money must be circumcised. The covenant is etched in flesh -- an everlasting covenant requires a permanent sign.

verses.text: The uncircumcised male who is not circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin, that soul shall be cut off from his people. He has broken my covenant.”
verses.explainer: Any uncircumcised male shall be cut off from his people -- he has broken the covenant. The consequence is exile from the community.

verses.text: God said to Abraham, “As for Sarai your wife, you shall not call her name Sarai, but her name will be Sarah.
verses.explainer: Sarai's name is changed to Sarah. Both names mean 'princess,' but the change parallels Abraham's and signals her new status. She is not just Abraham's wife but a matriarch in her own right.

verses.text: I will bless her, and moreover I will give you a son by her. Yes, I will bless her, and she will be a mother of nations. Kings of peoples will come from her.”
verses.explainer: God will bless Sarah and give Abraham a son by her. She will be the mother of nations; kings will come from her. The promise is no longer just about Abraham's offspring -- Sarah's role is essential and specific.

verses.text: Then Abraham fell on his face, and laughed, and said in his heart, “Will a child be born to him who is one hundred years old? Will Sarah, who is ninety years old, give birth?”
verses.explainer: Abraham falls on his face and laughs. He thinks: 'Shall a child be born to a man who is a hundred years old? Shall Sarah, who is ninety, bear a child?' The laughter is honest disbelief at the biological impossibility.

thenVsNow.then: In the ancient world, having children past a certain age was unthinkable. Abraham and Sarah are well past any natural hope of conception. The promise contradicts every known biological fact.
thenVsNow.now: Abraham's laughter names the fundamental posture of faith: believing something that your rational mind says is impossible. The child will be named Isaac ('he laughs') as a permanent memorial to this moment.

verses.text: Abraham said to God, “Oh that Ishmael might live before you!”
verses.explainer: Abraham says: 'Oh that Ishmael might live before you!' He wants God to fulfill the promise through the son he already has. God has a different plan.

verses.text: God said, “No, but Sarah, your wife, will bear you a son. You shall call his name Isaac. I will establish my covenant with him for an everlasting covenant for his offspring after him.
verses.explainer: God says: 'No, but Sarah your wife shall bear you a son, and you shall call his name Isaac.' God insists on the impossible. The covenant goes through the miraculous child, not the natural one.

verses.text: As for Ishmael, I have heard you. Behold, I have blessed him, and will make him fruitful, and will multiply him exceedingly. He will become the father of twelve princes, and I will make him a great nation.
verses.explainer: God hears Abraham's prayer for Ishmael: he will bless Ishmael, make him fruitful, multiply him greatly, and make him the father of twelve princes and a great nation. Ishmael is blessed but not the covenant heir.

verses.text: But my covenant I establish with Isaac, whom Sarah will bear to you at this set time next year.”
verses.explainer: But God's covenant will be established with Isaac, whom Sarah will bear at this appointed time next year. The specificity is striking: not someday, but next year.

verses.text: When he finished talking with him, God went up from Abraham.
verses.explainer: God finishes speaking and goes up from Abraham. The encounter ends.

verses.text: Abraham took Ishmael his son, all who were born in his house, and all who were bought with his money; every male among the men of Abraham’s house, and circumcised the flesh of their foreskin in the same day, as God had said to him.
verses.explainer: That very day, Abraham circumcises Ishmael and every male in his household. Immediate obedience -- no waiting, no hesitation.

verses.text: Abraham was ninety-nine years old, when he was circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin.
verses.explainer: Abraham is 99 when he is circumcised.

verses.text: Ishmael, his son, was thirteen years old when he was circumcised in the flesh of his foreskin.
verses.explainer: Ishmael is 13 when he is circumcised.

verses.text: In the same day both Abraham and Ishmael, his son, were circumcised.
verses.explainer: Abraham and Ishmael are circumcised on the same day.

verses.text: All the men of his house, those born in the house, and those bought with money from a foreigner, were circumcised with him.
verses.explainer: All the men of his household, born there or bought from foreigners, are circumcised with him. The entire household enters the covenant together.

explainer.summary: Three mysterious visitors appear at Abraham's tent by the oaks of Mamre. Abraham shows extravagant hospitality, and the visitors announce that Sarah will have a son within a year. Sarah, listening from the tent, laughs. The visitors then reveal that they are heading to Sodom to investigate its wickedness. Abraham engages in one of the most remarkable negotiations in the Bible, bargaining God down from fifty righteous to ten as the threshold for sparing Sodom.
themes: The justice and mercy of God
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter brilliantly weaves together the intimate (Sarah's laughter, the promise of a child) and the cosmic (the fate of Sodom). Abraham stands between God and the doomed cities, negotiating for mercy.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Abraham's bargaining with God for Sodom is the fir
wordStudies.meaning: Abraham's bargaining with God for Sodom is the first intercessory prayer in the Bible. He does not pray for himself but for others, appealing to God's justice: 'Shall not the Judge of all the earth do right?' This becomes a model for prophetic intercession throughout Scripture.

verses.text: Yahweh appeared to him by the oaks of Mamre, as he sat in the tent door in the heat of the day.
verses.explainer: The LORD appears to Abraham by the oaks of Mamre while he sits at the entrance of his tent in the heat of the day. The opening tells us this is a divine appearance, though Abraham may not realize it immediately.

verses.text: He lifted up his eyes and looked, and saw that three men stood opposite him. When he saw them, he ran to meet them from the tent door, and bowed himself to the earth,
verses.explainer: Abraham looks up and sees three men standing near him. He runs from the tent entrance to meet them and bows to the ground. The identity of the three visitors has been debated: two angels and the LORD? Three manifestations of God? The Trinity? The text is deliberately ambiguous.

verses.text: and said, “My lord, if now I have found favor in your sight, please don’t go away from your servant.
verses.explainer: Abraham says: 'My lord, if I have found favor in your eyes, do not pass by your servant.' He begs them to stay. In the ancient world, offering hospitality to travelers was a sacred obligation.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Hospitality', 'explanation': "In the ancient Near East, hospitality was not optional -- it was a moral and religious obligation. Refusing to host travelers was shameful. Abraham's elaborate reception (washing feet, preparing a feast) was the gold standard. The contrast with Sodom's reception of visitors in chapter 19 is the point."}

verses.text: Now let a little water be fetched, wash your feet, and rest yourselves under the tree.
verses.explainer: Abraham offers water to wash their feet and a place to rest under a tree. Foot-washing was the first act of hospitality for dusty travelers.

verses.text:
I will get a morsel of bread so you can refresh your heart. After that you may go your way, now that you have come to your servant.”
They said, “Very well, do as you have said.”
verses.explainer: Abraham offers bread as a 'morsel' -- a modest undersell. What he actually prepares is a feast.

verses.text: Abraham hurried into the tent to Sarah, and said, “Quickly prepare three seahs of fine meal, knead it, and make cakes.”
verses.explainer: Abraham rushes to Sarah and says: 'Quick! Three seahs of fine flour -- knead it and make cakes!' Three seahs is about 20 quarts -- an enormous amount of bread for three guests. The urgency and excess reveal Abraham's generous heart.

verses.text: Abraham ran to the herd, and fetched a tender and good calf, and gave it to the servant. He hurried to dress it.
verses.explainer: Abraham runs to the herd, selects a good calf, and gives it to a servant to prepare quickly. Running was undignified for an elderly chieftain. Abraham does not care about dignity; he cares about honoring his guests.

verses.text: He took butter, milk, and the calf which he had dressed, and set it before them. He stood by them under the tree, and they ate.
verses.explainer: Abraham brings curds, milk, and the prepared calf and serves them. He stands by them under the tree while they eat. The host serves; the guests eat. This is hospitality at its finest.

verses.text:
They asked him, “Where is Sarah, your wife?”
He said, “See, in the tent.”
verses.explainer: They ask: 'Where is Sarah your wife?' Abraham says she is in the tent. The question is pointed -- they know her name.

verses.text:
He said, “I will certainly return to you at about this time next year; and behold, Sarah your wife will have a son.”
Sarah heard in the tent door, which was behind him.
verses.explainer: The LORD says: 'I will surely return to you about this time next year, and Sarah your wife shall have a son.' The promise of Isaac's birth is now given with a specific timeline.

verses.text: Now Abraham and Sarah were old, well advanced in age. Sarah had passed the age of childbearing.
verses.explainer: Abraham and Sarah are old, and Sarah is past the age of childbearing. The narrator underscores the biological impossibility.

verses.text: Sarah laughed within herself, saying, “After I have grown old will I have pleasure, my lord being old also?”
verses.explainer: Sarah laughs to herself, thinking: 'After I am worn out and my lord is old, shall I have pleasure?' Her laughter is internal, private, and deeply human. She finds the idea absurd.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Abraham, “Why did Sarah laugh, saying, ‘Will I really bear a child, yet I am old?’
verses.explainer: The LORD asks Abraham: 'Why did Sarah laugh?' The fact that God knows about her silent, internal laughter reveals his omniscience.

verses.text: Is anything too hard for Yahweh? At the set time I will return to you, when the season comes round, and Sarah will have a son.”
verses.explainer: The LORD asks: 'Is anything too hard for the LORD?' This rhetorical question is the theological center of the chapter. The answer shapes everything that follows: nothing is too hard for God.

verses.text:
Then Sarah denied it, saying, “I didn’t laugh,” for she was afraid.
He said, “No, but you did laugh.”
verses.explainer: Sarah denies laughing because she is afraid. The LORD says: 'No, but you did laugh.' He does not condemn her -- he simply names the truth.

verses.text: The men rose up from there, and looked toward Sodom. Abraham went with them to see them on their way.
verses.explainer: The men set out toward Sodom, and Abraham walks with them to see them off. Hospitality extends to escorting guests on their way.

verses.text: Yahweh said, “Will I hide from Abraham what I do,
verses.explainer: The LORD asks himself: 'Shall I hide from Abraham what I am about to do?' God deliberates about whether to tell Abraham about Sodom's judgment. He decides Abraham deserves to know.

verses.text: since Abraham will surely become a great and mighty nation, and all the nations of the earth will be blessed in him?
verses.explainer: God reflects: Abraham will become a great nation, and all nations will be blessed through him. The blessing of all nations includes Sodom -- or should.

verses.text: For I have known him, to the end that he may command his children and his household after him, that they may keep the way of Yahweh, to do righteousness and justice; to the end that Yahweh may bring on Abraham that which he has spoken of him.”
verses.explainer: God has chosen Abraham so that he will command his children to keep the way of the LORD by doing righteousness and justice. The purpose of election is ethical: Abraham is chosen to form a just community.

verses.text: Yahweh said, “Because the cry of Sodom and Gomorrah is great, and because their sin is very grievous,
verses.explainer: The LORD says: 'The outcry against Sodom and Gomorrah is great, and their sin is very grave.' The language of 'outcry' is legal -- it describes the cry of victims reaching heaven.

verses.text: I will go down now, and see whether their deeds are as bad as the reports which have come to me. If not, I will know.”
verses.explainer: God says he will go down and see whether the outcry matches the reality. This mirrors the Babel narrative (11:5) -- God investigates before acting.

verses.text: The men turned from there, and went toward Sodom, but Abraham stood yet before Yahweh.
verses.explainer: The two men (angels) head toward Sodom, but Abraham remains standing before the LORD. The stage is set for the negotiation.

verses.text: Abraham came near, and said, “Will you consume the righteous with the wicked?
verses.explainer: Abraham steps forward and asks: 'Will you indeed sweep away the righteous with the wicked?' He challenges God's justice -- not impertinently but boldly.

verses.text: What if there are fifty righteous within the city? Will you consume and not spare the place for the fifty righteous who are in it?
verses.explainer: Abraham proposes: what if there are fifty righteous in the city? Will you spare it for fifty? He starts high.

verses.text: Be it far from you to do things like that, to kill the righteous with the wicked, so that the righteous should be like the wicked. May that be far from you. Shouldn’t the Judge of all the earth do right?”
verses.explainer: 'Far be it from you to do such a thing, to kill the righteous with the wicked! Shall not the Judge of all the earth do right?' This is one of the great theological questions in the Bible: does God's justice require distinguishing between the guilty and the innocent?

thenVsNow.then: In the ancient world, collective punishment was normal -- entire cities were destroyed in warfare without distinguishing combatants from civilians. Abraham's appeal to individual justice was revolutionary.
thenVsNow.now: The principle that a just God must distinguish between the innocent and the guilty remains foundational to Western jurisprudence and moral philosophy.

verses.text: Yahweh said, “If I find in Sodom fifty righteous within the city, then I will spare the whole place for their sake.”
verses.explainer: The LORD agrees: if there are fifty righteous, he will spare the whole city for their sake.

verses.text: Abraham answered, “See now, I have taken it on myself to speak to the Lord, although I am dust and ashes.
verses.explainer: Abraham acknowledges his audacity: 'I am but dust and ashes.' Humility frames the boldness.

verses.text:
What if there will lack five of the fifty righteous? Will you destroy all the city for lack of five?”
He said, “I will not destroy it if I find forty-five there.”
verses.explainer: What if there are forty-five? God agrees.

verses.text:
He spoke to him yet again, and said, “What if there are forty found there?”
He said, “I will not do it for the forty’s sake.”
verses.explainer: Forty? Agreed.

verses.text:
He said, “Oh don’t let the Lord be angry, and I will speak. What if there are thirty found there?”
He said, “I will not do it if I find thirty there.”
verses.explainer: Abraham says: 'Let not the Lord be angry.' Thirty? Agreed.

verses.text:
He said, “See now, I have taken it on myself to speak to the Lord. What if there are twenty found there?”
He said, “I will not destroy it for the twenty’s sake.”
verses.explainer: Twenty? Agreed.

verses.text:
He said, “Oh don’t let the Lord be angry, and I will speak just once more. What if ten are found there?”
He said, “I will not destroy it for the ten’s sake.”
verses.explainer: Abraham pushes one more time: 'Let not the Lord be angry, and I will speak again but this once. What if ten are found?' God agrees: for ten righteous, he will not destroy Sodom.

verses.text: Yahweh went his way, as soon as he had finished communing with Abraham, and Abraham returned to his place.
verses.explainer: The LORD goes his way when he has finished speaking with Abraham, and Abraham returns home. The negotiation is over. Ten is the threshold. Sodom does not have even ten.

explainer.summary: The two angels arrive in Sodom, and Lot receives them hospitably. But the men of Sodom surround Lot's house and demand the visitors be handed over for sexual assault. The angels strike the mob blind, warn Lot to flee, and destroy Sodom and Gomorrah with fire and brimstone. Lot's wife looks back and becomes a pillar of salt. The chapter ends with a deeply disturbing episode: Lot's daughters, believing civilization has ended, get their father drunk and become pregnant by him, giving birth to Moab and Ammon.
themes: The cost of lingering near sin
Moral collapse in the aftermath of catastrophe
explainer.contextInBook: Sodom's destruction fulfills the judgment announced in chapter 18 and validates Abraham's intercession (there were not even ten righteous). It also shows how deeply Sodom has corrupted Lot -- from pitching his tent near Sodom (13:12) to sitting in Sodom's gate (19:1) to losing everything.

verses.text: The two angels came to Sodom at evening. Lot sat in the gate of Sodom. Lot saw them, and rose up to meet them. He bowed himself with his face to the earth,
verses.explainer: The two angels arrive at Sodom in the evening. Lot is sitting in the gateway of the city. The gate was where business and legal matters were conducted -- Lot has become a civic leader in Sodom. He has gone from 'near' Sodom to 'inside' Sodom to 'in charge' in Sodom.

verses.text:
and he said, “See now, my lords, please come into your servant’s house, stay all night, wash your feet, and you can rise up early, and go on your way.”
They said, “No, but we will stay in the street all night.”
verses.explainer: Lot offers the visitors hospitality: spend the night, wash your feet, and continue your journey. He mirrors Abraham's hospitality from chapter 18, though with less extravagance.

verses.text: He urged them greatly, and they came in with him, and entered into his house. He made them a feast, and baked unleavened bread, and they ate.
verses.explainer: Lot presses them strongly, and they enter his house. He makes a feast and bakes unleavened bread. The contrast between Lot's hospitality and what the city is about to do could not be sharper.

verses.text: But before they lay down, the men of the city, the men of Sodom, surrounded the house, both young and old, all the people from every quarter.
verses.explainer: Before they go to bed, the men of Sodom -- young and old, from every quarter -- surround the house. This is not a small mob; it is the entire male population of the city.

verses.text: They called to Lot, and said to him, “Where are the men who came in to you this night? Bring them out to us, that we may have sex with them.”
verses.explainer: They demand: 'Where are the men who came to you tonight? Bring them out to us so that we may know them.' The Hebrew word 'know' (yada) here clearly means sexual assault. The sin of Sodom is not mere inhospitality but violent sexual aggression against vulnerable strangers.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'The sin of Sodom', 'explanation': "The biblical tradition identifies Sodom's sin with multiple offenses: Ezekiel 16:49 names pride, excess of food, prosperous ease, and failure to help the poor. Isaiah 1:10 associates it with injustice. Genesis 19 shows it as violent sexual assault. The common thread is a culture that used power to exploit and abuse the vulnerable."}

verses.text: Lot went out to them to the door, and shut the door after him.
verses.explainer: Lot goes out to the mob and shuts the door behind him.

verses.text: He said, “Please, my brothers, don’t act so wickedly.
verses.explainer: Lot begs: 'Do not act so wickedly.' He recognizes the evil of what they are proposing.

verses.text: See now, I have two virgin daughters. Please let me bring them out to you, and you may do to them what seems good to you. Only don’t do anything to these men, because they have come under the shadow of my roof.”
verses.explainer: Lot offers his two virgin daughters instead of the visitors. This is deeply disturbing by any standard. It reflects the extreme value placed on hospitality obligations in the ancient world, but it reveals how corrupted Lot's moral compass has become by living in Sodom.

verses.text: They said, “Stand back!” Then they said, “This one fellow came in to live as a foreigner, and he appoints himself a judge. Now will we deal worse with you, than with them!” They pressed hard on the man Lot, and came near to break the door.
verses.explainer: The mob threatens Lot: 'This fellow came to sojourn, and he would play the judge!' They turn on him and press hard against the door. Lot's outsider status is thrown in his face.

verses.text: But the men reached out their hand, and brought Lot into the house to them, and shut the door.
verses.explainer: The angels reach out and pull Lot inside, shutting the door. They protect both Lot and themselves.

verses.text: They struck the men who were at the door of the house with blindness, both small and great, so that they wearied themselves to find the door.
verses.explainer: The angels strike the men at the door with blindness. Even blinded, the men of Sodom keep groping for the door. The compulsion is so strong that losing their sight does not stop them.

verses.text: The men said to Lot, “Do you have anybody else here? Sons-in-law, your sons, your daughters, and whoever you have in the city, bring them out of the place:
verses.explainer: The angels tell Lot to get his family out -- sons-in-law, sons, daughters, anyone he has in the city -- because they are about to destroy it.

verses.text: for we will destroy this place, because the outcry against them has grown so great before Yahweh that Yahweh has sent us to destroy it.”
verses.explainer: The outcry against Sodom has reached the LORD, and he has sent them to destroy it. The 'outcry' (tsa'aqah) is the cry of Sodom's victims reaching heaven.

verses.text:
Lot went out, and spoke to his sons-in-law, who were pledged to marry his daughters, and said, “Get up! Get out of this place, for Yahweh will destroy the city.”
But he seemed to his sons-in-law to be joking.
verses.explainer: Lot goes out to tell his sons-in-law, but they think he is joking. They do not take the warning seriously.

verses.text: When the morning came, then the angels hurried Lot, saying, “Get up! Take your wife, and your two daughters who are here, lest you be consumed in the iniquity of the city.”
verses.explainer: At dawn, the angels urge Lot: 'Get up! Take your wife and two daughters, or you will be swept away in the city's punishment.'

verses.text: But he lingered; and the men grabbed his hand, his wife’s hand, and his two daughters’ hands, Yahweh being merciful to him; and they took him out, and set him outside of the city.
verses.explainer: Lot hesitates. The angels seize him, his wife, and his two daughters by the hand and lead them out of the city. The LORD is merciful to him. Note: Lot does not leave voluntarily. He is physically dragged to safety.

thenVsNow.then: Lot's hesitation reveals how deeply Sodom has gotten into him. He has built a life there -- status, property, connections. Leaving means losing everything.
thenVsNow.now: Lot's hesitation is a case study in the difficulty of leaving behind a compromised but comfortable life. The gravitational pull of what we know often outweighs the urgency of what we should do.

verses.text: It came to pass, when they had taken them out, that he said, “Escape for your life! Don’t look behind you, and don’t stay anywhere in the plain. Escape to the mountains, lest you be consumed!”
verses.explainer: The angels say: 'Flee for your life! Do not look back! Do not stop anywhere in the plain! Flee to the mountains!' The instructions are urgent and absolute.

verses.text: Lot said to them, “Oh, not so, my lord.
verses.explainer: Lot says: 'Oh, no, my lords.' Even while being rescued, he negotiates.

verses.text: See now, your servant has found favor in your sight, and you have magnified your loving kindness, which you have shown to me in saving my life. I can’t escape to the mountain, lest evil overtake me, and I die.
verses.explainer: Lot asks permission to flee to a nearby small town instead of the mountains. He fears the mountains will be his death.

verses.text: See now, this city is near to flee to, and it is a little one. Oh let me escape there (isn’t it a little one?), and my soul will live.”
verses.explainer: Lot points to the small town: 'It is a little one.' He wants the easy escape.

verses.text: He said to him, “Behold, I have granted your request concerning this thing also, that I will not overthrow the city of which you have spoken.
verses.explainer: The angel grants his request and agrees not to overthrow the small town.

verses.text: Hurry, escape there, for I can’t do anything until you get there.” Therefore the name of the city was called Zoar.
verses.explainer: The town is called Zoar ('small'). Lot arrives, and judgment begins.

verses.text: The sun had risen on the earth when Lot came to Zoar.
verses.explainer: The sun has risen on the earth when Lot enters Zoar.

verses.text: Then Yahweh rained on Sodom and on Gomorrah sulfur and fire from Yahweh out of the sky.
verses.explainer: The LORD rains sulfur and fire on Sodom and Gomorrah from the LORD out of heaven. The destruction comes from above. The language is deliberate: it reverses the blessed rain of creation and replaces it with destroying fire.

verses.text: He overthrew those cities, all the plain, all the inhabitants of the cities, and that which grew on the ground.
verses.explainer: God overthrows those cities, the entire plain, all the inhabitants, and all vegetation. Total destruction -- nothing survives.

verses.text: But his wife looked back from behind him, and she became a pillar of salt.
verses.explainer: Lot's wife looks back and becomes a pillar of salt. She was told not to look back. The backward glance represents her attachment to what she is leaving. She has left Sodom physically but not emotionally.

verses.text: Abraham got up early in the morning to the place where he had stood before Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Abraham gets up early and looks toward Sodom from the place where he stood before the LORD (where he interceded in chapter 18).

verses.text: He looked toward Sodom and Gomorrah, and toward all the land of the plain, and looked, and saw that the smoke of the land went up as the smoke of a furnace.
verses.explainer: Abraham looks down toward Sodom and Gomorrah and sees smoke rising from the land like smoke from a furnace. His intercession found fewer than ten righteous. The cities are gone.

verses.text: When God destroyed the cities of the plain, God remembered Abraham, and sent Lot out of the middle of the overthrow, when he overthrew the cities in which Lot lived.
verses.explainer: When God destroyed the cities, he remembered Abraham and sent Lot out of the midst of the overthrow. Lot is saved not because of his own righteousness but because of Abraham's relationship with God.

verses.text: Lot went up out of Zoar, and lived in the mountain, and his two daughters with him; for he was afraid to live in Zoar. He lived in a cave with his two daughters.
verses.explainer: Lot leaves Zoar and goes to live in a cave in the mountains -- exactly where the angels told him to go in the first place. Fear drove him to Zoar; now fear drives him to the mountains.

verses.text: The firstborn said to the younger, “Our father is old, and there is not a man in the earth to come in to us in the way of all the earth.
verses.explainer: The firstborn daughter says to the younger: 'Our father is old, and there is no man on earth to come in to us.' She may believe the destruction was global, or she may simply mean no man will marry daughters of Sodom.

verses.text: Come, let’s make our father drink wine, and we will lie with him, that we may preserve our father’s family line.”
verses.explainer: She proposes getting their father drunk and sleeping with him to preserve his family line. The plan echoes the moral corruption of Sodom itself.

verses.text: They made their father drink wine that night: and the firstborn went in, and lay with her father. He didn’t know when she lay down, nor when she arose.
verses.explainer: They get Lot drunk, and the firstborn sleeps with her father. He does not know when she lies down or when she rises.

verses.text: It came to pass on the next day, that the firstborn said to the younger, “Behold, I lay last night with my father. Let us make him drink wine again, tonight. You go in, and lie with him, that we may preserve our father’s family line.”
verses.explainer: The next day, the firstborn tells the younger, and they repeat the plan.

verses.text: They made their father drink wine that night also. The younger went and lay with him. He didn’t know when she lay down, nor when she got up.
verses.explainer: They get Lot drunk again, and the younger daughter sleeps with him.

verses.text: Thus both of Lot’s daughters were with child by their father.
verses.explainer: Both daughters become pregnant by their father.

verses.text: The firstborn bore a son, and named him Moab. He is the father of the Moabites to this day.
verses.explainer: The firstborn bears a son named Moab ('from father'). He is the ancestor of the Moabites. Ruth, the great-grandmother of David, will be a Moabite.

verses.text: The younger also bore a son, and called his name Ben Ammi. He is the father of the children of Ammon to this day.
verses.explainer: The younger bears a son named Ben-Ammi ('son of my people'). He is the ancestor of the Ammonites. Both nations will have a complicated relationship with Israel throughout the Old Testament.

explainer.summary: Abraham moves to the Negev and settles near Gerar. Incredibly, he repeats the wife-sister deception from chapter 12, telling King Abimelech that Sarah is his sister. God intervenes in a dream, warning Abimelech that Sarah is married. Abimelech, like Pharaoh before him, rebukes Abraham for his deception. Abraham explains his fear and the technical truth that Sarah is his half-sister. Abimelech restores Sarah with generous gifts and an open invitation to settle in his land.
themes: God protecting the covenant despite human failure
A pagan king with more integrity than the patriarch
God's faithfulness does not depend on human faithfulness
explainer.contextInBook: This episode, coming right before Isaac's birth, shows that Abraham has not outgrown his fundamental fear. The man about to become the father of the covenant people still cannot trust God to protect him. Yet God protects the promise anyway.

verses.text: Abraham traveled from there toward the land of the South, and lived between Kadesh and Shur. He lived as a foreigner in Gerar.
verses.explainer: Abraham journeys to the Negev and settles between Kadesh and Shur, sojourning in Gerar. The move puts him in Philistine territory.

verses.text: Abraham said about Sarah his wife, “She is my sister.” Abimelech king of Gerar sent, and took Sarah.
verses.explainer: Abraham says of Sarah: 'She is my sister.' Abimelech king of Gerar sends and takes Sarah. This is the same lie he told in Egypt (chapter 12). He has learned nothing.

verses.text: But God came to Abimelech in a dream of the night, and said to him, “Behold, you are a dead man, because of the woman whom you have taken. For she is a man’s wife.”
verses.explainer: God comes to Abimelech in a dream: 'You are a dead man because the woman you have taken is married.' God protects Sarah directly, bypassing the unreliable Abraham.

verses.text: Now Abimelech had not come near her. He said, “Lord, will you kill even a righteous nation?
verses.explainer: Abimelech has not approached Sarah. He pleads: 'Lord, will you kill a righteous nation?' He is innocent.

verses.text: Didn’t he tell me, ‘She is my sister?’ She, even she herself, said, ‘He is my brother.’ In the integrity of my heart and the innocence of my hands I have done this.”
verses.explainer: Abimelech says Abraham told him Sarah was his sister, and Sarah herself confirmed it. He acted with a clean conscience and clean hands.

verses.text: God said to him in the dream, “Yes, I know that in the integrity of your heart you have done this, and I also withheld you from sinning against me. Therefore I didn’t allow you to touch her.
verses.explainer: God acknowledges Abimelech's innocence and says he kept Abimelech from sinning by preventing him from touching Sarah. God protects both the covenant and the pagan king.

verses.text: Now therefore, restore the man’s wife. For he is a prophet, and he will pray for you, and you will live. If you don’t restore her, know for sure that you will die, you, and all who are yours.”
verses.explainer: God tells Abimelech to return Sarah because Abraham is a prophet who will pray for him. If not, Abimelech will die. Abraham is called a prophet for the first time -- a prophet who lies.

thenVsNow.then: The title 'prophet' (nabi) here means one who intercedes with God on behalf of others. Abraham's role is not primarily to predict the future but to serve as an intermediary between God and Abimelech.
thenVsNow.now: The fact that God calls Abraham a prophet in the same story where Abraham is exposed as a liar is a powerful statement about grace. God uses broken people without waiting for them to become worthy of the title.

verses.text: Abimelech rose early in the morning, and called all his servants, and told all these things in their ear. The men were very scared.
verses.explainer: Abimelech rises early, tells his servants, and they are all very afraid. The pagan court takes the divine warning more seriously than Abraham took God's promise.

verses.text: Then Abimelech called Abraham, and said to him, “What have you done to us? How have I sinned against you, that you have brought on me and on my kingdom a great sin? You have done deeds to me that ought not to be done!”
verses.explainer: Abimelech summons Abraham and confronts him: 'What have you done to us? What have I done to you that you have brought such great guilt on me and my kingdom?' The rebuke is deserved.

verses.text: Abimelech said to Abraham, “What did you see, that you have done this thing?”
verses.explainer: Abimelech asks: 'What did you see that you did this thing?' He wants to understand Abraham's reasoning.

verses.text: Abraham said, “Because I thought, ‘Surely the fear of God is not in this place. They will kill me for my wife’s sake.’
verses.explainer: Abraham explains: 'I thought there is no fear of God in this place, and they will kill me because of my wife.' He assumed the worst about Abimelech's people. He was wrong.

verses.text: Besides, she is indeed my sister, the daughter of my father, but not the daughter of my mother; and she became my wife.
verses.explainer: Abraham reveals the half-truth: Sarah is his half-sister, the daughter of his father but not his mother. The deception was technically true but morally dishonest.

verses.text: When God caused me to wander from my father’s house, I said to her, ‘This is your kindness which you shall show to me. Everywhere that we go, say of me, “He is my brother.”’”
verses.explainer: Abraham says this has been their practice everywhere they go: she says 'He is my brother.' This is not a one-time failure but a systematic survival strategy.

verses.text: Abimelech took sheep and cattle, male servants and female servants, and gave them to Abraham, and restored Sarah, his wife, to him.
verses.explainer: Abimelech gives Abraham sheep, oxen, servants, and restores Sarah. Once again, Abraham profits from the deception -- a troubling pattern.

verses.text: Abimelech said, “Behold, my land is before you. Dwell where it pleases you.”
verses.explainer: Abimelech offers Abraham the run of his land: 'Dwell wherever you please.' The pagan king is more generous than Abraham has been honest.

verses.text: To Sarah he said, “Behold, I have given your brother a thousand pieces of silver. Behold, it is for you a covering of the eyes to all that are with you. In front of all you are vindicated.”
verses.explainer: Abimelech gives Sarah a thousand pieces of silver as a vindication of her honor. He calls Abraham her 'brother' with pointed irony.

verses.text: Abraham prayed to God. God healed Abimelech, and his wife, and his female servants, and they bore children.
verses.explainer: Abraham prays to God, and God heals Abimelech, his wife, and his female servants so they can bear children.

verses.text: For Yahweh had closed up tight all the wombs of the house of Abimelech, because of Sarah, Abraham’s wife.
verses.explainer: The LORD had closed all the wombs in Abimelech's household because of Sarah. The irony: God closes wombs to protect the woman whose own womb God is about to open.

explainer.summary: The long-awaited promise is fulfilled: Isaac is born to Abraham and Sarah. But joy is followed by conflict: Sarah demands that Hagar and Ishmael be expelled. Abraham is distressed, but God tells him to listen to Sarah because the covenant goes through Isaac. God also promises to make Ishmael a nation. Hagar and Ishmael nearly die in the wilderness, but God provides water and repeats his promise for Ishmael. The chapter ends with Abraham making a treaty with Abimelech at Beersheba.
themes: Laughter transformed from doubt to joy
The painful expulsion of Hagar and Ishmael
explainer.contextInBook: Isaac's birth is the pivot of the Abraham narrative. Everything before it was about waiting for the promise; everything after it is about living with the promised child.

wordStudies.translatedAs: He laughs
wordStudies.meaning: He laughs. The name commemorates both Abraham's laughter of disbelief (17:17) and Sarah's laughter of joy (21:6). What began as incredulous laughter at an impossible promise becomes joyful laughter at its fulfillment.

verses.text: Yahweh visited Sarah as he had said, and Yahweh did to Sarah as he had spoken.
verses.explainer: The LORD visits Sarah as he said, and does for her as he promised. The narrative emphasizes God's faithfulness: he said it, he did it.

verses.text: Sarah conceived, and bore Abraham a son in his old age, at the set time of which God had spoken to him.
verses.explainer: Sarah conceives and bears Abraham a son in his old age, at the appointed time God had spoken. Every detail matches what God promised -- the child, the mother, the timing.

verses.text: Abraham called his son who was born to him, whom Sarah bore to him, Isaac.
verses.explainer: Abraham names his son Isaac, as God commanded. The name embeds laughter into the identity of the covenant heir.

verses.text: Abraham circumcised his son, Isaac, when he was eight days old, as God had commanded him.
verses.explainer: Abraham circumcises Isaac when he is eight days old, as God commanded. Obedience continues.

verses.text: Abraham was one hundred years old when his son, Isaac, was born to him.
verses.explainer: Abraham is 100 years old when Isaac is born. The emphasis on age underscores the miracle.

verses.text: Sarah said, “God has made me laugh. Everyone who hears will laugh with me.”
verses.explainer: Sarah says: 'God has made laughter for me; everyone who hears will laugh with me.' The laughter of doubt (18:12) has become the laughter of joy. What was ridiculous has become real.

thenVsNow.then: Sarah's laughter transforms from skepticism to celebration. The impossible has happened, and the appropriate response is joy shared with the community.
thenVsNow.now: Isaac's name -- 'laughter' -- suggests that the fulfillment of God's promises should produce joy, not grim duty. The birth of the covenant child is a comedy, not a tragedy.

verses.text: She said, “Who would have said to Abraham, that Sarah would nurse children? For I have borne him a son in his old age.”
verses.explainer: Sarah marvels: 'Who would have said to Abraham that Sarah would nurse children?' The rhetorical question celebrates the absurdity of grace.

verses.text: The child grew, and was weaned. Abraham made a great feast on the day that Isaac was weaned.
verses.explainer: Isaac grows and is weaned. Abraham throws a great feast on the day of weaning. In the ancient world, weaning typically happened around age three and was a milestone celebration.

verses.text: Sarah saw the son of Hagar the Egyptian, whom she had borne to Abraham, mocking.
verses.explainer: Sarah sees Ishmael laughing (or mocking). The same root as Isaac's name -- but Sarah perceives it as a threat. Whether Ishmael was playing innocently or mocking is debated; Sarah's response is extreme either way.

verses.text: Therefore she said to Abraham, “Cast out this servant and her son! For the son of this servant will not be heir with my son, Isaac.”
verses.explainer: Sarah tells Abraham: 'Cast out this slave woman with her son, for the son of this slave woman shall not be heir with my son Isaac.' The demand is brutal: exile Hagar and Ishmael to protect Isaac's inheritance.

verses.text: The thing was very grievous in Abraham’s sight on account of his son.
verses.explainer: The matter greatly distresses Abraham because of his son Ishmael. Abraham loves Ishmael. This is not easy for him.

verses.text: God said to Abraham, “Don’t let it be grievous in your sight because of the boy, and because of your servant. In all that Sarah says to you, listen to her voice. For your offspring will be named through Isaac.
verses.explainer: God tells Abraham: 'Do not be distressed. Listen to Sarah, because through Isaac your offspring shall be named.' God confirms Sarah's demand, not because it is kind but because the covenant must go through Isaac.

verses.text: I will also make a nation of the son of the servant, because he is your child.”
verses.explainer: God promises to make Ishmael into a nation because he is also Abraham's offspring. Ishmael is not abandoned; he is blessed differently.

verses.text: Abraham rose up early in the morning, and took bread and a bottle of water, and gave it to Hagar, putting it on her shoulder; and gave her the child, and sent her away. She departed, and wandered in the wilderness of Beersheba.
verses.explainer: Abraham rises early, gives Hagar bread and a skin of water, puts them on her shoulder, and sends her and the boy away. She wanders in the wilderness of Beersheba. The brevity is heartbreaking.

verses.text: The water in the bottle was spent, and she put the child under one of the shrubs.
verses.explainer: The water runs out. Hagar puts Ishmael under a bush.

verses.text: She went and sat down opposite him, a good way off, about a bow shot away. For she said, “Don’t let me see the death of the child.” She sat over against him, and lifted up her voice, and wept.
verses.explainer: She sits down a bowshot away because she cannot bear to watch her son die. She lifts up her voice and weeps. This is one of the most emotionally devastating scenes in Genesis.

verses.text:
God heard the voice of the boy.
The angel of God called to Hagar out of the sky, and said to her, “What ails you, Hagar? Don’t be afraid. For God has heard the voice of the boy where he is.
verses.explainer: God hears the voice of the boy. The angel of God calls to Hagar from heaven: 'Do not fear. God has heard the voice of the boy.' Ishmael's name means 'God hears,' and God lives up to the name.

verses.text: Get up, lift up the boy, and hold him in your hand. For I will make him a great nation.”
verses.explainer: God tells Hagar to lift up the boy and hold him, for God will make him a great nation.

verses.text: God opened her eyes, and she saw a well of water. She went, filled the bottle with water, and gave the boy drink.
verses.explainer: God opens Hagar's eyes, and she sees a well of water. She fills the skin and gives the boy a drink. God provides water in the wilderness -- a preview of what he will do for Israel in the Exodus.

verses.text: God was with the boy, and he grew. He lived in the wilderness, and became, as he grew up, an archer.
verses.explainer: God is with the boy. He grows up in the wilderness and becomes an archer.

verses.text: He lived in the wilderness of Paran. His mother took a wife for him out of the land of Egypt.
verses.explainer: He lives in the wilderness of Paran, and his mother gets a wife for him from Egypt. Ishmael's Egyptian connection is maintained through his mother and his wife.

verses.text: At that time, Abimelech and Phicol the captain of his army spoke to Abraham, saying, “God is with you in all that you do.
verses.explainer: Abimelech and Phicol his commander say to Abraham: 'God is with you in everything you do.' Even pagans recognize God's blessing on Abraham.

verses.text: Now, therefore, swear to me here by God that you will not deal falsely with me, nor with my son, nor with my son’s son. But according to the kindness that I have done to you, you shall do to me, and to the land in which you have lived as a foreigner.”
verses.explainer: Abimelech asks Abraham to swear that he will deal kindly with him and his land. Diplomacy between the patriarch and the local king.

verses.text: Abraham said, “I will swear.”
verses.explainer: Abraham says: 'I will swear.'

verses.text: Abraham complained to Abimelech because of a water well, which Abimelech’s servants had violently taken away.
verses.explainer: Abraham complains about a well that Abimelech's servants seized. Water rights were a matter of survival in the arid Negev.

verses.text: Abimelech said, “I don’t know who has done this thing. You didn’t tell me, and I didn’t hear of it until today.”
verses.explainer: Abimelech claims ignorance of the dispute.

verses.text: Abraham took sheep and cattle, and gave them to Abimelech. Those two made a covenant.
verses.explainer: Abraham gives sheep and oxen to Abimelech, and they make a covenant.

verses.text: Abraham set seven ewe lambs of the flock by themselves.
verses.explainer: Abraham sets seven ewe lambs apart.

verses.text: Abimelech said to Abraham, “What do these seven ewe lambs which you have set by themselves mean?”
verses.explainer: Abimelech asks what the seven lambs mean.

verses.text: He said, “You shall take these seven ewe lambs from my hand, that it may be a witness to me, that I have dug this well.”
verses.explainer: Abraham says they are witness that he dug the well. The lambs serve as evidence of ownership.

verses.text: Therefore he called that place Beersheba, because they both swore an oath there.
verses.explainer: The place is called Beersheba -- 'well of the oath' or 'well of seven.' Both meanings apply.

verses.text: So they made a covenant at Beersheba. Abimelech rose up with Phicol, the captain of his army, and they returned into the land of the Philistines.
verses.explainer: They make a covenant at Beersheba. Abimelech and Phicol return to Philistine territory.

verses.text: Abraham planted a tamarisk tree in Beersheba, and called there on the name of Yahweh, the Everlasting God.
verses.explainer: Abraham plants a tamarisk tree in Beersheba and calls on the name of the LORD, the Everlasting God (El Olam). A new name for God -- the one whose promises span eternity.

verses.text: Abraham lived as a foreigner in the land of the Philistines many days.
verses.explainer: Abraham sojourns in the land of the Philistines many days. He remains a stranger in the land of promise.

explainer.summary: The most dramatic and theologically dense chapter in Genesis. God tests Abraham by commanding him to sacrifice Isaac -- the son of promise, the child they waited twenty-five years for. Abraham obeys without protest, journeys three days to Mount Moriah, binds Isaac on an altar, and raises the knife. At the last moment, God stops him and provides a ram as a substitute. Abraham has proven that he fears God above all else. God reaffirms his covenant with an oath, and Abraham names the place 'The LORD will provide.'
explainer.contextInBook: The Akedah (binding) is the climax of Abraham's faith journey. He began by leaving his homeland (chapter 12); he ends by being willing to give up his son. Everything he has learned about trusting God is tested to the absolute limit.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To test or prove
wordStudies.meaning: To test or prove. The narrator tells us from the start that this is a test, not God's actual desire for child sacrifice. God is testing the depth of Abraham's trust -- whether he will hold anything back, even the son of promise.
