wordStudies.translatedAs: The name possibly means 'seen by the LORD' or 'the
wordStudies.meaning: The name possibly means 'seen by the LORD' or 'the LORD provides.' Jewish tradition identifies this mountain with the Temple Mount in Jerusalem, linking Abraham's sacrifice to Solomon's Temple and all the sacrifices offered there.

verses.text:
After these things, God tested Abraham, and said to him, “Abraham!”
He said, “Here I am.”
verses.explainer: After these things, God tests Abraham. He calls: 'Abraham!' Abraham answers: 'Here I am.' The Hebrew hinneni (here I am) expresses total availability and readiness. The narrator tells us it is a test before the story unfolds.

verses.text: He said, “Now take your son, your only son, whom you love, even Isaac, and go into the land of Moriah. Offer him there as a burnt offering on one of the mountains which I will tell you of.”
verses.explainer: God says: 'Take your son, your only son Isaac, whom you love, and go to the land of Moriah, and offer him there as a burnt offering.' Each phrase increases the agony: your son, your only son, the one you love. God names the cost before giving the command.

thenVsNow.then: Child sacrifice was practiced in the ancient Near East, particularly among the Canaanites. Abraham would have known this practice. What is shocking is not that a god would ask for a child but that this God will ultimately refuse it.
thenVsNow.now: Christians read this passage through the lens of John 3:16: God gave his only Son, the one he loved. What God asked Abraham to do and then stopped, God ultimately did himself. The Akedah becomes a window into the cross.

verses.text: Abraham rose early in the morning, and saddled his donkey, and took two of his young men with him, and Isaac his son. He split the wood for the burnt offering, and rose up, and went to the place of which God had told him.
verses.explainer: Abraham rises early in the morning, saddles his donkey, takes two servants and Isaac, cuts wood for the burnt offering, and goes to the place God told him. No argument, no negotiation, no delay. The man who bargained for Sodom says nothing when it comes to his own son.

verses.text: On the third day Abraham lifted up his eyes, and saw the place far off.
verses.explainer: On the third day, Abraham lifts his eyes and sees the place from a distance. Three days of walking with the knowledge of what he must do.

verses.text: Abraham said to his young men, “Stay here with the donkey. The boy and I will go yonder. We will worship, and come back to you.”
verses.explainer: Abraham tells the servants: 'Stay here. I and the boy will go over there and worship and come back to you.' He says 'we will come back.' Either Abraham is lying to the servants, or he believes that somehow, even through death, God will return Isaac. Hebrews 11:19 says he believed God could raise Isaac from the dead.

verses.text: Abraham took the wood of the burnt offering and laid it on Isaac his son. He took in his hand the fire and the knife. They both went together.
verses.explainer: Abraham puts the wood on Isaac's shoulders and carries the fire and the knife himself. The image of the son carrying the wood of his own sacrifice to the mountaintop has haunted readers for millennia.

verses.text:
Isaac spoke to Abraham his father, and said, “My father?”
He said, “Here I am, my son.”
He said, “Here is the fire and the wood, but where is the lamb for a burnt offering?”
verses.explainer: Isaac asks: 'Father?' Abraham answers: 'Here I am, my son.' The same hinneni he gave God, he now gives his son. Isaac asks: 'Where is the lamb for the burnt offering?'

verses.text: Abraham said, “God will provide himself the lamb for a burnt offering, my son.” So they both went together.
verses.explainer: Abraham says: 'God will provide for himself the lamb for the burnt offering, my son.' This is either the greatest statement of faith or the most desperate hope in the Bible. It turns out to be prophetic.

verses.text: They came to the place which God had told him of. Abraham built the altar there, and laid the wood in order, bound Isaac his son, and laid him on the altar, on the wood.
verses.explainer: They arrive at the place. Abraham builds the altar, arranges the wood, binds Isaac, and lays him on the altar on top of the wood. Isaac does not resist. He is old enough to carry wood but does not fight.

verses.text: Abraham stretched out his hand, and took the knife to kill his son.
verses.explainer: Abraham reaches out his hand and takes the knife to slaughter his son. The narrative slows to a frame-by-frame crawl at the most agonizing moment.

verses.text:
Yahweh’s angel called to him out of the sky, and said, “Abraham, Abraham!”
He said, “Here I am.”
verses.explainer: The angel of the LORD calls from heaven: 'Abraham! Abraham!' He answers: 'Here I am.' The third hinneni. God stops him.

verses.text: He said, “Don’t lay your hand on the boy or do anything to him. For now I know that you fear God, since you have not withheld your son, your only son, from me.”
verses.explainer: The angel says: 'Do not lay a hand on the boy. Now I know that you fear God, because you have not withheld your son, your only son, from me.' The test is passed. Abraham fears God more than he loves Isaac.

verses.text: Abraham lifted up his eyes, and looked, and saw that behind him was a ram caught in the thicket by his horns. Abraham went and took the ram, and offered him up for a burnt offering instead of his son.
verses.explainer: Abraham looks up and sees a ram caught in a thicket by its horns. He sacrifices the ram instead of Isaac. The substitute appears -- provided by God, not arranged by Abraham.

thenVsNow.then: The ram caught in the thicket becomes the first explicit substitutionary sacrifice in the Bible: an animal dies so that the son lives. This principle underlies the entire sacrificial system of Israel.
thenVsNow.now: Christians see the ram as a type of Christ: the substitute provided by God to die in the place of the one who should have died. The entire theology of atonement traces back to this thicket.

verses.text: Abraham called the name of that place Yahweh Will Provide. As it is said to this day, “On Yahweh’s mountain, it will be provided.”
verses.explainer: Abraham names the place 'The LORD will provide' (YHWH Yireh). The saying continues: 'On the mount of the LORD it shall be provided.' If Moriah is the Temple Mount, then sacrificial provision will continue there for a thousand years.

verses.text: Yahweh’s angel called to Abraham a second time out of the sky,
verses.explainer: The angel of the LORD calls a second time from heaven.

verses.text: and said, “I have sworn by myself, says Yahweh, because you have done this thing, and have not withheld your son, your only son,
verses.explainer: God swears by himself -- since there is no one greater to swear by -- that because Abraham has done this and not withheld his only son, God will bless him.

verses.text: that I will bless you greatly, and I will multiply your offspring greatly like the stars of the heavens, and like the sand which is on the seashore. Your offspring will possess the gate of his enemies.
verses.explainer: God will multiply Abraham's offspring as the stars of heaven and the sand on the seashore. His offspring will possess the gate of their enemies.

verses.text: All the nations of the earth will be blessed by your offspring, because you have obeyed my voice.”
verses.explainer: In Abraham's offspring, all the nations of the earth shall be blessed, because he obeyed God's voice. The universal promise of 12:3 is reaffirmed after the supreme test of faith.

verses.text: So Abraham returned to his young men, and they rose up and went together to Beersheba. Abraham lived at Beersheba.
verses.explainer: Abraham returns to his servants, and they go to Beersheba, where Abraham lives. Isaac is not mentioned -- a silence that has generated much speculation.

verses.text: After these things, Abraham was told, “Behold, Milcah, she also has borne children to your brother Nahor:
verses.explainer: Abraham receives news: his brother Nahor has had children -- eight sons by his wife Milcah. This genealogy serves to introduce Rebekah, who will become Isaac's wife.

verses.text: Uz his firstborn, Buz his brother, Kemuel the father of Aram,
verses.explainer: Uz (the firstborn), Buz, Kemuel (father of Aram).

verses.text: Chesed, Hazo, Pildash, Jidlaph, and Bethuel.”
verses.explainer: Chesed, Hazo, Pildash, Jidlaph, and Bethuel.

verses.text: Bethuel became the father of Rebekah. These eight Milcah bore to Nahor, Abraham’s brother.
verses.explainer: Bethuel fathers Rebekah. The notice is tucked into a genealogy, but it is the point: Isaac's future wife exists and has been born.

verses.text: His concubine, whose name was Reumah, also bore Tebah, Gaham, Tahash, and Maacah.
verses.explainer: Nahor's concubine Reumah also bears children: Tebah, Gaham, Tahash, and Maacah.

explainer.summary: Sarah dies at age 127 in Hebron. Abraham mourns and then enters negotiations with the Hittites to purchase a burial site. The negotiation is a masterclass in ancient Near Eastern business practice: Ephron the Hittite offers the field and cave 'for free,' Abraham insists on paying full price, and Ephron names an exorbitant sum that Abraham pays without haggling. The cave of Machpelah becomes the first piece of the Promised Land that Abraham actually owns.
themes: The first land purchase in the Promised Land
A burial cave as a claim to the land
explainer.contextInBook: After a lifetime of being promised land he never owned, Abraham finally purchases a tiny plot -- not for a house but for a grave. It is a down payment on the promise, a stake in the ground that says: we belong here.

verses.text: Sarah lived one hundred twenty-seven years. This was the length of Sarah’s life.
verses.explainer: Sarah lives 127 years. She is the only woman in the Bible whose age at death is recorded, reflecting her unique importance.

verses.text: Sarah died in Kiriath Arba (also called Hebron), in the land of Canaan. Abraham came to mourn for Sarah, and to weep for her.
verses.explainer: Sarah dies at Kiriath-arba (Hebron) in the land of Canaan. Abraham comes to mourn and weep for her. He grieves publicly and deeply.

verses.text: Abraham rose up from before his dead, and spoke to the children of Heth, saying,
verses.explainer: Abraham rises from before his dead and speaks to the Hittites: 'I am a stranger and a sojourner among you. Give me property among you for a burying place.' He openly acknowledges his outsider status.

verses.text: “I am a stranger and a foreigner living with you. Give me a possession of a burying-place with you, that I may bury my dead out of my sight.”
verses.explainer: The Hittites call Abraham 'a prince of God among us.' Despite being a resident alien, Abraham commands respect.

verses.text: The children of Heth answered Abraham, saying to him,
verses.explainer: The Hittites say: 'Hear us, my lord.' They address Abraham with honor.

verses.text: “Hear us, my lord. You are a prince of God among us. Bury your dead in the best of our tombs. None of us will withhold from you his tomb. Bury your dead.”
verses.explainer: They offer Abraham the choicest of their burial places. None will withhold his tomb from Abraham.

verses.text: Abraham rose up, and bowed himself to the people of the land, even to the children of Heth.
verses.explainer: Abraham rises and bows to the Hittites. The negotiation is conducted with elaborate courtesy on both sides.

verses.text: He talked with them, saying, “If you agree that I should bury my dead out of my sight, hear me, and entreat for me to Ephron the son of Zohar,
verses.explainer: Abraham asks them to intercede with Ephron son of Zohar on his behalf.

verses.text: that he may give me the cave of Machpelah, which he has, which is in the end of his field. For the full price let him give it to me among you as a possession for a burial place.”
verses.explainer: He wants the cave of Machpelah, which Ephron owns, at the end of his field. He will pay full price for it as a burial site.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Cave burial', 'explanation': 'Caves were the standard burial sites in ancient Canaan. Bodies were placed in niches cut into the rock walls. When the flesh had decomposed, the bones were gathered and placed in an ossuary or pushed aside to make room for the next burial. Family tombs were used for generations.'}

verses.text: Now Ephron was sitting in the middle of the children of Heth. Ephron the Hittite answered Abraham in the hearing of the children of Heth, even of all who went in at the gate of his city, saying,
verses.explainer: Ephron is sitting among the Hittites. He responds publicly, in the hearing of all who enter the city gate.

verses.text: “No, my lord, hear me. I give you the field, and I give you the cave that is in it. In the presence of the children of my people I give it to you. Bury your dead.”
verses.explainer: Ephron says: 'No, my lord, hear me: I give you the field and the cave. In the presence of my people I give it to you.' This 'free' offer is actually a negotiating tactic -- accepting it would create an obligation; insisting on paying establishes clear ownership.

thenVsNow.then: Ancient Near Eastern property negotiations followed elaborate protocols of feigned generosity. Offering 'for free' was the opening move, not a sincere gift. Accepting would mean the land could be reclaimed or contested later.
thenVsNow.now: This pattern of negotiation -- the generous offer that both parties know is not really free -- remains recognizable in Middle Eastern and many other cultures. Abraham insists on paying to ensure permanent, uncontestable ownership.

verses.text: Abraham bowed himself down before the people of the land.
verses.explainer: Abraham bows before the people of the land.

verses.text: He spoke to Ephron in the audience of the people of the land, saying, “But if you will, please hear me. I will give the price of the field. Take it from me, and I will bury my dead there.”
verses.explainer: Abraham says: 'I will give the price of the field. Accept it from me so that I may bury my dead there.' He insists on paying.

verses.text: Ephron answered Abraham, saying to him,
verses.explainer: Ephron answers: 'A piece of land worth 400 shekels of silver -- what is that between you and me?' He names the price while pretending it is nothing.

verses.text: “My lord, listen to me. What is a piece of land worth four hundred shekels of silver between me and you? Therefore bury your dead.”
verses.explainer: The 400 shekels is likely an inflated price. Jeremiah later buys a field for 17 shekels (Jeremiah 32:9). But Abraham does not haggle.

verses.text: Abraham listened to Ephron. Abraham weighed to Ephron the silver which he had named in the audience of the children of Heth, four hundred shekels of silver, according to the current merchants’ standard.
verses.explainer: Abraham weighs out 400 shekels of silver, current money with the merchant. He pays full price, in public, with verified weights. No one can later dispute the sale.

verses.text: So the field of Ephron, which was in Machpelah, which was before Mamre, the field, the cave which was in it, and all the trees that were in the field, that were in all of its borders, were deeded
verses.explainer: The field of Ephron in Machpelah, east of Mamre -- the field, the cave, and all the trees in the field -- are deeded to Abraham.

verses.text: to Abraham for a possession in the presence of the children of Heth, before all who went in at the gate of his city.
verses.explainer: The transfer is made in the presence of the Hittites, before all who enter the city gate. Multiple witnesses ensure legal validity.

verses.text: After this, Abraham buried Sarah his wife in the cave of the field of Machpelah before Mamre (that is, Hebron), in the land of Canaan.
verses.explainer: Abraham buries Sarah in the cave of the field of Machpelah facing Mamre (Hebron) in Canaan. The first burial in the first piece of the Promised Land.

verses.text: The field, and the cave that is in it, were deeded to Abraham by the children of Heth as a possession for a burial place.
verses.explainer: The field and cave are deeded to Abraham by the Hittites as a burial site. This tiny plot is the only land Abraham will ever own in Canaan -- but it is a stake in the ground, a claim that says the promise is real.

explainer.summary: The longest chapter in Genesis tells the story of Abraham sending his senior servant to find a wife for Isaac from among his relatives in Mesopotamia. The servant prays for a specific sign, and Rebekah immediately fulfills it. Her family agrees to the marriage, and she returns to Canaan, where she meets Isaac. The chapter is a masterpiece of narrative art and a profound meditation on divine guidance -- God's hand is visible in every detail, yet every character acts freely.
themes: The importance of finding the right partner
The continuation of the covenant through marriage
explainer.contextInBook: With Sarah dead and Abraham aging, the critical question is: who will Isaac marry? The covenant cannot continue without the next generation. The entire future of God's plan hinges on a woman watering camels at a well.

verses.text: Abraham was old, and well stricken in age. Yahweh had blessed Abraham in all things.
verses.explainer: Abraham is old, well-advanced in years. The LORD has blessed him in everything.

verses.text: Abraham said to his servant, the elder of his house, who ruled over all that he had, “Please put your hand under my thigh.
verses.explainer: Abraham tells his senior servant -- the one in charge of all he has -- to place his hand under Abraham's thigh and swear an oath.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Hand under the thigh', 'explanation': "This oath ceremony involved placing the hand near the reproductive organs, symbolizing that the oath was sworn on the patriarch's descendants. Breaking the oath would bring a curse on future generations. It was the most solemn form of oath in the ancient world."}

verses.text: I will make you swear by Yahweh, the God of heaven and the God of the earth, that you shall not take a wife for my son of the daughters of the Canaanites, among whom I live.
verses.explainer: Abraham makes the servant swear not to take a wife for Isaac from the Canaanites but to go to Abraham's homeland and relatives.

verses.text: But you shall go to my country, and to my relatives, and take a wife for my son Isaac.”
verses.explainer: The servant must go to Abraham's country and kindred to find a wife for Isaac.

verses.text: The servant said to him, “What if the woman isn’t willing to follow me to this land? Must I bring your son again to the land you came from?”
verses.explainer: The servant asks: 'What if the woman is unwilling to come? Should I take Isaac back there?'

verses.text: Abraham said to him, “Beware that you don’t bring my son there again.
verses.explainer: Abraham says emphatically: 'Do not take my son back there.' Isaac must stay in the Promised Land.

verses.text: Yahweh, the God of heaven, who took me from my father’s house, and from the land of my birth, who spoke to me, and who swore to me, saying, ‘I will give this land to your offspring. He will send his angel before you, and you shall take a wife for my son from there.
verses.explainer: Abraham trusts that God will send his angel ahead to ensure the servant's success. The same God who called Abraham from that land will not send his son back to it.

verses.text: If the woman isn’t willing to follow you, then you shall be clear from this oath to me. Only you shall not bring my son there again.”
verses.explainer: If the woman is unwilling, the servant is released from the oath. No one will be forced.

verses.text: The servant put his hand under the thigh of Abraham his master, and swore to him concerning this matter.
verses.explainer: The servant swears the oath.

verses.text: The servant took ten camels, of his master’s camels, and departed, having a variety of good things of his master’s with him. He arose, and went to Mesopotamia, to the city of Nahor.
verses.explainer: The servant takes ten camels loaded with gifts and travels to the city of Nahor in Mesopotamia. The caravan signals Abraham's wealth and the seriousness of the mission.

verses.text: He made the camels kneel down outside the city by the well of water at the time of evening, the time that women go out to draw water.
verses.explainer: He makes the camels kneel outside the city by the well at evening, when women come to draw water. Wells were the social centers of ancient communities.

verses.text: He said, “Yahweh, the God of my master Abraham, please give me success today, and show kindness to my master Abraham.
verses.explainer: The servant prays: 'O LORD, God of my master Abraham, please grant me success today and show steadfast love to my master.' He asks for specific guidance.

verses.text: Behold, I am standing by the spring of water. The daughters of the men of the city are coming out to draw water.
verses.explainer: He positions himself by the spring as the young women come out to draw water.

verses.text: Let it happen, that the young lady to whom I will say, ‘Please let down your pitcher, that I may drink,’ and she will say, ‘Drink, and I will also give your camels a drink,’—let her be the one you have appointed for your servant Isaac. By this I will know that you have shown kindness to my master.”
verses.explainer: He asks for a specific sign: the woman he asks for a drink who also offers to water his camels -- let her be the one appointed for Isaac. The sign tests both generosity and work ethic: watering ten camels is a massive undertaking.

thenVsNow.then: Ten camels can drink 25 gallons each -- 250 gallons total. Drawing that much water from a well by hand would take hours of hard physical labor. The servant is testing for extraordinary generosity and stamina.
thenVsNow.now: The servant's prayer for a specific sign has become a model for believers seeking guidance. The wisdom of his test lies in what it reveals about character: he is not looking for beauty or status but for generosity.

verses.text: Before he had finished speaking, behold, Rebekah came out, who was born to Bethuel the son of Milcah, the wife of Nahor, Abraham’s brother, with her pitcher on her shoulder.
verses.explainer: Before he finishes praying, Rebekah comes out with her jar on her shoulder. She is the daughter of Bethuel, son of Milcah, the wife of Abraham's brother Nahor. The answer comes before the prayer is complete.

verses.text: The young lady was very beautiful to look at, a virgin. No man had known her. She went down to the spring, filled her pitcher, and came up.
verses.explainer: Rebekah is very attractive, a virgin. She goes down to the spring, fills her jar, and comes up.

verses.text: The servant ran to meet her, and said, “Please give me a drink, a little water from your pitcher.”
verses.explainer: The servant runs to meet her and asks for a sip of water.

verses.text: She said, “Drink, my lord.” She hurried, and let down her pitcher on her hand, and gave him a drink.
verses.explainer: She says: 'Drink, my lord,' and quickly lowers her jar to give him water.

verses.text: When she had finished giving him a drink, she said, “I will also draw for your camels, until they have finished drinking.”
verses.explainer: After he drinks, she says: 'I will draw water for your camels too, until they have finished drinking.' She volunteers without being asked -- fulfilling the sign exactly.

verses.text: She hurried, and emptied her pitcher into the trough, and ran again to the well to draw, and drew for all his camels.
verses.explainer: She empties her jar into the trough, runs back to the well, and draws for all his camels. The running, the initiative, the exhausting labor -- Rebekah's character is demonstrated through action.

verses.text: The man looked steadfastly at her, remaining silent, to know whether Yahweh had made his journey prosperous or not.
verses.explainer: The servant watches her in silence, wondering whether the LORD has made his journey successful.

verses.text: As the camels had done drinking, the man took a golden ring of half a shekel weight, and two bracelets for her hands of ten shekels weight of gold,
verses.explainer: When the camels finish drinking, he gives her a gold nose ring and two gold bracelets. The gifts signal his master's wealth and his serious intent.

verses.text: and said, “Whose daughter are you? Please tell me. Is there room in your father’s house for us to stay?”
verses.explainer: He asks whose daughter she is and whether there is room at her father's house for lodging.

verses.text: She said to him, “I am the daughter of Bethuel the son of Milcah, whom she bore to Nahor.”
verses.explainer: She says: 'I am the daughter of Bethuel, the son of Milcah and Nahor.' She is Abraham's great-niece.

verses.text: She said moreover to him, “We have both straw and feed enough, and room to lodge in.”
verses.explainer: She adds: 'We have plenty of straw and fodder, and room to spend the night.'

verses.text: The man bowed his head, and worshiped Yahweh.
verses.explainer: The servant bows his head and worships the LORD.

verses.text: He said, “Blessed be Yahweh, the God of my master Abraham, who has not forsaken his loving kindness and his truth toward my master. As for me, Yahweh has led me on the way to the house of my master’s relatives.”
verses.explainer: He says: 'Blessed be the LORD, the God of my master Abraham, who has not forsaken his steadfast love and faithfulness. As for me, the LORD has led me in the way to the house of my master's kinsmen.' Providence and prayer have converged perfectly.

verses.text: The young lady ran, and told her mother’s house about these words.
verses.explainer: Rebekah runs to tell her mother's household. The news spreads.

verses.text: Rebekah had a brother, and his name was Laban. Laban ran out to the man, to the spring.
verses.explainer: Rebekah's brother Laban runs out to the servant when he sees the gold jewelry. Laban's motivation is hinted at -- he notices the wealth first.

verses.text: When he saw the ring, and the bracelets on his sister’s hands, and when he heard the words of Rebekah his sister, saying, “This is what the man said to me,” he came to the man. Behold, he was standing by the camels at the spring.
verses.explainer: Laban sees the ring and bracelets and hears Rebekah's account. He goes to the servant.

verses.text: He said, “Come in, you blessed of Yahweh. Why do you stand outside? For I have prepared the house, and room for the camels.”
verses.explainer: Laban invites the servant in: 'Come, blessed of the LORD. Why do you stand outside?'

verses.text: The man came into the house, and he unloaded the camels. He gave straw and feed for the camels, and water to wash his feet and the feet of the men who were with him.
verses.explainer: The servant enters. The camels are unloaded and fed. Water is provided for washing feet.

verses.text:
Food was set before him to eat, but he said, “I will not eat until I have told my message.”
He said, “Speak on.”
verses.explainer: Food is set before the servant, but he refuses to eat until he has told his errand. Business before pleasure.

verses.text: He said, “I am Abraham’s servant.
verses.explainer: He says: 'I am Abraham's servant.'

verses.text: Yahweh has blessed my master greatly. He has become great. He has given him flocks and herds, silver and gold, male servants and female servants, and camels and donkeys.
verses.explainer: He describes Abraham's wealth: sheep, cattle, silver, gold, servants, camels, donkeys. This establishes the quality of the match.

verses.text: Sarah, my master’s wife, bore a son to my master when she was old. He has given all that he has to him.
verses.explainer: He tells them Sarah bore a son in Abraham's old age, and Abraham has given Isaac everything.

verses.text: My master made me swear, saying, ‘You shall not take a wife for my son from the daughters of the Canaanites, in whose land I live,
verses.explainer: He recounts Abraham's oath: Isaac must not marry a Canaanite.

verses.text: but you shall go to my father’s house, and to my relatives, and take a wife for my son.’
verses.explainer: Abraham sent him to his father's house to find a wife for Isaac.

verses.text: I asked my master, ‘What if the woman will not follow me?’
verses.explainer: He recounts his concern that the woman might not come.

verses.text: He said to me, ‘Yahweh, before whom I walk, will send his angel with you, and prosper your way. You shall take a wife for my son from my relatives, and of my father’s house.
verses.explainer: Abraham assured him: 'The LORD, before whom I have walked, will send his angel with you and prosper your way.'

verses.text: Then will you be clear from my oath, when you come to my relatives. If they don’t give her to you, you shall be clear from my oath.’
verses.explainer: He would be released from the oath only if the family refused.

verses.text: I came today to the spring, and said, ‘Yahweh, the God of my master Abraham, if now you do prosper my way which I go—
verses.explainer: He recounts his prayer at the well.

verses.text: behold, I am standing by this spring of water. Let it happen, that the maiden who comes out to draw, to whom I will say, “Please give me a little water from your pitcher to drink,”
verses.explainer: He asked for a sign: the young woman who gives him water to drink...

verses.text: and she will tell me, “Drink, and I will also draw for your camels,”—let her be the woman whom Yahweh has appointed for my master’s son.’
verses.explainer: ...and offers to water his camels would be the one the LORD has appointed for Isaac.

verses.text: Before I had finished speaking in my heart, behold, Rebekah came out with her pitcher on her shoulder. She went down to the spring, and drew. I said to her, ‘Please let me drink.’
verses.explainer: Before he even finished praying silently, Rebekah appeared with her jar.

verses.text: She hurried and let down her pitcher from her shoulder, and said, ‘Drink, and I will also give your camels a drink.’ So I drank, and she also gave the camels a drink.
verses.explainer: She gave him water and offered to water the camels.

verses.text: I asked her, and said, ‘Whose daughter are you?’ She said, ‘The daughter of Bethuel, Nahor’s son, whom Milcah bore to him.’ I put the ring on her nose, and the bracelets on her hands.
verses.explainer: He asked whose daughter she was, and she identified herself as Bethuel's daughter. He gave her the ring and bracelets.

verses.text: I bowed my head, and worshiped Yahweh, and blessed Yahweh, the God of my master Abraham, who had led me in the right way to take my master’s brother’s daughter for his son.
verses.explainer: He bowed and worshipped the LORD who led him to the right place.

verses.text: Now if you will deal kindly and truly with my master, tell me. If not, tell me, that I may turn to the right hand, or to the left.”
verses.explainer: He asks: 'Will you show steadfast love and faithfulness to my master? Tell me, so I may turn to the right or to the left.' He needs a yes or no.

verses.text: Then Laban and Bethuel answered, “The thing proceeds from Yahweh. We can’t speak to you bad or good.
verses.explainer: Laban and Bethuel answer: 'This thing comes from the LORD; we cannot speak to you bad or good.' They recognize divine guidance.

verses.text: Behold, Rebekah is before you. Take her, and go, and let her be your master’s son’s wife, as Yahweh has spoken.”
verses.explainer: Rebekah is before the servant; he should take her and go. She shall be the wife of Abraham's son, as the LORD has spoken.

verses.text: When Abraham’s servant heard their words, he bowed himself down to the earth to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: The servant bows to the ground before the LORD. Prayer, fulfillment, gratitude -- the cycle is complete.

verses.text: The servant brought out jewels of silver, and jewels of gold, and clothing, and gave them to Rebekah. He also gave precious things to her brother and her mother.
verses.explainer: He brings out gold and silver jewelry and garments for Rebekah, and costly gifts for her brother and mother.

verses.text: They ate and drank, he and the men who were with him, and stayed all night. They rose up in the morning, and he said, “Send me away to my master.”
verses.explainer: They eat and drink and spend the night. In the morning, the servant says: 'Send me away to my master.'

verses.text: Her brother and her mother said, “Let the young lady stay with us a few days, at least ten. After that she will go.”
verses.explainer: Rebekah's brother and mother want her to stay at least ten days. They are reluctant to let her go.

verses.text: He said to them, “Don’t hinder me, since Yahweh has prospered my way. Send me away that I may go to my master.”
verses.explainer: The servant presses: 'Do not delay me, since the LORD has prospered my way.'

verses.text: They said, “We will call the young lady, and ask her.”
verses.explainer: They say: 'Let us call the young woman and ask her.' Remarkably, Rebekah is given a choice.

verses.text:
They called Rebekah, and said to her, “Will you go with this man?”
She said, “I will go.”
verses.explainer: They ask Rebekah: 'Will you go with this man?' She says: 'I will go.' Two words of extraordinary courage -- she chooses the unknown, just as Abraham did when God called him.

thenVsNow.then: In the ancient world, marriage was typically arranged without the woman's consent. That Rebekah is asked and that her answer matters is remarkable. Her willingness to leave everything for an unknown husband mirrors Abraham's willingness to leave everything for an unknown land.
thenVsNow.now: Rebekah's 'I will go' has inspired countless reflections on the courage of faith. She leaves her family, her homeland, and everything familiar for a man she has never met, trusting that God is in the arrangement.

verses.text: They sent away Rebekah, their sister, with her nurse, Abraham’s servant, and his men.
verses.explainer: They send Rebekah and her nurse with Abraham's servant.

verses.text: They blessed Rebekah, and said to her, “Our sister, may you be the mother of thousands of ten thousands, and let your offspring possess the gate of those who hate them.”
verses.explainer: They bless Rebekah: 'May you become thousands of ten thousands, and may your offspring possess the gate of their enemies.' The family blessing echoes God's covenant promises.

verses.text: Rebekah arose with her ladies. They rode on the camels, and followed the man. The servant took Rebekah, and went his way.
verses.explainer: Rebekah and her young women ride on the camels and follow the servant. They depart for Canaan.

verses.text: Isaac came from the way of Beer Lahai Roi, for he lived in the land of the South.
verses.explainer: Isaac has come from Beer-lahai-roi -- the well where God appeared to Hagar. He is living in the Negev.

verses.text: Isaac went out to meditate in the field at the evening. He lifted up his eyes and looked. Behold, there were camels coming.
verses.explainer: Isaac goes out to meditate in the field toward evening. He looks up and sees the camels coming.

verses.text: Rebekah lifted up her eyes, and when she saw Isaac, she got off the camel.
verses.explainer: Rebekah looks up and sees Isaac. She dismounts from the camel.

verses.text:
She said to the servant, “Who is the man who is walking in the field to meet us?”
The servant said, “It is my master.”
She took her veil, and covered herself.
verses.explainer: She asks the servant: 'Who is that man walking in the field to meet us?' The servant says: 'It is my master.' She covers herself with a veil.

verses.text: The servant told Isaac all the things that he had done.
verses.explainer: The servant tells Isaac everything he has done.

verses.text: Isaac brought her into his mother Sarah’s tent, and took Rebekah, and she became his wife. He loved her. Isaac was comforted after his mother’s death.
verses.explainer: Isaac brings Rebekah into his mother Sarah's tent. He takes her as his wife, and he loves her. Isaac is comforted after his mother's death. Love follows commitment; comfort follows grief. The chapter ends with tenderness.

explainer.summary: Abraham remarries, has more children, and dies at 175, buried by Isaac and Ishmael in the cave of Machpelah. Ishmael's genealogy is given (twelve princes, as promised). The narrative then shifts to Isaac and Rebekah, who struggle with infertility until God grants them twins. The twins struggle in the womb, and God tells Rebekah the older will serve the younger. Esau and Jacob are born -- Esau the red, hairy hunter; Jacob the smooth, quiet tent-dweller. The chapter ends with Esau selling his birthright to Jacob for a bowl of stew.
themes: The transition to the next generation
Twins in conflict from the womb
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter bridges the Abraham and Jacob narratives. Abraham's story ends; Jacob's begins. The twin conflict will dominate the next fifteen chapters.

verses.text: Abraham took another wife, and her name was Keturah.
verses.explainer: Abraham takes another wife, Keturah. He has more children in his old age, demonstrating continued vitality.

verses.text: She bore him Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, and Shuah.
verses.explainer: Keturah bears him Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, and Shuah. The Midianites, who play a role in later Israelite history, trace their ancestry here.

verses.text: Jokshan became the father of Sheba, and Dedan. The sons of Dedan were Asshurim, Letushim, and Leummim.
verses.explainer: Jokshan fathers Sheba and Dedan. The Arabian connections continue.

verses.text: The sons of Midian were: Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, and Eldaah. All these were the children of Keturah.
verses.explainer: Midian's sons: Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, and Eldaah. These are all sons of Keturah.

verses.text: Abraham gave all that he had to Isaac,
verses.explainer: Abraham gives everything he has to Isaac. The covenant inheritance goes to the son of promise.

verses.text: but to the sons of Abraham’s concubines, Abraham gave gifts. He sent them away from Isaac his son, while he yet lived, eastward, to the east country.
verses.explainer: Abraham gives gifts to his other sons and sends them away eastward, away from Isaac. He provides for them but separates them from the covenant heir.

verses.text: These are the days of the years of Abraham’s life which he lived: one hundred seventy-five years.
verses.explainer: Abraham lives 175 years.

verses.text: Abraham gave up his spirit, and died in a good old age, an old man, and full of years, and was gathered to his people.
verses.explainer: Abraham breathes his last and dies at a good old age, full of years, and is gathered to his people. 'Gathered to his people' suggests an afterlife where the dead are reunited.

verses.text: Isaac and Ishmael, his sons, buried him in the cave of Machpelah, in the field of Ephron, the son of Zohar the Hittite, which is before Mamre,
verses.explainer: Isaac and Ishmael bury Abraham in the cave of Machpelah. The two brothers reunite for their father's burial -- a moment of reconciliation.

verses.text: the field which Abraham purchased of the children of Heth. Abraham was buried there with Sarah, his wife.
verses.explainer: The field Abraham purchased from the Hittites -- where Sarah was buried. The cave bought for Sarah now holds Abraham too.

verses.text: After the death of Abraham, God blessed Isaac, his son. Isaac lived by Beer Lahai Roi.
verses.explainer: After Abraham's death, God blesses Isaac. Isaac settles at Beer-lahai-roi. The covenant blessing transfers to the next generation.

verses.text: Now this is the history of the generations of Ishmael, Abraham’s son, whom Hagar the Egyptian, Sarah’s servant, bore to Abraham.
verses.explainer: These are the generations of Ishmael, Abraham's son whom Hagar the Egyptian bore. Ishmael's line is documented before the narrative moves to Isaac.

verses.text: These are the names of the sons of Ishmael, by their names, according to the order of their birth: the firstborn of Ishmael, Nebaioth, then Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam,
verses.explainer: Ishmael's twelve sons by name: Nebaioth (firstborn), Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam.

verses.text: Mishma, Dumah, Massa,
verses.explainer: Mishma, Dumah, Massa.

verses.text: Hadad, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah.
verses.explainer: Hadad, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah.

verses.text: These are the sons of Ishmael, and these are their names, by their villages, and by their encampments: twelve princes, according to their nations.
verses.explainer: These are the twelve princes according to their settlements and camps -- twelve tribes from Ishmael, fulfilling God's promise in 17:20.

verses.text: These are the years of the life of Ishmael: one hundred thirty-seven years. He gave up his spirit and died, and was gathered to his people.
verses.explainer: Ishmael lives 137 years, then dies and is gathered to his people.

verses.text: They lived from Havilah to Shur that is before Egypt, as you go toward Assyria. He lived opposite all his relatives.
verses.explainer: Ishmael's descendants settle from Havilah to Shur, east of Egypt, toward Assyria. He settled over against all his kinsmen -- fulfilling the prophecy of 16:12.

verses.text: This is the history of the generations of Isaac, Abraham’s son. Abraham became the father of Isaac.
verses.explainer: These are the generations of Isaac, Abraham's son. Abraham fathered Isaac.

verses.text: Isaac was forty years old when he took Rebekah, the daughter of Bethuel the Syrian of Paddan Aram, the sister of Laban the Syrian, to be his wife.
verses.explainer: Isaac is 40 when he marries Rebekah, daughter of Bethuel the Aramean from Paddan-aram, sister of Laban.

verses.text: Isaac entreated Yahweh for his wife, because she was barren. Yahweh was entreated by him, and Rebekah his wife conceived.
verses.explainer: Isaac prays to the LORD for his wife because she is barren. The LORD answers his prayer, and Rebekah conceives. The pattern of barrenness followed by divine intervention continues: Sarah, now Rebekah, later Rachel.

verses.text: The children struggled together within her. She said, “If it is so, why do I live?” She went to inquire of Yahweh.
verses.explainer: The children struggle within her. Rebekah asks: 'If it is thus, why is this happening to me?' She goes to inquire of the LORD. The conflict begins before birth.

verses.text:
Yahweh said to her,
“Two nations are in your womb.
Two peoples will be separated from your body.
The one people will be stronger than the other people.
The elder will serve the younger.”
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Rebekah: 'Two nations are in your womb; two peoples will be separated from your body. One will be stronger than the other, and the older will serve the younger.' The divine oracle reverses the natural order of primogeniture.

thenVsNow.then: Primogeniture -- the firstborn's right to a double portion of inheritance and family leadership -- was the bedrock of ancient social order. God's oracle deliberately overturns this: the older will serve the younger.
thenVsNow.now: The pattern of God choosing the younger over the older (Isaac over Ishmael, Jacob over Esau, Joseph over his brothers, David over his brothers) challenges every human system of hierarchy based on birth order, seniority, or natural advantage.

verses.text: When her days to be delivered were fulfilled, behold, there were twins in her womb.
verses.explainer: When her days are fulfilled, there are indeed twins. The oracle is confirmed.

verses.text: The first came out red all over, like a hairy garment. They named him Esau.
verses.explainer: The first comes out red, all his body like a hairy garment, so they name him Esau. The description is vivid: red-haired, covered in hair from birth.

verses.text: After that, his brother came out, and his hand had hold on Esau’s heel. He was named Jacob. Isaac was sixty years old when she bore them.
verses.explainer: His brother comes out with his hand gripping Esau's heel, so he is named Jacob (Ya'aqov, meaning 'heel-grabber' or 'supplanter'). Isaac is 60 years old. Twenty years of waiting for children.

verses.text: The boys grew. Esau was a skillful hunter, a man of the field. Jacob was a quiet man, living in tents.
verses.explainer: The boys grow up: Esau becomes a skillful hunter, a man of the field. Jacob is a quiet man, dwelling in tents. The contrast is stark: outdoor vs. indoor, action vs. thought, impulsive vs. calculating.

verses.text: Now Isaac loved Esau, because he ate his venison. Rebekah loved Jacob.
verses.explainer: Isaac loves Esau because he eats his game. Rebekah loves Jacob. Parental favoritism -- the cancer that will tear this family apart.

verses.text: Jacob boiled stew. Esau came in from the field, and he was famished.
verses.explainer: Jacob is cooking stew when Esau comes in from the field, exhausted.

verses.text: Esau said to Jacob, “Please feed me with that same red stew, for I am famished.” Therefore his name was called Edom.
verses.explainer: Esau says: 'Let me eat some of that red stew, for I am exhausted!' He is called Edom ('red') because of this. He identifies himself by his appetite.

verses.text: Jacob said, “First, sell me your birthright.”
verses.explainer: Jacob says: 'Sell me your birthright first.' Jacob is calculating -- he sees an opportunity and pounces.

verses.text: Esau said, “Behold, I am about to die. What good is the birthright to me?”
verses.explainer: Esau says: 'I am about to die; of what use is a birthright to me?' He trades his future for his present hunger. The birthright included a double portion of inheritance and family leadership.

verses.text:
Jacob said, “Swear to me first.”
He swore to him. He sold his birthright to Jacob.
verses.explainer: Jacob makes Esau swear an oath, and Esau sells his birthright. A legal transaction, binding and irreversible.

verses.text: Jacob gave Esau bread and stew of lentils. He ate and drank, rose up, and went his way. So Esau despised his birthright.
verses.explainer: Jacob gives Esau bread and lentil stew. He eats and drinks, rises and goes his way. 'Thus Esau despised his birthright.' The narrator's verdict is devastating: Esau did not lose his birthright; he despised it.

explainer.summary: Isaac's own story is brief -- Genesis devotes far more space to Abraham and Jacob. During a famine, Isaac goes to Gerar (not Egypt, as God commands him to stay in the land). God renews the Abrahamic covenant with Isaac. But Isaac immediately repeats his father's wife-sister lie, calling Rebekah his sister. After Abimelech discovers the truth, Isaac prospers enormously, provoking the Philistines' jealousy. They stop up his wells, and Isaac digs new ones, naming them as he goes, until he reaches Beersheba, where God appears to him and Abimelech seeks a treaty.
themes: God renewing the covenant with the second generation
explainer.contextInBook: This is Isaac's only independent chapter. He is mostly a bridge between Abraham and Jacob, but here he demonstrates both the blessings and the weaknesses inherited from his father.

verses.text: There was a famine in the land, besides the first famine that was in the days of Abraham. Isaac went to Abimelech king of the Philistines, to Gerar.
verses.explainer: Another famine strikes the land, besides the earlier one in Abraham's day. Isaac goes to Abimelech king of the Philistines at Gerar. This may be a different Abimelech (the name may be a royal title, like Pharaoh).

verses.text: Yahweh appeared to him, and said, “Don’t go down into Egypt. Live in the land I will tell you about.
verses.explainer: The LORD appears to Isaac: 'Do not go down to Egypt. Dwell in the land I tell you.' God prevents Isaac from leaving the Promised Land.

verses.text: Live in this land, and I will be with you, and will bless you. For I will give to you, and to your offspring, all these lands, and I will establish the oath which I swore to Abraham your father.
verses.explainer: God promises to be with Isaac, bless him, and give his offspring all these lands, confirming the oath made to Abraham. The covenant is formally transferred.

verses.text: I will multiply your offspring as the stars of the sky, and will give all these lands to your offspring. In your offspring will all the nations of the earth be blessed,
verses.explainer: God will multiply Isaac's offspring as the stars of heaven and give them all these lands. In his offspring, all nations will be blessed. The Abrahamic promises repeat nearly verbatim.

verses.text: because Abraham obeyed my voice, and kept my requirements, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws.”
verses.explainer: This is because Abraham obeyed God's voice and kept his charge, commands, statutes, and laws. Abraham's obedience secures blessings for subsequent generations.

verses.text: Isaac lived in Gerar.
verses.explainer: Isaac settles in Gerar, as God instructed.

verses.text: The men of the place asked him about his wife. He said, “She is my sister,” for he was afraid to say, “My wife”, lest, he thought, “the men of the place might kill me for Rebekah, because she is beautiful to look at.”
verses.explainer: When the men of the place ask about his wife, Isaac says: 'She is my sister.' He fears they will kill him because Rebekah is beautiful. The same lie, the same fear, the third time this family has done this (12:13, 20:2, now 26:7).

verses.text: When he had been there a long time, Abimelech king of the Philistines looked out at a window, and saw, and, behold, Isaac was caressing Rebekah, his wife.
verses.explainer: After a long time, Abimelech looks out a window and sees Isaac laughing (or caressing) Rebekah. The Hebrew word for 'laughing' (metsacheq) is a wordplay on Isaac's name (Yitschaq).

verses.text:
Abimelech called Isaac, and said, “Behold, surely she is your wife. Why did you say, ‘She is my sister?’”
Isaac said to him, “Because I said, ‘Lest I die because of her.’”
verses.explainer: Abimelech confronts Isaac: 'She is your wife! Why did you say she is your sister?' Again, a pagan king has better ethics than a patriarch.

verses.text: Abimelech said, “What is this you have done to us? One of the people might easily have lain with your wife, and you would have brought guilt on us!”
verses.explainer: Abimelech says: 'One of the people might have slept with your wife, and you would have brought guilt on us.' Isaac's deception endangered the whole community.

verses.text: Abimelech commanded all the people, saying, “He who touches this man or his wife will surely be put to death.”
verses.explainer: Abimelech issues a decree protecting Isaac and Rebekah: anyone who touches them will be put to death. The pagan king provides the protection Isaac should have trusted God to provide.

verses.text: Isaac sowed in that land, and reaped in the same year one hundred times what he planted. Yahweh blessed him.
verses.explainer: Isaac sows in the land and reaps a hundredfold. The LORD blesses him.

verses.text: The man grew great, and grew more and more until he became very great.
verses.explainer: He becomes increasingly wealthy until he is very wealthy indeed.

verses.text: He had possessions of flocks, possessions of herds, and a great household. The Philistines envied him.
verses.explainer: He has flocks, herds, and many servants. The Philistines envy him.

verses.text: Now all the wells which his father’s servants had dug in the days of Abraham his father, the Philistines had stopped, and filled with earth.
verses.explainer: The Philistines stop up all the wells Abraham dug, filling them with earth. Blocking wells in an arid land is an act of hostility that threatens survival.

verses.text: Abimelech said to Isaac, “Go away from us, for you are much mightier than we.”
verses.explainer: Abimelech tells Isaac: 'Go away from us, for you have become much mightier than we.' Success has made Isaac unwelcome.

verses.text: Isaac departed from there, encamped in the valley of Gerar, and lived there.
verses.explainer: Isaac moves to the Valley of Gerar and settles there.

verses.text: Isaac dug again the wells of water, which they had dug in the days of Abraham his father. For the Philistines had stopped them after the death of Abraham. He called their names after the names by which his father had called them.
verses.explainer: Isaac digs again the wells Abraham had dug, which the Philistines had stopped up. He gives them the same names his father gave them. Recovering the father's wells is an act of reclaiming inheritance.

verses.text: Isaac’s servants dug in the valley, and found there a well of springing water.
verses.explainer: Isaac's servants dig and find a well of spring water.

verses.text: The herdsmen of Gerar argued with Isaac’s herdsmen, saying, “The water is ours.” He called the name of the well Esek, because they contended with him.
verses.explainer: The herdsmen of Gerar quarrel: 'The water is ours.' Isaac names the well Esek ('contention').

verses.text: They dug another well, and they argued over that, also. He called its name Sitnah.
verses.explainer: They dig another well. Another quarrel. He names it Sitnah ('enmity').

verses.text: He left that place, and dug another well. They didn’t argue over that one. He called it Rehoboth. He said, “For now Yahweh has made room for us, and we will be fruitful in the land.”
verses.explainer: Isaac moves on and digs another well. No one quarrels over it. He names it Rehoboth ('room'), saying: 'Now the LORD has made room for us, and we shall be fruitful in the land.' Isaac's strategy is to keep moving until conflict ends -- peace through patience.

verses.text: He went up from there to Beersheba.
verses.explainer: Isaac goes up to Beersheba.

verses.text: Yahweh appeared to him the same night, and said, “I am the God of Abraham your father. Don’t be afraid, for I am with you, and will bless you, and multiply your offspring for my servant Abraham’s sake.”
verses.explainer: The LORD appears to him that night: 'I am the God of Abraham your father. Fear not, for I am with you and will bless you and multiply your offspring for Abraham my servant's sake.' The covenant reassurance comes at a moment of displacement.

verses.text: He built an altar there, and called on Yahweh’s name, and pitched his tent there. There Isaac’s servants dug a well.
verses.explainer: Isaac builds an altar, calls on the LORD's name, and pitches his tent. The pattern continues: altar, prayer, tent. Worship, faith, dwelling.

verses.text: Then Abimelech went to him from Gerar, and Ahuzzath his friend, and Phicol the captain of his army.
verses.explainer: Abimelech comes to Isaac with his advisor and commander.

verses.text: Isaac said to them, “Why have you come to me, since you hate me, and have sent me away from you?”
verses.explainer: Isaac asks: 'Why have you come to me, since you hate me and sent me away?'

verses.text: They said, “We saw plainly that Yahweh was with you. We said, ‘Let there now be an oath between us, even between us and you, and let us make a covenant with you,
verses.explainer: They say: 'We see plainly that the LORD has been with you.' God's blessing on Isaac is so visible that even his enemies acknowledge it.

verses.text: that you will do us no harm, as we have not touched you, and as we have done to you nothing but good, and have sent you away in peace.’ You are now the blessed of Yahweh.”
verses.explainer: They propose an oath of peace, acknowledging that Isaac is 'the blessed of the LORD.'

verses.text: He made them a feast, and they ate and drank.
verses.explainer: Isaac makes them a feast. They eat and drink together.

verses.text: They rose up some time in the morning, and swore an oath to one another. Isaac sent them away, and they departed from him in peace.
verses.explainer: They rise early, exchange oaths, and part in peace.

verses.text: The same day, Isaac’s servants came, and told him concerning the well which they had dug, and said to him, “We have found water.”
verses.explainer: That same day, Isaac's servants report finding water. Good news follows the peace treaty.

verses.text: He called it Shibah. Therefore the name of the city is Beersheba to this day.
verses.explainer: Isaac names the well Shibah ('oath'). The city is called Beersheba to this day.

verses.text: When Esau was forty years old, he took as wife Judith, the daughter of Beeri the Hittite, and Basemath, the daughter of Elon the Hittite.
verses.explainer: Esau at 40 marries two Hittite women: Judith and Basemath. Esau marries Canaanite women, against his family's practice.

verses.text: They grieved Isaac’s and Rebekah’s spirits.
verses.explainer: They make life bitter for Isaac and Rebekah. The Canaanite marriages cause family strife and foreshadow Esau's disqualification from the covenant line.

explainer.summary: The great deception. Isaac, old and blind, prepares to give Esau the patriarchal blessing. Rebekah overhears and orchestrates a scheme for Jacob to impersonate Esau and steal the blessing. Jacob disguises himself with goat skins and Esau's clothing, brings Isaac a meal, and receives the blessing intended for his brother. When Esau arrives and the deception is discovered, Isaac trembles violently and Esau cries bitterly, but the blessing cannot be revoked. Esau vows to kill Jacob, and Rebekah sends Jacob away to her brother Laban.
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter is the crisis of the Jacob-Esau story. Every member of the family acts badly: Isaac favors Esau, Rebekah manipulates, Jacob lies, and Esau is willing to murder. Yet God's sovereign purpose -- the older serving the younger (25:23) -- is accomplished through this moral wreckage.

verses.text:
When Isaac was old, and his eyes were dim, so that he could not see, he called Esau his elder son, and said to him, “My son?”
He said to him, “Here I am.”
verses.explainer: Isaac is old and his eyes are dim. He calls Esau and tells him to prepare a meal so he can give him his blessing before he dies.

verses.text: He said, “See now, I am old. I don’t know the day of my death.
verses.explainer: Isaac says he does not know the day of his death and wants to bless Esau while he can. The urgency drives the narrative.

verses.text: Now therefore, please take your weapons, your quiver and your bow, and go out to the field, and take me venison.
verses.explainer: Isaac tells Esau to take his quiver and bow, hunt some game, and make him a savory dish.

verses.text: Make me savory food, such as I love, and bring it to me, that I may eat, and that my soul may bless you before I die.”
verses.explainer: He says: bring it to me so I may eat and bless you before I die. Isaac ties the blessing to a meal -- a sensory experience that will trigger his patriarchal authority.

verses.text: Rebekah heard when Isaac spoke to Esau his son. Esau went to the field to hunt for venison, and to bring it.
verses.explainer: Rebekah is listening. She hears the plan and immediately begins to counter it. She remembers the oracle: the older will serve the younger (25:23).

verses.text: Rebekah spoke to Jacob her son, saying, “Behold, I heard your father speak to Esau your brother, saying,
verses.explainer: Rebekah tells Jacob what she overheard.

verses.text: ‘Bring me venison, and make me savory food, that I may eat, and bless you before Yahweh before my death.’
verses.explainer: She relays Isaac's instructions to Esau word for word.

verses.text: Now therefore, my son, obey my voice according to that which I command you.
verses.explainer: Rebekah tells Jacob: 'Now, my son, obey my voice.' She takes full command.

verses.text: Go now to the flock and get me two good young goats from there. I will make them savory food for your father, such as he loves.
verses.explainer: She orders Jacob to get two goats so she can prepare a meal that Isaac likes.

verses.text: You shall bring it to your father, that he may eat, so that he may bless you before his death.”
verses.explainer: Jacob will bring it to Isaac, who will bless Jacob instead of Esau.

verses.text: Jacob said to Rebekah his mother, “Behold, Esau my brother is a hairy man, and I am a smooth man.
verses.explainer: Jacob objects -- not morally but practically: 'Esau is hairy and I am smooth. Father might touch me and discover the fraud.'

verses.text: What if my father touches me? I will seem to him as a deceiver, and I would bring a curse on myself, and not a blessing.”
verses.explainer: Jacob fears: 'I shall bring a curse upon myself, not a blessing.' His concern is consequences, not ethics.

verses.text: His mother said to him, “Let your curse be on me, my son. Only obey my voice, and go get them for me.”
verses.explainer: Rebekah says: 'Let your curse be on me, my son. Just obey my voice.' She absorbs the risk.

verses.text: He went, and got them, and brought them to his mother. His mother made savory food, such as his father loved.
verses.explainer: Jacob goes and gets the goats. Rebekah prepares the dish.

verses.text: Rebekah took the good clothes of Esau, her elder son, which were with her in the house, and put them on Jacob, her younger son.
verses.explainer: Rebekah takes Esau's best garments and puts them on Jacob. The smell of the field will be on him.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': "Esau's garments", 'explanation': "Clothing in the ancient world carried a person's identity -- their smell, their status, their occupation. Wearing Esau's garments was a form of identity theft. Isaac's blindness forces him to rely on smell and touch, which the disguise exploits."}

verses.text: She put the skins of the young goats on his hands, and on the smooth of his neck.
verses.explainer: She puts the goat skins on Jacob's hands and the smooth part of his neck to simulate Esau's hairiness.

verses.text: She gave the savory food and the bread, which she had prepared, into the hand of her son Jacob.
verses.explainer: She gives Jacob the prepared food and bread.

verses.text:
He came to his father, and said, “My father?”
He said, “Here I am. Who are you, my son?”
verses.explainer: Jacob goes to his father and says 'My father.' Isaac asks: 'Who are you, my son?'

verses.text: Jacob said to his father, “I am Esau your firstborn. I have done what you asked me to do. Please arise, sit and eat of my venison, that your soul may bless me.”
verses.explainer: Jacob lies: 'I am Esau your firstborn. I have done as you told me. Sit up and eat so you may bless me.' The lie is direct and comprehensive.

verses.text:
Isaac said to his son, “How is it that you have found it so quickly, my son?”
He said, “Because Yahweh your God gave me success.”
verses.explainer: Isaac asks: 'How did you find it so quickly, my son?' Jacob lies again: 'The LORD your God granted me success.' He invokes God to support his deception -- perhaps the worst moment in the scene.

verses.text: Isaac said to Jacob, “Please come near, that I may feel you, my son, whether you are really my son Esau or not.”
verses.explainer: Isaac is suspicious: 'Come near, so I may feel you.' Touch will be the test.

verses.text: Jacob went near to Isaac his father. He felt him, and said, “The voice is Jacob’s voice, but the hands are the hands of Esau.”
verses.explainer: Jacob comes near. Isaac feels him and says: 'The voice is Jacob's voice, but the hands are the hands of Esau.' Isaac doubts but proceeds.

verses.text: He didn’t recognize him, because his hands were hairy, like his brother, Esau’s hands. So he blessed him.
verses.explainer: Isaac does not recognize Jacob because his hands are hairy like Esau's. He blesses him.

verses.text:
He said, “Are you really my son Esau?”
He said, “I am.”
verses.explainer: Isaac asks one more time: 'Are you really my son Esau?' Jacob says: 'I am.' The lie is repeated under direct questioning.

verses.text:
He said, “Bring it near to me, and I will eat of my son’s venison, that my soul may bless you.”
He brought it near to him, and he ate. He brought him wine, and he drank.
verses.explainer: Isaac eats the meal and drinks wine.

verses.text: His father Isaac said to him, “Come near now, and kiss me, my son.”
verses.explainer: Isaac says: 'Come near and kiss me, my son.'

verses.text:
He came near, and kissed him. He smelled the smell of his clothing, and blessed him, and said,
“Behold, the smell of my son
is as the smell of a field which Yahweh has blessed.
verses.explainer: Jacob comes near and kisses him. Isaac smells Esau's garments and blesses him: 'The smell of my son is like the smell of a field the LORD has blessed.'

verses.text:
God give you of the dew of the sky,
of the fatness of the earth,
and plenty of grain and new wine.
verses.explainer: The blessing: 'May God give you the dew of heaven and the fatness of the earth, and plenty of grain and wine.' Agricultural abundance.

verses.text:
Let peoples serve you,
and nations bow down to you.
Be lord over your brothers.
Let your mother’s sons bow down to you.
Cursed be everyone who curses you.
Blessed be everyone who blesses you.”
verses.explainer: Let peoples serve you, and nations bow down to you. Be lord over your brothers. Cursed be everyone who curses you, and blessed be everyone who blesses you.' The blessing echoes God's promises to Abraham and the oracle about the twins.

thenVsNow.then: In the ancient world, a patriarchal blessing was not a wish but a performative speech act -- it set reality in motion. Once spoken, it could not be recalled. This is why Isaac trembles when he learns the truth.
thenVsNow.now: The irrevocability of the blessing raises profound questions about the relationship between divine sovereignty and human deception. God's purposes are accomplished, but through means that cause enormous suffering.

verses.text: As soon as Isaac had finished blessing Jacob, and Jacob had just gone out from the presence of Isaac his father, Esau his brother came in from his hunting.
verses.explainer: As soon as Isaac finishes blessing Jacob, Esau arrives from his hunt.

verses.text: He also made savory food, and brought it to his father. He said to his father, “Let my father arise, and eat of his son’s venison, that your soul may bless me.”
verses.explainer: Esau prepares a meal and brings it to his father: 'Let my father arise and eat.'

verses.text:
Isaac his father said to him, “Who are you?”
He said, “I am your son, your firstborn, Esau.”
verses.explainer: Isaac asks: 'Who are you?' Esau says: 'I am your son, your firstborn, Esau.'

verses.text: Isaac trembled violently, and said, “Who, then, is he who has taken venison, and brought it me, and I have eaten of all before you came, and have blessed him? Yes, he will be blessed.”
verses.explainer: Isaac trembles violently: 'Who was it then that hunted game and brought it to me? I ate it before you came and blessed him -- and indeed he shall be blessed.' Even in shock, Isaac cannot revoke the blessing.

verses.text: When Esau heard the words of his father, he cried with an exceeding great and bitter cry, and said to his father, “Bless me, even me also, my father.”
verses.explainer: Esau cries out with an exceedingly great and bitter cry: 'Bless me, even me also, O my father!' The most emotionally raw moment in Genesis.

verses.text: He said, “Your brother came with deceit, and has taken away your blessing.”
verses.explainer: Isaac says: 'Your brother came deceitfully and took away your blessing.'

verses.text: He said, “Isn’t he rightly named Jacob? For he has supplanted me these two times. He took away my birthright. See, now he has taken away my blessing.” He said, “Haven’t you reserved a blessing for me?”
verses.explainer: Esau says: 'Is he not rightly named Jacob? For he has cheated me these two times. He took my birthright, and now he has taken my blessing.' The name Jacob (heel-grabber/supplanter) proves prophetic.

verses.text: Isaac answered Esau, “Behold, I have made him your lord, and all his brothers I have given to him for servants. I have sustained him with grain and new wine. What then will I do for you, my son?”
verses.explainer: Isaac says: 'I have made him lord over you and given him all his brothers as servants. I have sustained him with grain and wine. What then can I do for you, my son?'

verses.text: Esau said to his father, “Have you but one blessing, my father? Bless me, even me also, my father.” Esau lifted up his voice, and wept.
verses.explainer: Esau says: 'Have you but one blessing, my father? Bless me, even me also, O my father!' Esau lifts up his voice and weeps.

verses.text:
Isaac his father answered him,
“Behold, of the fatness of the earth will be your dwelling,
and of the dew of the sky from above.
verses.explainer: Isaac gives Esau a secondary blessing: his dwelling will be away from the fatness of the earth and away from the dew of heaven.

verses.text:
By your sword will you live, and you will serve your brother.
It will happen, when you will break loose,
that you shall shake his yoke from off your neck.”
verses.explainer: Esau will live by the sword and serve his brother. But when he breaks free, he will throw off the yoke. A future of conflict and eventual independence.

verses.text: Esau hated Jacob because of the blessing with which his father blessed him. Esau said in his heart, “The days of mourning for my father are at hand. Then I will kill my brother Jacob.”
verses.explainer: Esau hates Jacob because of the blessing. He says in his heart: 'When my father dies, I will kill Jacob.' The pattern of Cain and Abel threatens to repeat.

verses.text: The words of Esau, her elder son, were told to Rebekah. She sent and called Jacob, her younger son, and said to him, “Behold, your brother Esau comforts himself about you by planning to kill you.
verses.explainer: Rebekah hears of Esau's plan. She tells Jacob: 'Esau plans to kill you.'

verses.text: Now therefore, my son, obey my voice. Arise, flee to Laban, my brother, in Haran.
verses.explainer: She says: 'Flee to my brother Laban in Haran.'

verses.text: Stay with him a few days, until your brother’s fury turns away;
verses.explainer: She says: 'Stay with him a while, until your brother's fury turns away.'

verses.text: until your brother’s anger turn away from you, and he forgets what you have done to him. Then I will send, and get you from there. Why should I be bereaved of you both in one day?”
verses.explainer: Rebekah fears losing both sons in one day -- Jacob to Esau's violence, and Esau to the death penalty for fratricide. She will never see Jacob again.

verses.text: Rebekah said to Isaac, “I am weary of my life because of the daughters of Heth. If Jacob takes a wife of the daughters of Heth, such as these, of the daughters of the land, what good will my life do me?”
verses.explainer: Rebekah tells Isaac she is weary of life because of the Hittite women (Esau's wives). She uses this as a pretext to send Jacob to Haran to find a wife -- avoiding mentioning the death threat.

explainer.summary: Isaac blesses Jacob again and sends him to Paddan-aram to find a wife from Laban's family. Esau, seeing his Canaanite wives displease his parents, takes an Ishmaelite wife. On his journey, Jacob stops for the night and dreams of a stairway reaching to heaven with angels ascending and descending. God stands at the top and renews the Abrahamic promises to Jacob personally. Jacob wakes up terrified, sets up a stone pillar, names the place Bethel ('house of God'), and makes a conditional vow.
themes: The transfer of the covenant promises to Jacob
Heaven touching earth in a dream
explainer.contextInBook: Jacob has just stolen a blessing and fled for his life. He is a fugitive and a fraud. And it is precisely at this low point that God appears to him. The covenant does not wait for worthy recipients.

wordStudies.translatedAs: House of God
wordStudies.meaning: House of God. Jacob renames the place from Luz to Bethel after his dream of the heavenly stairway. The name declares that this ordinary place of sleep became the gateway between heaven and earth.

verses.text: Isaac called Jacob, blessed him, and commanded him, “You shall not take a wife of the daughters of Canaan.
verses.explainer: Isaac calls Jacob and blesses him. He charges him not to take a Canaanite wife.

verses.text: Arise, go to Paddan Aram, to the house of Bethuel your mother’s father. Take a wife from there from the daughters of Laban, your mother’s brother.
verses.explainer: Isaac sends Jacob to Paddan-aram, to Bethuel's house, to take a wife from the daughters of Laban, Rebekah's brother.

verses.text: May God Almighty bless you, and make you fruitful, and multiply you, that you may be a company of peoples,
verses.explainer: Isaac blesses Jacob with the blessing of El Shaddai: 'May God Almighty bless you, make you fruitful, and multiply you.'

verses.text: and give you the blessing of Abraham, to you, and to your offspring with you, that you may inherit the land where you travel, which God gave to Abraham.”
verses.explainer: Isaac explicitly transfers the blessing of Abraham to Jacob: 'May he give the blessing of Abraham to you and your offspring, that you may take possession of the land.' The covenant passes from Abraham to Isaac to Jacob.

verses.text: Isaac sent Jacob away. He went to Paddan Aram to Laban, son of Bethuel the Syrian, Rebekah’s brother, Jacob’s and Esau’s mother.
verses.explainer: Isaac sends Jacob to Laban son of Bethuel the Aramean. Jacob departs.

verses.text: Now Esau saw that Isaac had blessed Jacob and sent him away to Paddan Aram, to take him a wife from there, and that as he blessed him he gave him a command, saying, “You shall not take a wife of the daughters of Canaan,”
verses.explainer: Esau sees that Isaac has blessed Jacob and sent him to find a non-Canaanite wife.

verses.text: and that Jacob obeyed his father and his mother, and was gone to Paddan Aram.
verses.explainer: Esau sees that Jacob obeyed his parents and went to Paddan-aram.

verses.text: Esau saw that the daughters of Canaan didn’t please Isaac, his father.
verses.explainer: Esau realizes his Canaanite wives displease his father. He belatedly tries to fix the problem.

verses.text: Esau went to Ishmael, and took, besides the wives that he had, Mahalath the daughter of Ishmael, Abraham’s son, the sister of Nebaioth, to be his wife.
verses.explainer: Esau goes to Ishmael and marries Mahalath, daughter of Ishmael, Abraham's son. He adds an Ishmaelite wife to his Canaanite wives, still not understanding what his parents wanted.

verses.text: Jacob went out from Beersheba, and went toward Haran.
verses.explainer: Jacob leaves Beersheba and goes toward Haran. The journey from southern Canaan to northern Mesopotamia is about 500 miles.

verses.text: He came to a certain place, and stayed there all night, because the sun had set. He took one of the stones of the place, and put it under his head, and lay down in that place to sleep.
verses.explainer: He reaches a certain place and stays the night because the sun has set. He takes a stone for a pillow and lies down. A fugitive sleeping on a rock -- this is not a man in a position of power.

verses.text: He dreamed. Behold, a stairway set upon the earth, and its top reached to heaven. Behold, the angels of God ascending and descending on it.
verses.explainer: Jacob dreams of a ladder (or stairway) set up on the earth with its top reaching to heaven. Angels of God are ascending and descending on it. The vision shows that heaven and earth are connected, and traffic flows between them. The angels go up first, suggesting they were already on earth before ascending.

thenVsNow.then: Mesopotamian ziggurats were designed as stairways between earth and heaven. At Babel, humans tried to build one. Here God reveals his own stairway, freely given to a sleeping fugitive.
thenVsNow.now: Jesus identifies himself as the true fulfillment of Jacob's ladder: 'You will see heaven open and the angels of God ascending and descending on the Son of Man' (John 1:51). Christ is the connection point between heaven and earth.

verses.text: Behold, Yahweh stood above it, and said, “I am Yahweh, the God of Abraham your father, and the God of Isaac. The land whereon you lie, to you will I give it, and to your offspring.
verses.explainer: The LORD stands above the ladder and says: 'I am the LORD, the God of Abraham your father and the God of Isaac.' God identifies himself through the covenant lineage.

verses.text: Your offspring will be as the dust of the earth, and you will spread abroad to the west, and to the east, and to the north, and to the south. In you and in your offspring will all the families of the earth be blessed.
verses.explainer: God promises Jacob the land, offspring like the dust of the earth spreading in every direction, and that all families of the earth will be blessed through him. The full Abrahamic covenant is restated to Jacob.

verses.text: Behold, I am with you, and will keep you, wherever you go, and will bring you again into this land. For I will not leave you, until I have done that which I have spoken of to you.”
verses.explainer: God adds a personal promise: 'I am with you and will keep you wherever you go, and will bring you back to this land. I will not leave you until I have done what I have promised.' This is the first time God promises to go with someone on a journey.

verses.text: Jacob awakened out of his sleep, and he said, “Surely Yahweh is in this place, and I didn’t know it.”
verses.explainer: Jacob wakes up and says: 'Surely the LORD is in this place, and I did not know it.' The fugitive discovers that God is present in the wilderness, not just in sacred spaces.

verses.text: He was afraid, and said, “How dreadful is this place! This is none other than God’s house, and this is the gate of heaven.”
verses.explainer: Jacob is afraid: 'How awesome is this place! This is none other than the house of God, and this is the gate of heaven.' Awe and fear mix.

verses.text: Jacob rose up early in the morning, and took the stone that he had put under his head, and set it up for a pillar, and poured oil on its top.
verses.explainer: Jacob takes the stone he used as a pillow, sets it up as a pillar, and pours oil on it. He marks the spot where heaven touched earth.

verses.text: He called the name of that place Bethel, but the name of the city was Luz at the first.
verses.explainer: He names the place Bethel (house of God). It was formerly called Luz.

verses.text: Jacob vowed a vow, saying, “If God will be with me, and will keep me in this way that I go, and will give me bread to eat, and clothing to put on,
verses.explainer: Jacob makes a vow -- but it is conditional: 'If God will be with me and keep me... and give me bread to eat and clothing to wear...'

verses.text: so that I come again to my father’s house in peace, and Yahweh will be my God,
verses.explainer: ...and I return safely to my father's house, then the LORD shall be my God. Jacob's faith is still transactional: if God performs, then Jacob will worship. He is not yet the man God will make him.

verses.text: then this stone, which I have set up for a pillar, will be God’s house. Of all that you will give me I will surely give a tenth to you.”
verses.explainer: Jacob promises that the stone he set up will be God's house, and he will give God a tenth of everything. Even conditional faith is a starting point.
