explainer.summary: Jacob arrives in Haran and meets Rachel at a well. He rolls the stone away, waters her flock, weeps, and kisses her. He works for Laban seven years for Rachel's hand, but on the wedding night, Laban substitutes Leah, the older daughter. The deceiver is deceived. Jacob works seven more years for Rachel. God sees that Leah is unloved and opens her womb, while Rachel remains barren. Leah bears four sons: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah -- each name reflecting her desperate hope for her husband's love.
themes: The pain of the unloved wife
explainer.contextInBook: Jacob, who deceived his father and stole from his brother, now experiences deception firsthand. The poetic justice is exact: the man who pretended to be the firstborn is given the firstborn daughter instead of the younger.

verses.text: Then Jacob went on his journey, and came to the land of the children of the east.
verses.explainer: Jacob continues his journey and comes to the land of the people of the east. He has arrived in Mesopotamia.

verses.text: He looked, and behold, a well in the field, and, behold, three flocks of sheep lying there by it. For out of that well they watered the flocks. The stone on the well’s mouth was large.
verses.explainer: He sees a well in the field with three flocks of sheep lying beside it. A large stone covers the well.

verses.text: There all the flocks were gathered. They rolled the stone from the well’s mouth, and watered the sheep, and put the stone again on the well’s mouth in its place.
verses.explainer: When all the flocks are gathered, the shepherds roll the stone away, water the sheep, and put the stone back. It is a communal resource with communal rules.

verses.text:
Jacob said to them, “My relatives, where are you from?”
They said, “We are from Haran.”
verses.explainer: Jacob asks the shepherds where they are from. They say: 'Haran.'

verses.text:
He said to them, “Do you know Laban, the son of Nahor?”
They said, “We know him.”
verses.explainer: Jacob asks if they know Laban, son of Nahor. They do.

verses.text:
He said to them, “Is it well with him?”
They said, “It is well. See, Rachel, his daughter, is coming with the sheep.”
verses.explainer: Jacob asks if Laban is well. They say: 'He is well, and here is Rachel his daughter, coming with the sheep.'

verses.text: He said, “Behold, it is still the middle of the day, not time to gather the livestock together. Water the sheep, and go and feed them.”
verses.explainer: Jacob suggests they water the sheep and take them back to pasture. He is trying to clear the scene.

verses.text: They said, “We can’t, until all the flocks are gathered together, and they roll the stone from the well’s mouth. Then we water the sheep.”
verses.explainer: The shepherds say they cannot water until all the flocks are gathered and the stone is rolled away.

verses.text: While he was yet speaking with them, Rachel came with her father’s sheep, for she kept them.
verses.explainer: While Jacob is still talking, Rachel arrives with her father's sheep. She is a shepherdess.

verses.text: When Jacob saw Rachel the daughter of Laban, his mother’s brother, and the sheep of Laban, his mother’s brother, Jacob went near, and rolled the stone from the well’s mouth, and watered the flock of Laban his mother’s brother.
verses.explainer: When Jacob sees Rachel with Laban's sheep, he rolls the stone from the well's mouth single-handedly and waters Laban's flock. Adrenaline-fueled strength -- he does alone what normally requires multiple shepherds.

verses.text: Jacob kissed Rachel, and lifted up his voice, and wept.
verses.explainer: Jacob kisses Rachel and weeps aloud. The emotion is overwhelming. After a 500-mile journey as a fugitive, he has found family and beauty.

verses.text: Jacob told Rachel that he was her father’s brother, and that he was Rebekah’s son. She ran and told her father.
verses.explainer: Jacob tells Rachel he is her father's relative, Rebekah's son. She runs and tells her father.

verses.text: When Laban heard the news of Jacob, his sister’s son, he ran to meet Jacob, and embraced him, and kissed him, and brought him to his house. Jacob told Laban all these things.
verses.explainer: Laban runs to meet Jacob, embraces and kisses him, and brings him to his house. Jacob tells Laban everything.

verses.text: Laban said to him, “Surely you are my bone and my flesh.” He stayed with him for a month.
verses.explainer: Laban says: 'You are my bone and flesh.' Family recognition. Jacob stays a month.

verses.text: Laban said to Jacob, “Because you are my brother, should you therefore serve me for nothing? Tell me, what will your wages be?”
verses.explainer: Laban says: 'Just because you are my relative, should you work for me for nothing? Tell me what your wages should be.'

verses.text: Laban had two daughters. The name of the elder was Leah, and the name of the younger was Rachel.
verses.explainer: Laban has two daughters: Leah the older and Rachel the younger.

verses.text: Leah’s eyes were weak, but Rachel was beautiful in form and attractive.
verses.explainer: Leah has weak (or tender) eyes. Rachel is beautiful in form and appearance. The contrast is deliberate and sets up the coming conflict.

verses.text: Jacob loved Rachel. He said, “I will serve you seven years for Rachel, your younger daughter.”
verses.explainer: Jacob loves Rachel. He offers to work seven years for her. This is an extraordinarily generous bride-price.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Bride-price', 'explanation': "In the ancient Near East, the groom or his family paid a bride-price (mohar) to the bride's family. It compensated for the loss of the daughter's labor and established the legal marriage. Jacob, a penniless fugitive, can only offer his labor."}

verses.text: Laban said, “It is better that I give her to you, than that I should give her to another man. Stay with me.”
verses.explainer: Laban says: 'Better I give her to you than to another man. Stay with me.'

verses.text: Jacob served seven years for Rachel. They seemed to him but a few days, for the love he had for her.
verses.explainer: Jacob works seven years for Rachel, and they seem like only a few days because of his love for her. One of the most romantic sentences in the Bible.

verses.text: Jacob said to Laban, “Give me my wife, for my days are fulfilled, that I may go in to her.”
verses.explainer: Jacob says: 'Give me my wife, for my time is completed.' He is ready to consummate the marriage.

verses.text: Laban gathered together all the men of the place, and made a feast.
verses.explainer: Laban gathers all the people and makes a feast.

verses.text: In the evening, he took Leah his daughter, and brought her to him. He went in to her.
verses.explainer: In the evening, Laban brings Leah to Jacob instead of Rachel, and Jacob sleeps with her. The deception exploits the darkness of the wedding tent and probably Jacob's intoxication from the feast.

thenVsNow.then: The switch was possible because the bride was veiled and the tent was dark. In a world without electric light, the darkness was absolute. Laban exploited every advantage.
thenVsNow.now: The irony is devastating: the man who disguised himself as his older brother to steal a blessing is given the older sister in disguise. The deceiver is deceived by the exact same kind of trick he used on his own father.

verses.text: Laban gave Zilpah his servant to his daughter Leah for a servant.
verses.explainer: Laban gives his servant Zilpah to Leah as her servant.

verses.text: In the morning, behold, it was Leah! He said to Laban, “What is this you have done to me? Didn’t I serve with you for Rachel? Why then have you deceived me?”
verses.explainer: In the morning, Jacob discovers it is Leah. He confronts Laban: 'What is this you have done to me? Did I not serve you for Rachel? Why have you deceived me?' The man who said 'I am Esau' now asks 'why have you deceived me?'

verses.text: Laban said, “It is not done so in our place, to give the younger before the firstborn.
verses.explainer: Laban says: 'It is not the custom in our country to give the younger before the firstborn.' The defense is culturally sound but deliberately ironic: the man who stole the firstborn's rights is now told the firstborn comes first.

verses.text: Fulfill the week of this one, and we will give you the other also for the service which you will serve with me yet seven other years.”
verses.explainer: Laban offers: complete the bridal week with Leah, then receive Rachel too -- for another seven years of service.

verses.text: Jacob did so, and fulfilled her week. He gave him Rachel his daughter as wife.
verses.explainer: Jacob agrees. He finishes the week with Leah and receives Rachel.

verses.text: Laban gave to Rachel his daughter Bilhah, his servant, to be her servant.
verses.explainer: Laban gives his servant Bilhah to Rachel as her servant.

verses.text: He went in also to Rachel, and he loved also Rachel more than Leah, and served with him yet seven other years.
verses.explainer: Jacob loves Rachel more than Leah. He serves Laban another seven years. The household is built on unequal love -- a recipe for disaster.

verses.text: Yahweh saw that Leah was hated, and he opened her womb, but Rachel was barren.
verses.explainer: The LORD sees that Leah is unloved and opens her womb. Rachel is barren. God shows particular concern for the rejected wife.

verses.text: Leah conceived, and bore a son, and she named him Reuben. For she said, “Because Yahweh has looked at my affliction; for now my husband will love me.”
verses.explainer: Leah conceives and bears a son: Reuben ('see, a son'). She says: 'The LORD has seen my affliction; now my husband will love me.' Each name is a prayer for love that Jacob will not give.

verses.text: She conceived again, and bore a son, and said, “Because Yahweh has heard that I am hated, he has therefore given me this son also.” She named him Simeon.
verses.explainer: She bears Simeon ('hearing'): 'The LORD has heard that I am hated.'

verses.text: She conceived again, and bore a son. Said, “Now this time will my husband be joined to me, because I have borne him three sons.” Therefore his name was called Levi.
verses.explainer: She bears Levi ('attached'): 'Now this time my husband will be attached to me, because I have borne him three sons.' The desperation in each name is heartbreaking.

verses.text: She conceived again, and bore a son. She said, “This time will I praise Yahweh.” Therefore she named him Judah. Then she stopped bearing.
verses.explainer: She bears Judah ('praise'): 'This time I will praise the LORD.' With Judah, Leah stops looking to Jacob for validation and turns to God. The shift from 'my husband will love me' to 'I will praise the LORD' is her spiritual breakthrough. Then she ceases bearing.

explainer.summary: The competition between Rachel and Leah intensifies as both women, along with their servants Bilhah and Zilpah, bear children for Jacob. Rachel's barrenness drives her to desperate measures -- offering Bilhah as a surrogate, bargaining for mandrakes, and finally conceiving Joseph herself. Jacob also negotiates his wages with Laban, using a clever breeding strategy with the livestock that makes him wealthy. The chapter is a portrait of a dysfunctional family producing the twelve tribes of Israel.
themes: The rivalry between Rachel and Leah
Surrogate motherhood and ancient fertility practices
The birth of the tribal ancestors
explainer.contextInBook: The births of the twelve sons (and one daughter) of Jacob form the origins of the twelve tribes of Israel. The names of these children -- born from jealousy, competition, and desperation -- will become the names of a nation.

verses.text: When Rachel saw that she bore Jacob no children, Rachel envied her sister. She said to Jacob, “Give me children, or else I will die.”
verses.explainer: Rachel, seeing she bears no children, is jealous of Leah. She says to Jacob: 'Give me children, or I shall die!' The desperation of barrenness in the ancient world was existential.

verses.text: Jacob’s anger burned against Rachel, and he said, “Am I in God’s place, who has withheld from you the fruit of the womb?”
verses.explainer: Jacob's anger burns: 'Am I in the place of God, who has withheld from you the fruit of the womb?' Jacob rightly identifies that fertility is God's domain, but his anger shows little compassion.

verses.text: She said, “Behold, my maid Bilhah. Go in to her, that she may bear on my knees, and I also may obtain children by her.”
verses.explainer: Rachel gives Jacob her servant Bilhah as a wife: 'She may bear on my behalf, that through her I also may have children.' The Sarah-Hagar pattern repeats.

verses.text: She gave him Bilhah her servant as wife, and Jacob went in to her.
verses.explainer: Rachel gives Bilhah to Jacob, and he sleeps with her.

verses.text: Bilhah conceived, and bore Jacob a son.
verses.explainer: Bilhah conceives and bears Jacob a son.

verses.text: Rachel said, “God has judged me, and has also heard my voice, and has given me a son.” Therefore called she his name Dan.
verses.explainer: Rachel names him Dan ('he judged'): 'God has judged me and heard my voice and given me a son.' Rachel counts Bilhah's son as her own.

verses.text: Bilhah, Rachel’s servant, conceived again, and bore Jacob a second son.
verses.explainer: Bilhah conceives again and bears a second son.

verses.text: Rachel said, “I have wrestled with my sister with mighty wrestlings, and have prevailed.” She named him Naphtali.
verses.explainer: Rachel names him Naphtali ('my wrestling'): 'With great wrestlings I have wrestled with my sister and have prevailed.' The competition is explicit.

verses.text: When Leah saw that she had finished bearing, she took Zilpah, her servant, and gave her to Jacob as a wife.
verses.explainer: When Leah sees she has stopped bearing, she gives her servant Zilpah to Jacob as a wife. The surrogate warfare escalates.

verses.text: Zilpah, Leah’s servant, bore Jacob a son.
verses.explainer: Zilpah bears Jacob a son.

verses.text: Leah said, “How fortunate!” She named him Gad.
verses.explainer: Leah names him Gad ('fortune' or 'a troop comes').

verses.text: Zilpah, Leah’s servant, bore Jacob a second son.
verses.explainer: Zilpah bears a second son.

verses.text: Leah said, “Happy am I, for the daughters will call me happy.” She named him Asher.
verses.explainer: Leah names him Asher ('happy'): 'Women will call me happy.'

verses.text: Reuben went in the days of wheat harvest, and found mandrakes in the field, and brought them to his mother, Leah. Then Rachel said to Leah, “Please give me some of your son’s mandrakes.”
verses.explainer: During wheat harvest, Reuben finds mandrakes in the field and brings them to Leah. Rachel asks for some.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Mandrakes', 'explanation': "Mandrakes (dudaim) were believed to be an aphrodisiac and fertility aid in the ancient world. The root of the mandrake plant resembles a human figure, which contributed to its mystical reputation. Rachel's desire for them reflects her desperate pursuit of fertility."}

verses.text:
She said to her, “Is it a small matter that you have taken away my husband? Would you take away my son’s mandrakes, also?”
Rachel said, “Therefore he will lie with you tonight for your son’s mandrakes.”
verses.explainer: Leah responds bitterly: 'Is it a small matter that you have taken away my husband? Would you take away my son's mandrakes also?' Rachel offers a night with Jacob in exchange for the mandrakes. Jacob has become a commodity traded between his wives.

verses.text:
Jacob came from the field in the evening, and Leah went out to meet him, and said, “You must come in to me; for I have surely hired you with my son’s mandrakes.”
He lay with her that night.
verses.explainer: When Jacob comes from the field, Leah meets him: 'You must come in to me, for I have hired you with my son's mandrakes.' Jacob sleeps with Leah. The patriarch is directed by his wives.

verses.text: God listened to Leah, and she conceived, and bore Jacob a fifth son.
verses.explainer: God listens to Leah, and she conceives and bears a fifth son.

verses.text: Leah said, “God has given me my hire, because I gave my servant to my husband.” She named him Issachar.
verses.explainer: Leah names him Issachar ('reward' or 'hired'): 'God has given me my reward because I gave my servant to my husband.'

verses.text: Leah conceived again, and bore a sixth son to Jacob.
verses.explainer: Leah conceives again and bears a sixth son.

verses.text: Leah said, “God has endowed me with a good dowry. Now my husband will live with me, because I have borne him six sons.” She named him Zebulun.
verses.explainer: Leah names him Zebulun ('honor' or 'dwelling'): 'God has endowed me with a good endowment; now my husband will honor me, because I have borne him six sons.'

verses.text: Afterwards, she bore a daughter, and named her Dinah.
verses.explainer: Afterward she bears a daughter, Dinah. The only daughter named, she will play a tragic role in chapter 34.

verses.text: God remembered Rachel, and God listened to her, and opened her womb.
verses.explainer: God remembers Rachel, listens to her, and opens her womb. The same word 'remember' (zakar) used for Noah (8:1). God acts on behalf of the desperate.

verses.text: She conceived, bore a son, and said, “God has taken away my reproach.”
verses.explainer: Rachel conceives and bears a son: 'God has taken away my reproach.'

verses.text: She named him Joseph, saying, “May Yahweh add another son to me.”
verses.explainer: She names him Joseph ('may he add'): 'May the LORD add to me another son.' Even in the moment of fulfillment, she looks forward to more. Joseph will become the most prominent of Jacob's sons.

verses.text: When Rachel had borne Joseph, Jacob said to Laban, “Send me away, that I may go to my own place, and to my country.
verses.explainer: After Joseph is born, Jacob asks Laban to send him away: 'Give me my wives and children, and let me go.'

verses.text: Give me my wives and my children for whom I have served you, and let me go; for you know my service with which I have served you.”
verses.explainer: Jacob reminds Laban of all his service and asks to depart.

verses.text: Laban said to him, “If now I have found favor in your eyes, stay here, for I have divined that Yahweh has blessed me for your sake.”
verses.explainer: Laban says: 'I have learned by divination that the LORD has blessed me because of you.' Even Laban recognizes God's blessing on Jacob.

verses.text: He said, “Appoint me your wages, and I will give it.”
verses.explainer: Laban offers: 'Name your wages, and I will give it.'

verses.text: He said to him, “You know how I have served you, and how your livestock have fared with me.
verses.explainer: Jacob recounts how hard he has worked and how Laban's livestock have increased.

verses.text: For it was little which you had before I came, and it has increased to a multitude. Yahweh has blessed you wherever I turned. Now when will I provide for my own house also?”
verses.explainer: The little Laban had before Jacob came has increased enormously. Jacob asks: 'When shall I provide for my own household?'

verses.text:
He said, “What shall I give you?”
Jacob said, “You shall not give me anything. If you will do this thing for me, I will again feed your flock and keep it.
verses.explainer: Laban asks: 'What shall I give you?' Jacob says: 'Give me nothing.' He proposes a different arrangement.

verses.text: I will pass through all your flock today, removing from there every speckled and spotted one, and every black one among the sheep, and the spotted and speckled among the goats. This will be my hire.
verses.explainer: Jacob will pass through all the flock and remove every speckled, spotted, and dark-colored animal. These will be his wages.

verses.text: So my righteousness will answer for me hereafter, when you come concerning my hire that is before you. Every one that is not speckled and spotted among the goats, and black among the sheep, that might be with me, will be considered stolen.”
verses.explainer: Jacob says his honesty will be self-evident: any animal among his wages that is not speckled or spotted will be considered stolen.

verses.text: Laban said, “Behold, let it be according to your word.”
verses.explainer: Laban agrees. It seems like a good deal for Laban -- most animals are solid-colored.

verses.text: That day, he removed the male goats that were streaked and spotted, and all the female goats that were speckled and spotted, every one that had white in it, and all the black ones among the sheep, and gave them into the hand of his sons.
verses.explainer: But Laban immediately cheats: he removes all the striped and spotted animals and gives them to his sons, three days' journey away.

verses.text: He set three days’ journey between himself and Jacob, and Jacob fed the rest of Laban’s flocks.
verses.explainer: Laban puts three days' distance between himself and Jacob. Jacob feeds the rest of Laban's flock.

verses.text: Jacob took to himself rods of fresh poplar, almond, plane tree, peeled white streaks in them, and made the white appear which was in the rods.
verses.explainer: Jacob takes fresh rods of poplar, almond, and plane trees, and peels white streaks in them, exposing the white.

verses.text: He set the rods which he had peeled opposite the flocks in the gutters in the watering-troughs where the flocks came to drink. They conceived when they came to drink.
verses.explainer: He sets the rods in the watering troughs where the flocks come to drink, opposite them, so they breed when they come to drink.

verses.text: The flocks conceived before the rods, and the flocks produced streaked, speckled, and spotted.
verses.explainer: The flocks breed before the rods and produce striped, speckled, and spotted offspring. The ancient belief was that what animals saw at conception affected their offspring.

verses.text: Jacob separated the lambs, and set the faces of the flocks toward the streaked and all the black in the flock of Laban: and he put his own droves apart, and didn’t put them into Laban’s flock.
verses.explainer: Jacob separates the lambs and makes the flocks face the striped and dark animals in Laban's flock. He builds his own flocks.

verses.text: Whenever the stronger of the flock conceived, Jacob laid the rods in front of the eyes of the flock in the gutters, that they might conceive among the rods;
verses.explainer: When the stronger animals breed, Jacob sets the rods before their eyes. When the weaker animals breed, he does not. So the stronger animals become Jacob's.

verses.text: but when the flock were feeble, he didn’t put them in. So the feebler were Laban’s, and the stronger Jacob’s.
verses.explainer: The feebler animals go to Laban; the stronger to Jacob.

verses.text: The man increased exceedingly, and had large flocks, female servants and male servants, and camels and donkeys.
verses.explainer: Jacob becomes exceedingly prosperous with large flocks, female and male servants, camels, and donkeys. The trickster prospers -- but God will later reveal (31:12) that it was divine intervention, not the rods, that produced the results.

explainer.summary: After twenty years with Laban, Jacob hears Laban's sons grumbling that he has taken their father's wealth. God tells Jacob to return home. Jacob gathers his wives, who agree to leave -- they feel exploited by their own father. Jacob flees secretly, but Rachel steals Laban's household gods. Laban pursues, God warns him in a dream not to harm Jacob, and the two men confront each other. After heated accusations, they make a covenant and part ways.
themes: Rachel's theft of the household gods
The end of the Laban chapter
explainer.contextInBook: Jacob has been away from the Promised Land for twenty years. God now calls him back to face the brother he wronged and to take possession of his inheritance.

verses.text: He heard the words of Laban’s sons, saying, “Jacob has taken away all that was our father’s. He has obtained all this wealth from that which was our father’s.”
verses.explainer: Jacob hears Laban's sons saying: 'Jacob has taken all that was our father's.' They see Jacob's prosperity as their loss.

verses.text: Jacob saw the expression on Laban’s face, and, behold, it was not toward him as before.
verses.explainer: Jacob notices that Laban's attitude toward him has changed. The welcome has worn off.

verses.text: Yahweh said to Jacob, “Return to the land of your fathers, and to your relatives, and I will be with you.”
verses.explainer: The LORD tells Jacob: 'Return to the land of your fathers and your kindred, and I will be with you.' The same God who met him at Bethel now sends him home.

verses.text: Jacob sent and called Rachel and Leah to the field to his flock,
verses.explainer: Jacob calls Rachel and Leah to the field for a private conversation away from Laban's household.

verses.text: and said to them, “I see the expression on your father’s face, that it is not toward me as before; but the God of my father has been with me.
verses.explainer: Jacob tells his wives that their father's attitude has changed, but God has been with him.

verses.text: You know that I have served your father with all of my strength.
verses.explainer: He reminds them he has served their father with all his strength.

verses.text: Your father has deceived me, and changed my wages ten times, but God didn’t allow him to hurt me.
verses.explainer: Laban has cheated him, changing his wages ten times. But God has not allowed Laban to harm him.

verses.text: If he said this, ‘The speckled will be your wages,’ then all the flock bore speckled. If he said this, ‘The streaked will be your wages,’ then all the flock bore streaked.
verses.explainer: When Laban said the speckled would be his wages, all the flock bore speckled. When Laban switched to striped, all the flock bore striped. God overruled Laban's schemes.

verses.text: Thus God has taken away your father’s livestock, and given them to me.
verses.explainer: God has taken away their father's livestock and given them to Jacob. Jacob credits God, not the rods.

verses.text: During mating season, I lifted up my eyes, and saw in a dream, and behold, the male goats which leaped on the flock were streaked, speckled, and grizzled.
verses.explainer: Jacob recounts a dream: in the breeding season, he saw that the male goats mating with the flock were striped, speckled, and spotted.

verses.text: The angel of God said to me in the dream, ‘Jacob,’ and I said, ‘Here I am.’
verses.explainer: The angel of God spoke to Jacob in the dream.

verses.text: He said, ‘Now lift up your eyes, and behold, all the male goats which leap on the flock are streaked, speckled, and grizzled, for I have seen all that Laban does to you.
verses.explainer: The angel said: 'I have seen everything Laban has been doing to you.' God was the one directing the breeding, not Jacob's trick with the rods.

verses.text: I am the God of Bethel, where you anointed a pillar, where you vowed a vow to me. Now arise, get out from this land, and return to the land of your birth.’”
verses.explainer: The angel identifies himself as the God of Bethel, where Jacob anointed the pillar and made a vow. He commands: 'Leave this land and return to the land of your birth.'

verses.text: Rachel and Leah answered him, “Is there yet any portion or inheritance for us in our father’s house?
verses.explainer: Rachel and Leah respond: 'Is there any portion or inheritance left for us in our father's house?' They feel disowned.

verses.text: Aren’t we considered as foreigners by him? For he has sold us, and has also used up our money.
verses.explainer: They say: 'Are we not regarded by him as foreigners? He sold us and has used up the money paid for us.' Laban treated his daughters' bride-price as profit rather than holding it in trust for them.

verses.text: For all the riches which God has taken away from our father, that is ours and our children’s. Now then, whatever God has said to you, do.”
verses.explainer: They conclude: 'All the wealth God has taken from our father belongs to us and our children. Do whatever God has told you.' United support for the departure.

verses.text: Then Jacob rose up, and set his sons and his wives on the camels,
verses.explainer: Jacob sets his children and wives on camels.

verses.text: and he took away all his livestock, and all his possessions which he had gathered, including the livestock which he had gained in Paddan Aram, to go to Isaac his father, to the land of Canaan.
verses.explainer: He drives away all his livestock and possessions, heading for his father Isaac in Canaan.

verses.text:
Now Laban had gone to shear his sheep: and Rachel stole the teraphim
 that were her father’s.
verses.explainer: While Laban is away shearing sheep, Rachel steals her father's household gods (teraphim). These small figurines may have been associated with inheritance rights or household protection.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Household gods (teraphim)', 'explanation': "Small figurines kept in homes for protection and divination. In some ancient Near Eastern cultures, possessing the family teraphim may have constituted a claim to the family inheritance. Rachel may have stolen them to secure her family's claim or simply out of superstition."}

verses.text: Jacob deceived Laban the Syrian, in that he didn’t tell him that he was running away.
verses.explainer: Jacob deceives Laban the Aramean by not telling him he is fleeing.

verses.text: So he fled with all that he had. He rose up, passed over the River, and set his face toward the mountain of Gilead.
verses.explainer: Jacob flees with everything he has, crosses the Euphrates, and heads for the hill country of Gilead.

verses.text: Laban was told on the third day that Jacob had fled.
verses.explainer: On the third day, Laban is told Jacob has fled.

verses.text: He took his relatives with him, and pursued him seven days’ journey. He overtook him in the mountain of Gilead.
verses.explainer: Laban takes his kinsmen and pursues Jacob for seven days, catching up in the hill country of Gilead.

verses.text: God came to Laban, the Syrian, in a dream of the night, and said to him, “Be careful that you don’t speak to Jacob either good or bad.”
verses.explainer: God comes to Laban in a dream at night: 'Be careful not to say anything to Jacob, either good or bad.' God protects Jacob from Laban's wrath.

verses.text: Laban caught up with Jacob. Now Jacob had pitched his tent in the mountain, and Laban with his relatives encamped in the mountain of Gilead.
verses.explainer: Laban overtakes Jacob. Jacob's tent is pitched in the hill country, and Laban encamps nearby.

verses.text: Laban said to Jacob, “What have you done, that you have deceived me, and carried away my daughters like captives of the sword?
verses.explainer: Laban accuses: 'What have you done, deceiving me and carrying off my daughters like captives of war?'

verses.text: Why did you flee secretly, and deceive me, and didn’t tell me, that I might have sent you away with mirth and with songs, with tambourine and with harp;
verses.explainer: He says: 'Why did you flee secretly and not tell me? I would have sent you off with joy, songs, tambourine, and lyre.' A dubious claim.

verses.text: and didn’t allow me to kiss my sons and my daughters? Now have you done foolishly.
verses.explainer: Laban says: 'You did not even let me kiss my grandchildren and daughters goodbye. You have done foolishly.'

verses.text: It is in the power of my hand to hurt you, but the God of your father spoke to me last night, saying, ‘Be careful that you don’t speak to Jacob either good or bad.’
verses.explainer: Laban admits: 'It is in my power to do you harm, but the God of your father spoke to me last night, saying be careful.' Only God's warning restrains him.

verses.text: Now, you want to be gone, because you greatly longed for your father’s house, but why have you stolen my gods?”
verses.explainer: Laban says: 'But why did you steal my gods?' He is personally hurt by this.

verses.text: Jacob answered Laban, “Because I was afraid, for I said, ‘Lest you should take your daughters from me by force.’
verses.explainer: Jacob explains he was afraid Laban would take his daughters by force.

verses.text: Anyone you find your gods with shall not live. Before our relatives, discern what is yours with me, and take it.” For Jacob didn’t know that Rachel had stolen them.
verses.explainer: Jacob says: 'Anyone with whom you find your gods shall not live.' He does not know Rachel took them. The declaration is rash and dangerous.

verses.text: Laban went into Jacob’s tent, into Leah’s tent, and into the tent of the two female servants; but he didn’t find them. He went out of Leah’s tent, and entered into Rachel’s tent.
verses.explainer: Laban searches Jacob's tent, Leah's tent, and the servants' tents. He finds nothing.

verses.text: Now Rachel had taken the teraphim, put them in the camel’s saddle, and sat on them. Laban felt around all the tent, but didn’t find them.
verses.explainer: Rachel has hidden the gods in the camel's saddle and is sitting on them. She tells her father: 'I cannot rise before you, for the way of women is upon me.' She lies about menstruation to avoid being searched.

verses.text: She said to her father, “Don’t let my lord be angry that I can’t rise up before you; for I’m having my period.” He searched, but didn’t find the teraphim.
verses.explainer: Laban searches but does not find the gods. Rachel's deception succeeds.

verses.text: Jacob was angry, and argued with Laban. Jacob answered Laban, “What is my trespass? What is my sin, that you have hotly pursued me?
verses.explainer: Jacob becomes angry and confronts Laban: 'What is my offense? What is my sin that you have hotly pursued me?'

verses.text: Now that you have felt around in all my stuff, what have you found of all your household stuff? Set it here before my relatives and your relatives, that they may judge between us two.
verses.explainer: Jacob challenges: 'What have you found of all your household goods? Set it here before my kinsmen and yours, that they may judge between us.'

verses.text: “These twenty years I have been with you. Your ewes and your female goats have not cast their young, and I haven’t eaten the rams of your flocks.
verses.explainer: Jacob catalogs his twenty years of service: Laban's ewes and goats never miscarried; Jacob never ate Laban's rams.

verses.text: That which was torn of animals, I didn’t bring to you. I bore its loss. Of my hand you required it, whether stolen by day or stolen by night.
verses.explainer: What was torn by wild animals, Jacob bore the loss himself. Laban demanded restitution for every lost animal.

verses.text: This was my situation: in the day the drought consumed me, and the frost by night; and my sleep fled from my eyes.
verses.explainer: Jacob endured heat by day and frost by night, and sleep fled from his eyes.

verses.text: These twenty years I have been in your house. I served you fourteen years for your two daughters, and six years for your flock, and you have changed my wages ten times.
verses.explainer: Twenty years: fourteen for the two daughters, six for the flock. Laban changed Jacob's wages ten times.

verses.text: Unless the God of my father, the God of Abraham, and the fear of Isaac, had been with me, surely now you would have sent me away empty. God has seen my affliction and the labor of my hands, and rebuked you last night.”
verses.explainer: Jacob says: 'If the God of my father -- the God of Abraham and the Fear of Isaac -- had not been on my side, you would have sent me away empty-handed. God saw my affliction and the labor of my hands and rebuked you last night.'

verses.text: Laban answered Jacob, “The daughters are my daughters, the children are my children, the flocks are my flocks, and all that you see is mine: and what can I do today to these my daughters, or to their children whom they have borne?
verses.explainer: Laban claims: 'The daughters are my daughters, the children are my children, the flocks are my flocks. Everything you see is mine.' He is delusional about his ownership.

verses.text: Now come, let us make a covenant, you and I; and let it be for a witness between me and you.”
verses.explainer: Laban proposes: 'Come, let us make a covenant.' A peace treaty to end the conflict.

verses.text: Jacob took a stone, and set it up for a pillar.
verses.explainer: Jacob takes a stone and sets it up as a pillar.

verses.text: Jacob said to his relatives, “Gather stones.” They took stones, and made a heap. They ate there by the heap.
verses.explainer: Jacob tells his kinsmen to gather stones. They make a heap and eat there.

verses.text: Laban called it Jegar Sahadutha, but Jacob called it Galeed.
verses.explainer: Laban calls it Jegar-sahadutha (Aramaic) and Jacob calls it Galeed (Hebrew). Both mean 'heap of witness.' The bilingual naming reflects their different cultures.

verses.text: Laban said, “This heap is witness between me and you today.” Therefore it was named Galeed
verses.explainer: Laban says: 'This heap is a witness between you and me today.'

verses.text: and Mizpah, for he said, “Yahweh watch between me and you, when we are absent one from another.
verses.explainer: It is also called Mizpah ('watchtower'): 'The LORD watch between you and me, when we are out of each other's sight.' Often quoted as a blessing but actually a veiled threat: God watches to make sure neither of us cheats.

verses.text: If you afflict my daughters, or if you take wives besides my daughters, no man is with us; behold, God is witness between me and you.”
verses.explainer: Laban stipulates: 'If you mistreat my daughters or take other wives, God is witness.' He does not trust Jacob.

verses.text: Laban said to Jacob, “See this heap, and see the pillar, which I have set between me and you.
verses.explainer: Laban points to the pillar and heap: these are witnesses.

verses.text: May this heap be a witness, and the pillar be a witness, that I will not pass over this heap to you, and that you will not pass over this heap and this pillar to me, for harm.
verses.explainer: Neither party will cross this boundary to harm the other. It functions as a border treaty.

verses.text: The God of Abraham, and the God of Nahor, the God of their father, judge between us.” Then Jacob swore by the fear of his father, Isaac.
verses.explainer: They swear by the God of Abraham and the God of Nahor. Jacob swears by the Fear of his father Isaac.

verses.text: Jacob offered a sacrifice in the mountain, and called his relatives to eat bread. They ate bread, and stayed all night in the mountain.
verses.explainer: Jacob offers a sacrifice on the mountain and invites his kinsmen to eat bread. They eat and spend the night.

verses.text: Early in the morning, Laban rose up, and kissed his sons and his daughters, and blessed them. Laban departed and returned to his place.
verses.explainer: Early in the morning, Laban kisses his grandchildren and daughters, blesses them, and departs for home. The Laban era ends.

explainer.summary: Jacob sends messengers ahead to Esau and learns that Esau is coming with 400 men. Terrified, Jacob divides his camp, prays desperately, and sends extravagant gifts ahead to appease his brother. That night, alone at the Jabbok River, Jacob wrestles with a mysterious man until daybreak. The man cannot overpower Jacob, touches his hip and dislocates it, but Jacob refuses to let go without a blessing. The man renames him Israel ('he struggles with God') and Jacob names the place Peniel ('face of God'). This is the defining moment of Jacob's life.
themes: A new name for a new identity
explainer.contextInBook: Jacob is caught between Laban behind him and Esau before him. In this vulnerable moment, God meets him -- not to comfort but to wrestle. The trickster must be broken before he can be blessed.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Most commonly understood as 'he struggles with God
wordStudies.meaning: Most commonly understood as 'he struggles with God' or 'God strives.' The name commemorates the wrestling match and defines the relationship between God and his people: it is a struggle, not a passive acceptance. The nation that will bear this name will live up to it.

verses.text: Jacob went on his way, and the angels of God met him.
verses.explainer: Jacob goes on his way, and the angels of God meet him. Angels met him when he left Canaan (Bethel, 28:12); they meet him again as he returns.

verses.text: When he saw them, Jacob said, “This is God’s army.” He called the name of that place Mahanaim.
verses.explainer: Jacob sees them and says: 'This is God's camp!' He names the place Mahanaim ('two camps'). The name will prove ironic when Jacob soon divides his own company into two camps.

verses.text: Jacob sent messengers in front of him to Esau, his brother, to the land of Seir, the field of Edom.
verses.explainer: Jacob sends messengers ahead to Esau in the land of Seir (Edom). After twenty years, he must face the brother he wronged.

verses.text: He commanded them, saying, “This is what you shall tell my lord, Esau: ‘This is what your servant, Jacob, says. I have lived as a foreigner with Laban, and stayed until now.
verses.explainer: He instructs the messengers: 'Say to my lord Esau: thus says your servant Jacob...' The man who stole the lordship now calls Esau 'my lord' and himself 'your servant.'

verses.text: I have cattle, donkeys, flocks, male servants, and female servants. I have sent to tell my lord, that I may find favor in your sight.’”
verses.explainer: Jacob tells Esau about his livestock and servants, hoping to find favor. He leads with his wealth, perhaps hoping it will satisfy Esau's sense of injustice.

verses.text: The messengers returned to Jacob, saying, “We came to your brother Esau. Not only that, but he comes to meet you, and four hundred men with him.”
verses.explainer: The messengers return: 'We came to your brother Esau, and he is coming to meet you, and four hundred men are with him.' Four hundred men is an army. The message brings no words from Esau -- only the ominous approach.

verses.text: Then Jacob was greatly afraid and was distressed. He divided the people who were with him, and the flocks, and the herds, and the camels, into two companies;
verses.explainer: Jacob is greatly afraid and distressed. He divides his people, flocks, herds, and camels into two camps, reasoning that if Esau attacks one, the other may escape.

verses.text: and he said, “If Esau comes to the one company, and strikes it, then the company which is left will escape.”
verses.explainer: This is a strategy born of terror, not faith. He is preparing for the worst.

verses.text: Jacob said, “God of my father Abraham, and God of my father Isaac, Yahweh, who said to me, ‘Return to your country, and to your relatives, and I will do you good,’
verses.explainer: Jacob prays: 'O God of my father Abraham and God of my father Isaac, O LORD who said to me, Return to your country and I will do you good.' He holds God to his word.

verses.text: I am not worthy of the least of all the loving kindnesses, and of all the truth, which you have shown to your servant; for with just my staff I crossed over this Jordan; and now I have become two companies.
verses.explainer: Jacob confesses: 'I am not worthy of the least of all the steadfast love and all the faithfulness that you have shown to your servant.' This is the first time Jacob has expressed humility before God. He crossed the Jordan with nothing but a staff; now he has two camps of wealth.

verses.text: Please deliver me from the hand of my brother, from the hand of Esau: for I fear him, lest he come and strike me, and the mothers with the children.
verses.explainer: He pleads: 'Please deliver me from the hand of my brother, from the hand of Esau, for I fear him, that he may come and attack me, the mothers with the children.' Raw, honest fear.

verses.text: You said, ‘I will surely do you good, and make your offspring as the sand of the sea, which can’t be counted because there are so many.’”
verses.explainer: Jacob reminds God of his promise: 'You said, I will surely do you good, and make your offspring as the sand of the sea.' He grounds his prayer in God's own words.

verses.text: He stayed there that night, and took from that which he had with him, a present for Esau, his brother:
verses.explainer: Jacob selects an enormous gift for Esau: 200 female goats, 20 male goats, 200 ewes, 20 rams.

verses.text: two hundred female goats and twenty male goats, two hundred ewes and twenty rams,
verses.explainer: 30 milking camels with their young, 40 cows, 10 bulls, 20 female donkeys, 10 male donkeys. The total is staggering -- a fortune in livestock.

verses.text: thirty milk camels and their colts, forty cows, ten bulls, twenty female donkeys and ten foals.
verses.explainer: He puts each herd in charge of a servant, with spaces between them. The gifts will arrive in waves, each one softening Esau's heart a little more.

verses.text: He delivered them into the hands of his servants, every herd by itself, and said to his servants, “Pass over before me, and put a space between herd and herd.”
verses.explainer: Jacob instructs the first servant: 'When Esau meets you and asks whose these are and where you are going...'

verses.text: He commanded the foremost, saying, “When Esau, my brother, meets you, and asks you, saying, ‘Whose are you? Where are you going? Whose are these before you?’
verses.explainer: Say: 'They belong to your servant Jacob. It is a gift sent to my lord Esau. And he is behind us.'

verses.text: Then you shall say, ‘They are your servant, Jacob’s. It is a present sent to my lord, Esau. Behold, he also is behind us.’”
verses.explainer: He gives the same instructions to the second and third servants. Each wave repeats the message.

verses.text: He commanded also the second, and the third, and all that followed the herds, saying, “This is how you shall speak to Esau, when you find him.
verses.explainer: Jacob thinks: 'I may appease him with the gift that goes ahead of me, and afterward I will see his face. Perhaps he will accept me.'

verses.text: You shall say, ‘Not only that, but behold, your servant, Jacob, is behind us.’” For, he said, “I will appease him with the present that goes before me, and afterward I will see his face. Perhaps he will accept me.”
verses.explainer: The gifts go ahead. Jacob stays in the camp that night.

verses.text: So the present passed over before him, and he himself stayed that night in the camp.
verses.explainer: That night Jacob rises, takes his two wives, two servants, and eleven children, and crosses the ford of the Jabbok.

verses.text: He rose up that night, and took his two wives, and his two servants, and his eleven sons, and crossed over the ford of the Jabbok.
verses.explainer: He sends them across the stream with everything he has.

verses.text: He took them, and sent them over the stream, and sent over that which he had.
verses.explainer: Jacob is left alone. And a man wrestles with him until daybreak. The most mysterious and transformative encounter in the Old Testament. The identity of the 'man' is gradually revealed -- he is more than human.

thenVsNow.then: The Jabbok is a tributary of the Jordan River. Its name sounds like the Hebrew for 'wrestle' (ye'abeq). Jacob (ya'aqov) wrestles (ye'abeq) at the Jabbok (yabboq). The wordplay is deliberate.
thenVsNow.now: This scene has become the defining image for the spiritual life as struggle. The name Israel -- 'he struggles with God' -- defines not just Jacob but all who follow: faith is not passive acceptance but active, even agonizing, engagement with God.

verses.text: Jacob was left alone, and wrestled with a man there until the breaking of the day.
verses.explainer: When the man sees he cannot overpower Jacob, he touches his hip socket, and it is dislocated. Jacob continues to hold on despite the pain.

verses.text: When he saw that he didn’t prevail against him, he touched the hollow of his thigh, and the hollow of Jacob’s thigh was strained, as he wrestled.
verses.explainer: The man says: 'Let me go, for the day has broken.' Jacob says: 'I will not let you go unless you bless me.' The grasper clings to what matters most.

verses.text:
The man said, “Let me go, for the day breaks.”
Jacob said, “I won’t let you go, unless you bless me.”
verses.explainer: The man asks: 'What is your name?' Jacob says: 'Jacob.' To speak his own name is to confess his identity: the heel-grabber, the supplanter, the deceiver.

verses.text:
He said to him, “What is your name?”
He said, “Jacob”.
verses.explainer: The man says: 'Your name shall no longer be Jacob, but Israel, for you have striven with God and with humans and have prevailed.' The old name dies; a new identity is given. The trickster becomes a prince.

verses.text: He said, “Your name will no longer be called Jacob, but Israel; for you have fought with God and with men, and have prevailed.”
verses.explainer: Jacob asks: 'Please tell me your name.' The man says: 'Why do you ask my name?' He does not give it -- but his actions reveal it. Then he blesses Jacob.

verses.text:
Jacob asked him, “Please tell me your name.”
He said, “Why is it that you ask what my name is?” He blessed him there.
verses.explainer: Jacob names the place Peniel ('face of God'): 'I have seen God face to face, and yet my life has been delivered.' He recognizes his wrestling partner as God himself.

verses.text: Jacob called the name of the place Peniel: for, he said, “I have seen God face to face, and my life is preserved.”
verses.explainer: The sun rises on Jacob as he passes Peniel. He is limping because of his hip. The new man walks with a limp -- transformed but permanently marked by the encounter.

thenVsNow.then: Jacob went into the night as a schemer running from his past. He emerged with a new name, a new identity, and a limp. He was simultaneously blessed and broken.
thenVsNow.now: The image of the wounded hero -- blessed by God but limping from the encounter -- has shaped countless reflections on spiritual transformation. Growth comes through struggle, and the marks of the struggle remain.

verses.text: The sun rose on him as he passed over Peniel, and he limped because of his thigh.
verses.explainer: Therefore the people of Israel do not eat the sinew of the hip to this day, because the man touched Jacob's hip socket. A dietary custom preserved as a memorial.

verses.text: Therefore the children of Israel don’t eat the sinew of the hip, which is on the hollow of the thigh, to this day, because he touched the hollow of Jacob’s thigh in the sinew of the hip.
verses.explainer: The sun rises on him. He limps across the Jabbok into his future, carrying both a blessing and a wound.

explainer.summary: The dreaded meeting with Esau turns out to be a tearful reconciliation. Jacob bows seven times as he approaches; Esau runs to him, embraces him, falls on his neck, and weeps. Esau initially refuses Jacob's gifts but eventually accepts them. They part peacefully -- Esau returns to Seir, and Jacob settles near Shechem, where he buys land and builds an altar.
themes: The end of the Jacob-Esau conflict
explainer.contextInBook: After the wrestling match, Jacob is a changed man -- humbled, limping, newly named. His encounter with Esau mirrors his encounter with God: he expected destruction but received embrace.

verses.text: Jacob lifted up his eyes, and looked, and, behold, Esau was coming, and with him four hundred men. He divided the children between Leah, Rachel, and the two servants.
verses.explainer: Jacob looks up and sees Esau coming with 400 men. He divides the children among Leah, Rachel, and the two servants. He places the servants and their children first, then Leah, then Rachel and Joseph last -- the loved ones most protected.

verses.text: He put the servants and their children in front, Leah and her children after, and Rachel and Joseph at the rear.
verses.explainer: He puts the servants first, Leah and her children next, Rachel and Joseph last.

verses.text: He himself passed over in front of them, and bowed himself to the ground seven times, until he came near to his brother.
verses.explainer: Jacob himself goes ahead and bows to the ground seven times as he approaches Esau. Seven bows is the gesture of a vassal before a king in ancient Near Eastern diplomacy.

verses.text: Esau ran to meet him, embraced him, fell on his neck, kissed him, and they wept.
verses.explainer: Esau runs to meet Jacob, embraces him, falls on his neck, and kisses him. They both weep. The man who vowed to kill Jacob (27:41) runs to embrace him. Grace comes from the last person Jacob expected.

thenVsNow.then: Esau's response is a complete reversal of his death threat. Twenty years have passed, and either time or God or both have softened his heart. The running, embracing, and weeping mirror the father's response to the prodigal son in Luke 15.
thenVsNow.now: Jesus' parable of the prodigal son (Luke 15:20) appears to echo this scene: the father runs, embraces, and kisses the returning son. Esau becomes an unlikely icon of grace and forgiveness.

verses.text:
He lifted up his eyes, and saw the women and the children; and said, “Who are these with you?”
He said, “The children whom God has graciously given your servant.”
verses.explainer: Esau asks: 'Who are these with you?' Jacob says: 'The children God has graciously given your servant.' Jacob credits God and uses the language of servitude.

verses.text: Then the servants came near with their children, and they bowed themselves.
verses.explainer: The servants approach with their children and bow down.

verses.text: Leah also and her children came near, and bowed themselves. After them, Joseph came near with Rachel, and they bowed themselves.
verses.explainer: Leah and her children come and bow down. Then Joseph and Rachel come and bow down.

verses.text:
Esau said, “What do you mean by all this company which I met?”
Jacob said, “To find favor in the sight of my lord.”
verses.explainer: Esau asks about all the droves he met. Jacob says: 'To find favor in your eyes, my lord.'

verses.text: Esau said, “I have enough, my brother; let that which you have be yours.”
verses.explainer: Esau says: 'I have enough, my brother. Keep what you have for yourself.' The man who sold his birthright for stew says he has enough. Esau has prospered on his own.

verses.text: Jacob said, “Please, no, if I have now found favor in your sight, then receive my present at my hand, because I have seen your face, as one sees the face of God, and you were pleased with me.
verses.explainer: Jacob says: 'If I have found favor in your sight, accept my gift, for I have seen your face, which is like seeing the face of God.' Jacob uses 'face' language -- he saw God's face at Peniel and now sees grace in Esau's face.

verses.text: Please take the gift that I brought to you, because God has dealt graciously with me, and because I have enough.” He urged him, and he took it.
verses.explainer: Jacob presses Esau to accept the gifts: 'God has dealt graciously with me, and I have enough.' The word 'enough' echoes Esau's. Both brothers have prospered. Esau accepts.

verses.text: Esau said, “Let us take our journey, and let us go, and I will go before you.”
verses.explainer: Esau proposes they travel together.

verses.text: Jacob said to him, “My lord knows that the children are tender, and that the flocks and herds with me have their young, and if they overdrive them one day, all the flocks will die.
verses.explainer: Jacob declines, citing the children and the nursing flocks that need to travel slowly.

verses.text: Please let my lord pass over before his servant, and I will lead on gently, according to the pace of the livestock that are before me and according to the pace of the children, until I come to my lord to Seir.”
verses.explainer: Jacob promises to come to Esau in Seir eventually. It is unclear whether he ever does.

verses.text:
Esau said, “Let me now leave with you some of the folk who are with me.”
He said, “Why? Let me find favor in the sight of my lord.”
verses.explainer: Esau offers to leave some of his men with Jacob. Jacob declines: 'There is no need.'

verses.text: So Esau returned that day on his way to Seir.
verses.explainer: Esau returns to Seir that day.

verses.text: Jacob traveled to Succoth, built himself a house, and made shelters for his livestock. Therefore the name of the place is called Succoth.
verses.explainer: Jacob goes to Succoth, builds a house, and makes shelters for his livestock. The name Succoth means 'shelters.'

verses.text: Jacob came in peace to the city of Shechem, which is in the land of Canaan, when he came from Paddan Aram; and encamped before the city.
verses.explainer: Jacob arrives safely at the city of Shechem in Canaan, coming from Paddan-aram. He camps before the city.

verses.text: He bought the parcel of ground where he had spread his tent, at the hand of the children of Hamor, Shechem’s father, for one hundred pieces of money.
verses.explainer: Jacob buys the piece of land where he pitches his tent from the sons of Hamor, Shechem's father, for a hundred pieces of money. Like Abraham purchasing Machpelah, this is a claim to the Promised Land through legitimate purchase.

verses.text: He erected an altar there, and called it El Elohe Israel.
verses.explainer: Jacob sets up an altar and calls it El-Elohe-Israel: 'God, the God of Israel.' For the first time, Jacob uses his new name in worship. The altar names God as his personal God.

explainer.summary: Dinah, Jacob's daughter by Leah, visits the women of the land. Shechem, the prince of the region, rapes her and then falls in love and wants to marry her. His father Hamor proposes a marriage alliance between the two peoples. Simeon and Levi, Dinah's full brothers, deceive the Shechemites into getting circumcised, then slaughter them while they are recovering. Jacob rebukes his sons for endangering the family, but they retort: 'Should he treat our sister like a prostitute?'
themes: The corruption of the covenant sign (circumcision)
Jacob's passivity and his sons' fury
explainer.contextInBook: This dark chapter shows the next generation's capacity for both victimization and violence. The covenant sign of circumcision is used as a weapon. Jacob's passive response contrasts sharply with his sons' extreme action.

verses.text: Dinah, the daughter of Leah, whom she bore to Jacob, went out to see the daughters of the land.
verses.explainer: Dinah, Leah's daughter, goes out to visit the women of the land. This is the first time Dinah acts independently in the narrative.

verses.text: Shechem the son of Hamor the Hivite, the prince of the land, saw her. He took her, lay with her, and humbled her.
verses.explainer: Shechem, the son of Hamor the Hivite, the prince of the land, sees her, seizes her, and rapes her. The verbs are violent and clear.

verses.text: His soul joined to Dinah, the daughter of Jacob, and he loved the young lady, and spoke kindly to the young lady.
verses.explainer: His soul is drawn to Dinah. He loves the young woman and speaks tenderly to her. The juxtaposition of rape and love is deeply disturbing.

verses.text: Shechem spoke to his father, Hamor, saying, “Get me this young lady as a wife.”
verses.explainer: Shechem tells his father Hamor: 'Get me this young woman for my wife.'

verses.text: Now Jacob heard that he had defiled Dinah, his daughter; and his sons were with his livestock in the field. Jacob held his peace until they came.
verses.explainer: Jacob hears that Shechem has defiled Dinah, but his sons are in the field with the livestock. He keeps quiet until they come home. Jacob's silence is troubling.

verses.text: Hamor the father of Shechem went out to Jacob to talk with him.
verses.explainer: Hamor goes out to Jacob to negotiate.

verses.text: The sons of Jacob came in from the field when they heard it. The men were grieved, and they were very angry, because he had done folly in Israel in lying with Jacob’s daughter; a thing ought not to be done.
verses.explainer: Jacob's sons come from the field. They are indignant and furious because Shechem has committed an outrage in Israel by lying with Jacob's daughter -- something that should not be done.

verses.text: Hamor talked with them, saying, “The soul of my son, Shechem, longs for your daughter. Please give her to him as a wife.
verses.explainer: Hamor proposes: 'My son Shechem's soul longs for your daughter. Please give her to him as a wife.'

verses.text: Make marriages with us. Give your daughters to us, and take our daughters for yourselves.
verses.explainer: Hamor proposes intermarriage between the two peoples: 'Give your daughters to us, and take our daughters for yourselves.'

verses.text: You shall dwell with us, and the land will be before you. Live and trade in it, and get possessions in it.”
verses.explainer: He offers: 'You shall dwell with us, and the land shall be open to you. Live and trade in it, and get property.'

verses.text: Shechem said to her father and to her brothers, “Let me find favor in your eyes, and whatever you will tell me I will give.
verses.explainer: Shechem offers to pay whatever bride-price they demand. He is willing to pay anything.

verses.text: Ask me a great amount for a dowry, and I will give whatever you ask of me, but give me the young lady as a wife.”
verses.explainer: He says: 'Ask me for as great a bride-price and gift as you will, and I will give whatever you say. Only give me the young woman as my wife.'

verses.text: The sons of Jacob answered Shechem and Hamor his father with deceit, and spoke, because he had defiled Dinah their sister,
verses.explainer: Jacob's sons answer Shechem and Hamor deceitfully. They set a trap using the covenant sign.

verses.text: and said to them, “We can’t do this thing, to give our sister to one who is uncircumcised; for that is a reproach to us.
verses.explainer: They say: 'We cannot give our sister to an uncircumcised man. That would be a disgrace to us.'

verses.text: Only on this condition will we consent to you. If you will be as we are, that every male of you be circumcised;
verses.explainer: They propose: 'Only on this condition will we agree: if you become like us, with every male among you circumcised.'

verses.text: then will we give our daughters to you, and we will take your daughters to us, and we will dwell with you, and we will become one people.
verses.explainer: Then we will intermarry, live with you, and become one people.

verses.text: But if you will not listen to us, to be circumcised, then we will take our sister, and we will be gone.”
verses.explainer: But if you will not be circumcised, we will take our sister and go.

verses.text: Their words pleased Hamor and Shechem, Hamor’s son.
verses.explainer: The proposal pleases Hamor and Shechem.

verses.text: The young man didn’t wait to do this thing, because he had delight in Jacob’s daughter, and he was honored above all the house of his father.
verses.explainer: Shechem does not delay because he delights in Dinah. He is the most honored of his father's house.

verses.text: Hamor and Shechem, his son, came to the gate of their city, and talked with the men of their city, saying,
verses.explainer: Hamor and Shechem go to the city gate to persuade the men.

verses.text: “These men are peaceful with us. Therefore let them live in the land and trade in it. For behold, the land is large enough for them. Let us take their daughters to us for wives, and let us give them our daughters.
verses.explainer: They tell the townspeople: 'These men are at peace with us. Let them live and trade in the land. There is plenty of room.'

verses.text: Only on this condition will the men consent to us to live with us, to become one people, if every male among us is circumcised, as they are circumcised.
verses.explainer: They explain the condition: every male must be circumcised.

verses.text: Won’t their livestock and their possessions and all their animals be ours? Only let us give our consent to them, and they will dwell with us.”
verses.explainer: They add the economic incentive: 'Will not their livestock, their property, and all their beasts be ours? Let us agree and they will dwell with us.'

verses.text: All who went out of the gate of his city listened to Hamor, and to Shechem his son; and every male was circumcised, all who went out of the gate of his city.
verses.explainer: All the men of the city agree and are circumcised.

verses.text: On the third day, when they were sore, two of Jacob’s sons, Simeon and Levi, Dinah’s brothers, each took his sword, came upon the unsuspecting city, and killed all the males.
verses.explainer: On the third day, when the men are still in pain, Simeon and Levi take their swords and attack the unsuspecting city, killing every male.

verses.text: They killed Hamor and Shechem, his son, with the edge of the sword, and took Dinah out of Shechem’s house, and went away.
verses.explainer: They kill Hamor and Shechem and take Dinah from Shechem's house.

verses.text: Jacob’s sons came on the dead, and plundered the city, because they had defiled their sister.
verses.explainer: Jacob's other sons plunder the city because their sister had been defiled.

verses.text: They took their flocks, their herds, their donkeys, that which was in the city, that which was in the field,
verses.explainer: They take the flocks, herds, donkeys, and everything in the city and the field.

verses.text: and all their wealth. They took captive all their little ones and their wives, and took as plunder everything that was in the house.
verses.explainer: They take all the wealth, all the women and children, and plunder the houses.

verses.text: Jacob said to Simeon and Levi, “You have troubled me, to make me odious to the inhabitants of the land, among the Canaanites and the Perizzites. I am few in number. They will gather themselves together against me and strike me, and I will be destroyed, I and my house.”
verses.explainer: Jacob rebukes Simeon and Levi: 'You have brought trouble on me by making me stink to the inhabitants of the land. We are few in number; if they gather against me, I shall be destroyed.' Jacob's concern is practical, not moral.

verses.text: They said, “Should he deal with our sister as with a prostitute?”
verses.explainer: They answer: 'Should he treat our sister like a prostitute?' The chapter ends with this unanswered question. No one comes out well: Shechem was a rapist, Jacob was passive, and Simeon and Levi were murderers.

explainer.summary: God tells Jacob to return to Bethel and build an altar. Jacob commands his household to put away foreign gods, purifies themselves, and journeys to Bethel, where God reaffirms his covenant and the name Israel. Rachel dies giving birth to Benjamin -- her last words name him 'son of my sorrow,' but Jacob renames him 'son of my right hand.' Reuben sleeps with Bilhah, Jacob's concubine, disqualifying himself as firstborn. Isaac dies and is buried by Esau and Jacob together.
themes: Return to Bethel and spiritual renewal
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter closes several narrative threads: Jacob's journey from Bethel comes full circle, Rachel dies completing the twelve sons, and Isaac passes away. The stage is set for the Joseph story.

verses.text: God said to Jacob, “Arise, go up to Bethel, and live there. Make there an altar to God, who appeared to you when you fled from the face of Esau your brother.”
verses.explainer: God tells Jacob: 'Arise, go up to Bethel and dwell there. Make an altar to the God who appeared to you when you fled from Esau.' God calls Jacob back to the place of his original encounter.

verses.text: Then Jacob said to his household, and to all who were with him, “Put away the foreign gods that are among you, purify yourselves, change your garments.
verses.explainer: Jacob tells his household: 'Put away the foreign gods that are among you, purify yourselves, and change your garments.' The family has accumulated pagan objects that must be removed.

verses.text: Let us arise, and go up to Bethel. I will make there an altar to God, who answered me in the day of my distress, and was with me on the way which I went.”
verses.explainer: Jacob says they will go to Bethel, where he will build an altar to God who answered him in his distress. He now acknowledges what God did for him.

verses.text: They gave to Jacob all the foreign gods which were in their hands, and the rings which were in their ears; and Jacob hid them under the oak which was by Shechem.
verses.explainer: They give Jacob all the foreign gods and the rings in their ears. Jacob buries them under the oak near Shechem. A symbolic burial of paganism.

verses.text: They traveled, and a terror of God was on the cities that were around them, and they didn’t pursue the sons of Jacob.
verses.explainer: As they journey, a terror from God falls on the surrounding cities, and no one pursues them. After the Shechem massacre, God protects the vulnerable family.

verses.text: So Jacob came to Luz (that is, Bethel), which is in the land of Canaan, he and all the people who were with him.
verses.explainer: Jacob arrives at Luz (Bethel) in Canaan with all who are with him.

verses.text: He built an altar there, and called the place El Beth El; because there God was revealed to him, when he fled from the face of his brother.
verses.explainer: He builds an altar and calls the place El-bethel ('God of Bethel') because there God revealed himself when Jacob fled from Esau.

verses.text: Deborah, Rebekah’s nurse, died, and she was buried below Bethel under the oak; and its name was called Allon Bacuth.
verses.explainer: Deborah, Rebekah's nurse, dies and is buried under an oak. The tree is called Allon-bacuth ('oak of weeping'). A small detail that suggests Rebekah has already died, and her nurse had joined Jacob's household.

verses.text: God appeared to Jacob again, when he came from Paddan Aram, and blessed him.
verses.explainer: God appears to Jacob again and blesses him.

verses.text: God said to him, “Your name is Jacob. Your name shall not be Jacob any more, but your name will be Israel.” He named him Israel.
verses.explainer: God says: 'Your name is Jacob; no longer shall your name be called Jacob, but Israel shall be your name.' The renaming is confirmed by God directly, not just by the wrestler at Peniel.

verses.text: God said to him, “I am God Almighty. Be fruitful and multiply. A nation and a company of nations will be from you, and kings will come out of your body.
verses.explainer: God identifies himself as El Shaddai: 'Be fruitful and multiply. A nation and a company of nations shall come from you, and kings shall come from your own body.'

verses.text: The land which I gave to Abraham and Isaac, I will give it to you, and to your offspring after you will I give the land.”
verses.explainer: God gives Jacob the land he gave to Abraham and Isaac. The covenant transfer is complete.

verses.text: God went up from him in the place where he spoke with him.
verses.explainer: God goes up from Jacob at the place where he spoke with him.

verses.text: Jacob set up a pillar in the place where he spoke with him, a pillar of stone. He poured out a drink offering on it, and poured oil on it.
verses.explainer: Jacob sets up a stone pillar and pours a drink offering and oil on it.

verses.text: Jacob called the name of the place where God spoke with him “Bethel”.
verses.explainer: Jacob names the place Bethel.

verses.text: They traveled from Bethel. There was still some distance to come to Ephrath, and Rachel travailed. She had hard labor.
verses.explainer: They journey from Bethel. When they are still some distance from Ephrath, Rachel goes into labor, and it is a difficult birth.

verses.text: When she was in hard labor, the midwife said to her, “Don’t be afraid, for now you will have another son.”
verses.explainer: During the hard labor, the midwife says: 'Do not fear, for you have another son.' Rachel's second son is born.

verses.text: As her soul was departing (for she died), she named him Benoni, but his father named him Benjamin.
verses.explainer: As her life is slipping away, Rachel names the boy Ben-oni ('son of my sorrow'). But Jacob renames him Benjamin ('son of my right hand'). Rachel's last act is to name her child; Jacob's response is to transform the name from sorrow to honor. Rachel dies.

thenVsNow.then: Death in childbirth was tragically common in the ancient world. Rachel's death fulfills the bitter irony of Jacob's rash oath in 31:32 -- the one who stole Laban's gods shall not live. Rachel, who stole the teraphim, dies.
thenVsNow.now: Rachel's tomb near Bethlehem became a place of pilgrimage. Jeremiah 31:15 pictures Rachel weeping for her children -- an image Matthew applies to the slaughter of the innocents near Bethlehem.

verses.text: Rachel died, and was buried on the way to Ephrath (also called Bethlehem).
verses.explainer: Rachel is buried on the way to Ephrath (Bethlehem). The beloved wife rests near the city where David -- and later Jesus -- will be born.

verses.text: Jacob set up a pillar on her grave. The same is the Pillar of Rachel’s grave to this day.
verses.explainer: Jacob sets a pillar on her grave. It remains Rachel's tomb to this day.

verses.text: Israel traveled, and spread his tent beyond the tower of Eder.
verses.explainer: Israel journeys on and pitches his tent beyond the tower of Eder.

verses.text:
While Israel lived in that land, Reuben went and lay with Bilhah, his father’s concubine, and Israel heard of it.
Now the sons of Jacob were twelve.
verses.explainer: While Israel lives in that region, Reuben sleeps with Bilhah, his father's concubine. Israel hears of it. The mention is brief and devastating. Sleeping with a father's concubine was an attempt to seize family authority. Reuben loses his firstborn rights for this act (49:3-4).

verses.text: The sons of Leah: Reuben (Jacob’s firstborn), Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun.
verses.explainer: The sons of Leah: Reuben (firstborn), Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun.

verses.text: The sons of Rachel: Joseph and Benjamin.
verses.explainer: The sons of Rachel: Joseph and Benjamin.

verses.text: The sons of Bilhah (Rachel’s servant): Dan and Naphtali.
verses.explainer: The sons of Bilhah (Rachel's servant): Dan and Naphtali.

verses.text: The sons of Zilpah (Leah’s servant): Gad and Asher. These are the sons of Jacob, who were born to him in Paddan Aram.
verses.explainer: The sons of Zilpah (Leah's servant): Gad and Asher. These are Jacob's sons, born in Paddan-aram (except Benjamin).

verses.text: Jacob came to Isaac his father, to Mamre, to Kiriath Arba (which is Hebron), where Abraham and Isaac lived as foreigners.
verses.explainer: Jacob comes to his father Isaac at Mamre (Hebron), where Abraham and Isaac sojourned.

verses.text: The days of Isaac were one hundred eighty years.
verses.explainer: Isaac lives 180 years.

verses.text: Isaac gave up the spirit, and died, and was gathered to his people, old and full of days. Esau and Jacob, his sons, buried him.
verses.explainer: Isaac breathes his last and dies, old and full of days. Esau and Jacob bury him together. Like Abraham's burial by Isaac and Ishmael, the estranged brothers unite for their father's funeral.

explainer.summary: A comprehensive genealogy of Esau and his descendants, who become the nation of Edom. The chapter records Esau's wives, sons, chieftains, and kings. It establishes Edom as a significant nation with organized leadership -- notably, Edom has kings before Israel does. This genealogy formally closes the Esau narrative before the text turns entirely to Jacob's family.
themes: Esau's prosperity and the nation of Edom
Kings in Edom before kings in Israel
The parting of ways between the brothers
explainer.contextInBook: As with Ishmael's genealogy (chapter 25), Esau's line is documented and set aside before the main narrative continues. Esau is blessed but is not the covenant line.

verses.text: Now this is the history of the generations of Esau (that is, Edom).
verses.explainer: These are the generations of Esau (that is, Edom). Esau's alternate name establishes the identity of his descendants as the Edomites.

verses.text: Esau took his wives from the daughters of Canaan: Adah the daughter of Elon, the Hittite; and Oholibamah the daughter of Anah, the daughter of Zibeon, the Hivite;
verses.explainer: Esau takes his wives from the Canaanites: Adah, Oholibamah, and Basemath. The names differ from earlier lists, possibly reflecting different naming traditions or marriage stages.

verses.text: and Basemath, Ishmael’s daughter, sister of Nebaioth.
verses.explainer: Basemath is Ishmael's daughter, sister of Nebaioth. The Ishmaelite-Edomite connection.

verses.text: Adah bore to Esau Eliphaz. Basemath bore Reuel.
verses.explainer: Adah bears Eliphaz to Esau. Eliphaz will appear later as Job's friend in the book of Job.

verses.text: Oholibamah bore Jeush, Jalam, and Korah. These are the sons of Esau, who were born to him in the land of Canaan.
verses.explainer: Oholibamah bears Jeush, Jalam, and Korah. Basemath bears Reuel. These are Esau's sons, born in Canaan.

verses.text: Esau took his wives, his sons, his daughters, and all the members of his household, with his livestock, all his animals, and all his possessions, which he had gathered in the land of Canaan, and went into a land away from his brother Jacob.
verses.explainer: Esau takes his wives, children, household members, livestock, and all property acquired in Canaan and moves away from Jacob.

verses.text: For their substance was too great for them to dwell together, and the land of their travels couldn’t bear them because of their livestock.
verses.explainer: Their possessions are too great for them to dwell together. The land cannot support both -- echoing the separation of Abraham and Lot (13:6).

verses.text: Esau lived in the hill country of Seir. Esau is Edom.
verses.explainer: Esau settles in the hill country of Seir. Esau is Edom.

verses.text: This is the history of the generations of Esau the father of the Edomites in the hill country of Seir:
verses.explainer: These are the generations of Esau, father of the Edomites, in the hill country of Seir.

verses.text: these are the names of Esau’s sons: Eliphaz, the son of Adah, the wife of Esau; and Reuel, the son of Basemath, the wife of Esau.
verses.explainer: Esau's sons: Eliphaz (son of Adah) and Reuel (son of Basemath).

verses.text: The sons of Eliphaz were Teman, Omar, Zepho, and Gatam, and Kenaz.
verses.explainer: Eliphaz's sons: Teman, Omar, Zepho, Gatam, and Kenaz.

verses.text: Timna was concubine to Eliphaz, Esau’s son; and she bore to Eliphaz Amalek. These are the sons of Adah, Esau’s wife.
verses.explainer: Timna, a concubine of Eliphaz, bears Amalek. The Amalekites become one of Israel's most persistent enemies.

verses.text: These are the sons of Reuel: Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah. These were the sons of Basemath, Esau’s wife.
verses.explainer: Reuel's sons: Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah. These are the sons of Basemath, Esau's wife.

verses.text: These were the sons of Oholibamah, the daughter of Anah, the daughter of Zibeon, Esau’s wife: she bore to Esau Jeush, Jalam, and Korah.
verses.explainer: Oholibamah's sons: Jeush, Jalam, and Korah.
