verses.text: These are the chiefs of the sons of Esau: the sons of Eliphaz the firstborn of Esau: chief Teman, chief Omar, chief Zepho, chief Kenaz,
verses.explainer: The chiefs (alluphim) of Esau's descendants are listed. Chief Teman, Chief Omar, Chief Zepho, Chief Kenaz.

verses.text: chief Korah, chief Gatam, chief Amalek: these are the chiefs who came of Eliphaz in the land of Edom; these are the sons of Adah.
verses.explainer: Chief Korah, Chief Gatam, Chief Amalek. These are the chiefs descended from Eliphaz in Edom.

verses.text: These are the sons of Reuel, Esau’s son: chief Nahath, chief Zerah, chief Shammah, chief Mizzah: these are the chiefs who came of Reuel in the land of Edom; these are the sons of Basemath, Esau’s wife.
verses.explainer: Chiefs from Reuel: Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, Mizzah. These are chiefs in Edom descended from Basemath.

verses.text: These are the sons of Oholibamah, Esau’s wife: chief Jeush, chief Jalam, chief Korah: these are the chiefs who came of Oholibamah the daughter of Anah, Esau’s wife.
verses.explainer: Chiefs from Oholibamah: Jeush, Jalam, Korah.

verses.text: These are the sons of Esau (that is, Edom), and these are their chiefs.
verses.explainer: These are the sons of Esau (Edom) and their chiefs.

verses.text: These are the sons of Seir the Horite, the inhabitants of the land: Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah,
verses.explainer: The sons of Seir the Horite, the original inhabitants of the land: Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah.

verses.text: Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan. These are the chiefs who came of the Horites, the children of Seir in the land of Edom.
verses.explainer: Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan. These are the Horite chiefs in the land of Seir.

verses.text: The children of Lotan were Hori and Heman. Lotan’s sister was Timna.
verses.explainer: Lotan's sons: Hori and Hemam. Lotan's sister is Timna (Eliphaz's concubine, mother of Amalek).

verses.text: These are the children of Shobal: Alvan, Manahath, Ebal, Shepho, and Onam.
verses.explainer: Shobal's sons: Alvan, Manahath, Ebal, Shepho, and Onam.

verses.text: These are the children of Zibeon: Aiah and Anah. This is Anah who found the hot springs in the wilderness, as he fed the donkeys of Zibeon his father.
verses.explainer: Zibeon's sons: Aiah and Anah. Anah is the one who found the hot springs in the wilderness while pasturing his father's donkeys.

verses.text: These are the children of Anah: Dishon and Oholibamah, the daughter of Anah.
verses.explainer: Anah's children: Dishon and Oholibamah (Esau's wife).

verses.text: These are the children of Dishon: Hemdan, Eshban, Ithran, and Cheran.
verses.explainer: Dishon's sons: Hemdan, Eshban, Ithran, and Cheran.

verses.text: These are the children of Ezer: Bilhan, Zaavan, and Akan.
verses.explainer: Ezer's sons: Bilhan, Zaavan, and Akan.

verses.text: These are the children of Dishan: Uz and Aran.
verses.explainer: Dishan's sons: Uz and Aran.

verses.text: These are the chiefs who came of the Horites: chief Lotan, chief Shobal, chief Zibeon, chief Anah,
verses.explainer: The Horite chiefs: Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah.

verses.text: chief Dishon, chief Ezer, and chief Dishan: these are the chiefs who came of the Horites, according to their chiefs in the land of Seir.
verses.explainer: Dishon, Ezer, Dishan. These are the Horite chiefs in Seir.

verses.text: These are the kings who reigned in the land of Edom, before any king reigned over the children of Israel.
verses.explainer: These are the kings who reigned in the land of Edom before any king reigned over the Israelites. This note presupposes the later Israelite monarchy and is often seen as evidence of later editorial work.

verses.text: Bela, the son of Beor, reigned in Edom. The name of his city was Dinhabah.
verses.explainer: Bela son of Beor reigned in Edom, with his city Dinhabah.

verses.text: Bela died, and Jobab, the son of Zerah of Bozrah, reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: When Bela died, Jobab son of Zerah from Bozrah reigned.

verses.text: Jobab died, and Husham of the land of the Temanites reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: When Jobab died, Husham from the land of the Temanites reigned.

verses.text: Husham died, and Hadad, the son of Bedad, who struck Midian in the field of Moab, reigned in his place. The name of his city was Avith.
verses.explainer: When Husham died, Hadad son of Bedad reigned. He defeated Midian in the country of Moab. His city was Avith.

verses.text: Hadad died, and Samlah of Masrekah reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: When Hadad died, Samlah from Masrekah reigned.

verses.text: Samlah died, and Shaul of Rehoboth by the river, reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: When Samlah died, Shaul from Rehoboth on the Euphrates reigned.

verses.text: Shaul died, and Baal Hanan, the son of Achbor reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: When Shaul died, Baal-hanan son of Achbor reigned.

verses.text: Baal Hanan the son of Achbor died, and Hadar reigned in his place. The name of his city was Pau. His wife’s name was Mehetabel, the daughter of Matred, the daughter of Mezahab.
verses.explainer: When Baal-hanan died, Hadar reigned. His city was Pau, and his wife was Mehetabel.

verses.text: These are the names of the chiefs who came from Esau, according to their families, after their places, and by their names: chief Timna, chief Alvah, chief Jetheth,
verses.explainer: These are the chiefs of Esau by name, clan, and territory: Timna, Alvah, Jetheth.

verses.text: chief Oholibamah, chief Elah, chief Pinon,
verses.explainer: Oholibamah, Elah, Pinon.

verses.text: chief Kenaz, chief Teman, chief Mibzar,
verses.explainer: Kenaz, Teman, Mibzar.

verses.text: chief Magdiel, and chief Iram. These are the chiefs of Edom, according to their habitations in the land of their possession. This is Esau, the father of the Edomites.
verses.explainer: Magdiel and Iram. These are the chiefs of Edom according to their dwelling places. This is Esau, the father of Edom.

explainer.summary: The Joseph story begins. Jacob loves Joseph more than his other sons and gives him a special coat. Joseph's brothers hate him, and when he reports two dreams showing them bowing to him, the hatred deepens. When Joseph visits his brothers in the field, they plot to kill him. Reuben persuades them to throw him into a pit instead, planning to rescue him. While Reuben is away, Judah suggests selling Joseph to passing merchants. Joseph is taken to Egypt and sold to Potiphar. The brothers dip the coat in goat's blood and tell Jacob that Joseph is dead.
themes: The beginning of Joseph's journey to Egypt
explainer.contextInBook: The Joseph story (chapters 37-50) is the most sustained narrative in Genesis. It is also the bridge between the patriarchal era and the Exodus: Joseph's journey to Egypt sets the stage for Israel's sojourn, slavery, and eventual liberation.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The exact meaning is uncertain
wordStudies.meaning: The exact meaning is uncertain. It may be a 'long-sleeved robe,' a 'richly ornamented garment,' or a 'coat of many colors' (following the Greek translation). Whatever its design, it marked Joseph as the favored son and signaled Jacob's intention to give him the primary inheritance.

verses.text: Jacob lived in the land of his father’s travels, in the land of Canaan.
verses.explainer: Jacob dwells in the land of his father's sojournings, in the land of Canaan. After all his wandering, he is back in the Promised Land.

verses.text: This is the history of the generations of Jacob. Joseph, being seventeen years old, was feeding the flock with his brothers. He was a boy with the sons of Bilhah and Zilpah, his father’s wives. Joseph brought an evil report of them to their father.
verses.explainer: These are the generations of Jacob. Joseph is seventeen, a helper with the flocks alongside his brothers (the sons of Bilhah and Zilpah). Joseph brings a bad report about them to their father. The tale-bearing makes him unpopular immediately.

verses.text: Now Israel loved Joseph more than all his children, because he was the son of his old age, and he made him a coat of many colors.
verses.explainer: Israel loves Joseph more than any of his sons because he is the son of his old age. He makes him a coat of many colors (ketonet passim). The favoritism is public and visible -- the coat is a walking advertisement of Joseph's privileged status.

thenVsNow.then: A distinctive garment marked the wearer as exempt from manual labor and designated for leadership. By giving Joseph this coat, Jacob was publicly announcing that Joseph -- the eleventh son, not the firstborn -- was his chosen heir.
thenVsNow.now: The phrase 'coat of many colors' has become a cultural idiom for favoritism and privilege. The story of Joseph's coat is one of the best-known Bible narratives in popular culture.

verses.text: His brothers saw that their father loved him more than all his brothers, and they hated him, and couldn’t speak peaceably to him.
verses.explainer: His brothers see that their father loves Joseph more than them. They hate him and cannot speak peaceably to him. The dysfunction of Jacob's family -- rooted in his own experience of parental favoritism -- repeats in the next generation.

verses.text: Joseph dreamed a dream, and he told it to his brothers, and they hated him all the more.
verses.explainer: Joseph has a dream and tells his brothers. Their hatred increases. He seems oblivious to how the dream will be received.

verses.text: He said to them, “Please hear this dream which I have dreamed:
verses.explainer: Joseph says: 'Hear this dream I have dreamed.'

verses.text: for behold, we were binding sheaves in the field, and behold, my sheaf arose and also stood upright; and behold, your sheaves came around, and bowed down to my sheaf.”
verses.explainer: In the dream, they are binding sheaves in the field. Joseph's sheaf rises and stands upright while their sheaves gather around and bow to his. The meaning is unmistakable.

verses.text: His brothers said to him, “Will you indeed reign over us? Or will you indeed have dominion over us?” They hated him all the more for his dreams and for his words.
verses.explainer: The brothers say: 'Are you indeed to reign over us?' They hate him even more for his dreams and his words.

verses.text: He dreamed yet another dream, and told it to his brothers, and said, “Behold, I have dreamed yet another dream: and behold, the sun and the moon and eleven stars bowed down to me.”
verses.explainer: Joseph dreams again: the sun, moon, and eleven stars are bowing to him. He tells his brothers and his father.

verses.text: He told it to his father and to his brothers. His father rebuked him, and said to him, “What is this dream that you have dreamed? Will I and your mother and your brothers indeed come to bow ourselves down to you to the earth?”
verses.explainer: Jacob rebukes him: 'Shall I and your mother and your brothers indeed come to bow before you?' Even Jacob is startled. But the narrator adds that Jacob 'kept the matter in mind.'

verses.text: His brothers envied him, but his father kept this saying in mind.
verses.explainer: His brothers are jealous, but his father keeps the saying in mind. Jacob suspects the dreams may be from God.

verses.text: His brothers went to feed their father’s flock in Shechem.
verses.explainer: Joseph's brothers go to pasture their father's flock near Shechem.

verses.text: Israel said to Joseph, “Aren’t your brothers feeding the flock in Shechem? Come, and I will send you to them.” He said to him, “Here I am.”
verses.explainer: Jacob sends Joseph to check on them: 'Go and see if it is well with your brothers and the flock.' Joseph answers: 'Here I am.' The same hinneni of Abraham.

verses.text: He said to him, “Go now, see whether it is well with your brothers, and well with the flock; and bring me word again.” So he sent him out of the valley of Hebron, and he came to Shechem.
verses.explainer: Jacob sends him from the Valley of Hebron. Joseph arrives at Shechem.

verses.text: A certain man found him, and behold, he was wandering in the field. The man asked him, “What are you looking for?”
verses.explainer: A man finds Joseph wandering in the field and asks what he is looking for.

verses.text: He said, “I am looking for my brothers. Tell me, please, where they are feeding the flock.”
verses.explainer: Joseph says: 'I am looking for my brothers.' A statement that will take on deeper meaning as the story unfolds.

verses.text:
The man said, “They have left here, for I heard them say, ‘Let us go to Dothan.’”
Joseph went after his brothers, and found them in Dothan.
verses.explainer: The man says they have moved to Dothan. Joseph follows.

verses.text: They saw him afar off, and before he came near to them, they conspired against him to kill him.
verses.explainer: They see Joseph coming from a distance. Before he reaches them, they conspire to kill him.

verses.text: They said to one another, “Behold, this dreamer comes.
verses.explainer: They say to one another: 'Here comes this dreamer.'

verses.text: Come now therefore, and let’s kill him, and cast him into one of the pits, and we will say, ‘An evil animal has devoured him.’ We will see what will become of his dreams.”
verses.explainer: They plan to kill him, throw him in a pit, and say a wild animal devoured him. 'Then we will see what becomes of his dreams!'

verses.text: Reuben heard it, and delivered him out of their hand, and said, “Let’s not take his life.”
verses.explainer: Reuben hears and delivers Joseph from their hands: 'Let us not take his life.' As the firstborn, he tries to protect Joseph.

verses.text: Reuben said to them, “Shed no blood. Throw him into this pit that is in the wilderness, but lay no hand on him”—that he might deliver him out of their hand, to restore him to his father.
verses.explainer: Reuben says: 'Shed no blood. Throw him into this pit in the wilderness, but do not lay a hand on him.' He intends to rescue Joseph later and restore him to his father.

verses.text: When Joseph came to his brothers, they stripped Joseph of his coat, the coat of many colors that was on him;
verses.explainer: When Joseph arrives, they strip him of his coat -- the hated symbol of favoritism.

verses.text: and they took him, and threw him into the pit. The pit was empty. There was no water in it.
verses.explainer: They throw him into the pit. The pit is empty, with no water. Joseph is alive but helpless.

verses.text: They sat down to eat bread, and they lifted up their eyes and looked, and saw a caravan of Ishmaelites was coming from Gilead, with their camels bearing spices and balm and myrrh, going to carry it down to Egypt.
verses.explainer: They sit down to eat a meal while their brother is in the pit. The casual cruelty is chilling. They see a caravan of Ishmaelites coming from Gilead, carrying spices, balm, and myrrh to Egypt.

verses.text: Judah said to his brothers, “What profit is it if we kill our brother and conceal his blood?
verses.explainer: Judah says: 'What profit is it if we kill our brother and conceal his blood?' He proposes selling Joseph instead. The reasoning is mercenary, not merciful.

verses.text: Come, and let’s sell him to the Ishmaelites, and not let our hand be on him; for he is our brother, our flesh.” His brothers listened to him.
verses.explainer: Judah says: 'Let us sell him to the Ishmaelites and not lay our hands on him, for he is our brother.' The proposal sounds reasonable but is still monstrous.

verses.text: Midianites who were merchants passed by, and they drew and lifted up Joseph out of the pit, and sold Joseph to the Ishmaelites for twenty pieces of silver. They brought Joseph into Egypt.
verses.explainer: Midianite traders pass by. They pull Joseph out of the pit and sell him to the Ishmaelites for twenty shekels of silver. He is carried to Egypt. The price of a slave.

verses.text: Reuben returned to the pit; and saw that Joseph wasn’t in the pit; and he tore his clothes.
verses.explainer: Reuben returns to the pit and finds Joseph gone. He tears his clothes in grief.

verses.text: He returned to his brothers, and said, “The child is no more; and I, where will I go?”
verses.explainer: Reuben returns to his brothers: 'The boy is gone! And I, where shall I go?' His plan to rescue Joseph has failed.

verses.text: They took Joseph’s coat, and killed a male goat, and dipped the coat in the blood.
verses.explainer: They take Joseph's coat, slaughter a goat, and dip the coat in the blood. Jacob, who used goat skins to deceive his father (27:16), is now deceived by a goat's blood.

verses.text: They took the coat of many colors, and they brought it to their father, and said, “We have found this. Examine it, now, whether it is your son’s coat or not.”
verses.explainer: They bring the bloody coat to Jacob: 'We found this. Please identify whether it is your son's coat.'

verses.text: He recognized it, and said, “It is my son’s coat. An evil animal has devoured him. Joseph is without doubt torn in pieces.”
verses.explainer: Jacob identifies it: 'It is my son's coat. A fierce animal has devoured him. Joseph is surely torn to pieces.'

verses.text: Jacob tore his clothes, and put sackcloth on his waist, and mourned for his son many days.
verses.explainer: Jacob tears his garments, puts sackcloth on his loins, and mourns for his son many days. The grief is genuine and deep.

verses.text: All his sons and all his daughters rose up to comfort him, but he refused to be comforted. He said, “For I will go down to Sheol to my son mourning.” His father wept for him.
verses.explainer: All his sons and daughters try to comfort him, but he refuses to be comforted: 'I shall go down to Sheol to my son, mourning.' Jacob will grieve for Joseph for over twenty years.

verses.text: The Midianites sold him into Egypt to Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh’s, the captain of the guard.
verses.explainer: Meanwhile, the Midianites sell Joseph in Egypt to Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh, the captain of the guard. Joseph's descent is complete: from favored son to foreign slave. But God is not finished.

explainer.summary: The Joseph narrative pauses for a seemingly unrelated story about Judah. Judah marries a Canaanite woman and has three sons. His firstborn Er marries Tamar, but God kills Er for his wickedness. The second son Onan refuses to fulfill his duty to give Tamar a child and also dies. Judah withholds his third son from Tamar. Tamar disguises herself as a prostitute, sleeps with Judah, and becomes pregnant with twins. When Judah discovers the truth, he admits: 'She is more righteous than I.' The line of Judah -- the line of David and Jesus -- comes through this scandalous encounter.
themes: Judah's moral decline away from his brothers
explainer.contextInBook: This interruption in the Joseph story is deliberately placed. While Joseph is resisting sexual temptation in Egypt (chapter 39), Judah is falling into it in Canaan. The contrast is pointed. But Tamar's story also advances the genealogy of the Messiah.

verses.text: At that time, Judah went down from his brothers, and visited a certain Adullamite, whose name was Hirah.
verses.explainer: At that time, Judah leaves his brothers and settles near a man named Hirah the Adullamite. Judah separates from the family -- a move that leads to assimilation.

verses.text: Judah saw there a certain Canaanite man named Shua’s daughter. He took her, and went in to her.
verses.explainer: Judah sees the daughter of a Canaanite named Shua and marries her. He does exactly what Abraham and Isaac tried to prevent: marrying into the local population.

verses.text: She conceived, and bore a son; and he named him Er.
verses.explainer: She bears Er. Judah names him.

verses.text: She conceived again, and bore a son; and she named him Onan.
verses.explainer: She bears Onan.

verses.text: She yet again bore a son, and named him Shelah: and he was at Chezib, when she bore him.
verses.explainer: She bears Shelah. Judah is at Chezib when Shelah is born.

verses.text: Judah took a wife for Er, his firstborn, and her name was Tamar.
verses.explainer: Judah takes a wife named Tamar for Er, his firstborn.

verses.text: Er, Judah’s firstborn, was wicked in Yahweh’s sight. Yahweh killed him.
verses.explainer: Er is wicked in the sight of the LORD, and the LORD puts him to death. The text does not specify the wickedness.

verses.text: Judah said to Onan, “Go in to your brother’s wife, and perform the duty of a husband’s brother to her, and raise up offspring for your brother.”
verses.explainer: Judah tells Onan to go in to his brother's wife and perform the duty of a brother-in-law (levirate marriage), raising up offspring for his dead brother.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Levirate marriage', 'explanation': "From the Latin levir (brother-in-law). When a man died without children, his brother was obligated to marry the widow and produce a son who would carry on the dead man's name and inherit his property. This preserved the family line and protected the widow. It was later codified in Deuteronomy 25:5-10."}

verses.text: Onan knew that the offspring wouldn’t be his; and when he went in to his brother’s wife, he spilled it on the ground, lest he should give offspring to his brother.
verses.explainer: Onan knows the offspring would not be his. When he sleeps with Tamar, he spills his seed on the ground to avoid giving his brother a child. The act is not about contraception but about refusing a sacred obligation -- he wants his brother's inheritance for himself.

verses.text: The thing which he did was evil in Yahweh’s sight, and he killed him also.
verses.explainer: What Onan does displeases the LORD, and God puts him to death too.

verses.text: Then Judah said to Tamar, his daughter-in-law, “Remain a widow in your father’s house, until Shelah, my son, is grown up”; for he said, “Lest he also die, like his brothers.” Tamar went and lived in her father’s house.
verses.explainer: Judah tells Tamar to remain a widow in her father's house until Shelah grows up. He fears Shelah will die too. In reality, Judah is withholding his son, leaving Tamar in permanent limbo.

verses.text: After many days, Shua’s daughter, the wife of Judah, died. Judah was comforted, and went up to his sheep shearers to Timnah, he and his friend Hirah, the Adullamite.
verses.explainer: In time, Judah's wife dies. After mourning, Judah goes to his sheepshearers at Timnah with Hirah.

verses.text: Tamar was told, “Behold, your father-in-law is going up to Timnah to shear his sheep.”
verses.explainer: Tamar is told: 'Your father-in-law is going to Timnah to shear sheep.'

verses.text: She took off her the garments of her widowhood, and covered herself with her veil, and wrapped herself, and sat in the gate of Enaim, which is by the way to Timnah; for she saw that Shelah was grown up, and she wasn’t given to him as a wife.
verses.explainer: Tamar takes off her widow's garments, covers herself with a veil, and sits at the entrance to Enaim on the road to Timnah. She has realized Judah will never give her Shelah. She takes matters into her own hands.

verses.text: When Judah saw her, he thought that she was a prostitute, for she had covered her face.
verses.explainer: Judah sees her and thinks she is a prostitute because her face is covered.

verses.text:
He turned to her by the way, and said, “Please come, let me come in to you,” for he didn’t know that she was his daughter-in-law.
She said, “What will you give me, that you may come in to me?”
verses.explainer: He propositions her, not knowing she is his daughter-in-law. She asks: 'What will you give me?'

verses.text:
He said, “I will send you a young goat from the flock.”
She said, “Will you give me a pledge, until you send it?”
verses.explainer: Judah promises a young goat from the flock. She asks for a pledge until it is sent.

verses.text:
He said, “What pledge will I give you?”
She said, “Your signet and your cord, and your staff that is in your hand.”
He gave them to her, and came in to her, and she conceived by him.
verses.explainer: She asks for his signet, cord, and staff -- his personal identification, the ancient equivalent of an ID card and credit card. Judah gives them and sleeps with her. She conceives.

verses.text: She arose, and went away, and put off her veil from her, and put on the garments of her widowhood.
verses.explainer: Tamar goes home, removes the veil, and puts on her widow's garments again.

verses.text: Judah sent the young goat by the hand of his friend, the Adullamite, to receive the pledge from the woman’s hand, but he didn’t find her.
verses.explainer: Judah sends his friend Hirah to retrieve the pledge by delivering the goat. Hirah cannot find the woman.

verses.text:
Then he asked the men of her place, saying, “Where is the prostitute, that was at Enaim by the road?”
They said, “There has been no prostitute here.”
verses.explainer: Hirah asks the locals about the cult prostitute at Enaim. They say there has been no cult prostitute there.

verses.text: He returned to Judah, and said, “I haven’t found her; and also the men of the place said, ‘There has been no prostitute here.’”
verses.explainer: Hirah returns: 'I could not find her, and the men said there was no prostitute there.'

verses.text: Judah said, “Let her keep it, lest we be shamed. Behold, I sent this young goat, and you haven’t found her.”
verses.explainer: Judah says: 'Let her keep the things, lest we be laughed at.' He would rather lose his identity tokens than face public embarrassment.

verses.text:
About three months later, Judah was told, “Tamar, your daughter-in-law, has played the prostitute. Moreover, behold, she is with child by prostitution.”
Judah said, “Bring her out, and let her be burned.”
verses.explainer: About three months later, Judah is told: 'Your daughter-in-law Tamar has been immoral, and she is pregnant.' Judah says: 'Bring her out, and let her be burned.' His moral outrage is hypocritical -- he is the father.

verses.text: When she was brought out, she sent to her father-in-law, saying, “By the man, whose these are, I am with child.” She also said, “Please discern whose are these—the signet, and the cords, and the staff.”
verses.explainer: As she is being brought out, Tamar sends the signet, cord, and staff to Judah: 'The man to whom these belong made me pregnant. Please identify whose these are.' The same words the brothers used when presenting Joseph's coat: 'Please identify.' Judah is forced to recognize himself.

thenVsNow.then: Tamar's revelation is brilliantly strategic. Rather than publicly accusing Judah, she presents the evidence and lets him draw his own conclusion. The approach preserves his dignity while making the truth undeniable.
thenVsNow.now: Tamar appears in the genealogy of Jesus (Matthew 1:3). A Canaanite woman who disguised herself as a prostitute to get justice becomes an ancestor of the Messiah. God's lineage runs through scandal, not respectability.

verses.text:
Judah acknowledged them, and said, “She is more righteous than I, because I didn’t give her to Shelah, my son.”
He knew her again no more.
verses.explainer: Judah recognizes them and says: 'She is more righteous than I, because I did not give her to my son Shelah.' He confesses publicly. This is a turning point for Judah -- the first time he takes responsibility for his actions.

verses.text: In the time of her travail, behold, twins were in her womb.
verses.explainer: When Tamar gives birth, there are twins in her womb.

verses.text: When she travailed, one put out a hand, and the midwife took and tied a scarlet thread on his hand, saying, “This came out first.”
verses.explainer: During delivery, one puts out a hand. The midwife ties a scarlet thread on it, saying: 'This one came out first.'

verses.text: As he drew back his hand, behold, his brother came out, and she said, “Why have you made a breach for yourself?” Therefore his name was called Perez.
verses.explainer: But the hand is drawn back, and his brother comes out first. She says: 'What a breach you have made!' He is named Perez ('breach' or 'breaking out').

verses.text: Afterward his brother came out, that had the scarlet thread on his hand, and his name was called Zerah.
verses.explainer: Then his brother with the scarlet thread comes out. He is named Zerah ('scarlet' or 'dawning'). Perez becomes the ancestor of David and, through David, of Jesus.

explainer.summary: Joseph arrives in Egypt as a slave in the house of Potiphar, captain of Pharaoh's guard. The LORD is with Joseph, and everything he touches prospers. Potiphar puts him in charge of his entire household. But Potiphar's wife repeatedly tries to seduce Joseph. He refuses on moral and theological grounds: 'How can I do this great wickedness and sin against God?' When she grabs his garment and he flees, she accuses him of attempted rape. Potiphar imprisons Joseph. But even in prison, the LORD is with him.
themes: God's presence with Joseph in adversity
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter contrasts directly with chapter 38: Judah falls into sexual sin; Joseph resists it. The garment motif continues: Joseph's coat was stripped (37:23); now his garment is again taken and used against him.

verses.text: Joseph was brought down to Egypt. Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh’s, the captain of the guard, an Egyptian, bought him from the hand of the Ishmaelites that had brought him down there.
verses.explainer: Joseph is brought down to Egypt. Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh and captain of the guard, buys him from the Ishmaelites.

verses.text: Yahweh was with Joseph, and he was a prosperous man. He was in the house of his master the Egyptian.
verses.explainer: The LORD is with Joseph, and he becomes a successful man. He is in the house of his Egyptian master. The key phrase of the chapter: 'The LORD was with Joseph.' It appears four times.

verses.text: His master saw that Yahweh was with him, and that Yahweh made all that he did prosper in his hand.
verses.explainer: Potiphar sees that the LORD is with Joseph and that the LORD causes everything he does to prosper. Even a pagan master recognizes divine favor.

verses.text: Joseph found favor in his sight. He ministered to him, and he made him overseer over his house, and all that he had he put into his hand.
verses.explainer: Joseph finds favor in Potiphar's sight and serves him. Potiphar makes him overseer of his house and puts everything he owns in Joseph's care.

verses.text: From the time that he made him overseer in his house, and over all that he had, Yahweh blessed the Egyptian’s house for Joseph’s sake. Yahweh’s blessing was on all that he had, in the house and in the field.
verses.explainer: From the time Potiphar put Joseph in charge, the LORD blesses the Egyptian's household for Joseph's sake. The blessing is on everything -- in house and field. God's blessing flows through Joseph to benefit even non-Israelites.

thenVsNow.then: The idea that God would bless a pagan household because of a Hebrew slave was remarkable. It fulfills the Abrahamic promise: 'In you all families of the earth shall be blessed.'
thenVsNow.now: The principle that God's people can be a source of blessing to their surrounding communities -- even when those communities do not share their faith -- remains a powerful motivation for civic engagement.

verses.text:
He left all that he had in Joseph’s hand. He didn’t concern himself with anything, except for the food which he ate.
Joseph was well-built and handsome.
verses.explainer: Potiphar leaves everything in Joseph's hands and concerns himself with nothing except the food he eats. Joseph is given total authority. The narrator adds: Joseph is handsome in form and appearance.

verses.text: After these things, his master’s wife set her eyes on Joseph; and she said, “Lie with me.”
verses.explainer: After a time, Potiphar's wife looks at Joseph and says: 'Lie with me.' The proposition is direct and blunt.

verses.text: But he refused, and said to his master’s wife, “Behold, my master doesn’t know what is with me in the house, and he has put all that he has into my hand.
verses.explainer: Joseph refuses: 'My master has withheld nothing from me except you, because you are his wife.' He appeals to loyalty and honor.

verses.text: No one is greater in this house than I am, and he has not kept back anything from me but you, because you are his wife. How then can I do this great wickedness, and sin against God?”
verses.explainer: Joseph says: 'How then can I do this great wickedness and sin against God?' His ultimate reason is theological: sexual immorality is sin against God, not just against Potiphar. This is moral reasoning at its highest.

verses.text: As she spoke to Joseph day by day, he didn’t listen to her, to lie by her, or to be with her.
verses.explainer: She speaks to Joseph day after day. He refuses to lie with her or even be near her. The temptation is not a single moment but a sustained campaign.

verses.text: About this time, he went into the house to do his work, and there were none of the men of the house inside.
verses.explainer: One day Joseph goes into the house to do his work, and no one else is in the house.

verses.text:
She caught him by his garment, saying, “Lie with me!”
He left his garment in her hand, and ran outside.
verses.explainer: She grabs him by his garment: 'Lie with me!' He leaves his garment in her hand and flees outside. For the third time in Genesis, a garment is stripped from Joseph and used against him.

verses.text: When she saw that he had left his garment in her hand, and had run outside,
verses.explainer: When she sees he has fled and left his garment, she calls the household servants.

verses.text: she called to the men of her house, and spoke to them, saying, “Behold, he has brought a Hebrew in to us to mock us. He came in to me to lie with me, and I cried with a loud voice.
verses.explainer: She tells them: 'See, he has brought a Hebrew to laugh at us. He came in to lie with me, and I cried out.' She blames Potiphar for bringing a Hebrew into the house.

verses.text: When he heard that I lifted up my voice and cried, he left his garment by me, and ran outside.”
verses.explainer: She says: 'When he heard me cry out, he left his garment and fled.'

verses.text: She laid up his garment by her, until his master came home.
verses.explainer: She keeps the garment beside her until Potiphar comes home.

verses.text: She spoke to him according to these words, saying, “The Hebrew servant, whom you have brought to us, came in to me to mock me,
verses.explainer: She repeats the story to Potiphar: 'The Hebrew servant you brought to us came in to mock me.'

verses.text: and as I lifted up my voice and cried, he left his garment by me, and ran outside.”
verses.explainer: She says: 'When I raised my voice, he left his garment and fled.'

verses.text: When his master heard the words of his wife, which she spoke to him, saying, “This is what your servant did to me,” his wrath was kindled.
verses.explainer: When Potiphar hears his wife's words, his anger is kindled. The text does not say his anger is directed at Joseph -- some scholars suggest Potiphar may have doubted his wife's story but had to act to save face.

verses.text: Joseph’s master took him, and put him into the prison, the place where the king’s prisoners were bound, and he was there in custody.
verses.explainer: Potiphar puts Joseph in prison -- the prison where the king's prisoners are confined. This is not a common jail but a royal prison, which may suggest Potiphar went easier on Joseph than a full accusation of attempted rape would warrant.

verses.text: But Yahweh was with Joseph, and showed kindness to him, and gave him favor in the sight of the keeper of the prison.
verses.explainer: But the LORD is with Joseph and shows him steadfast love and gives him favor in the sight of the keeper of the prison. The same pattern repeats: adversity, God's presence, favor.

verses.text: The keeper of the prison committed to Joseph’s hand all the prisoners who were in the prison. Whatever they did there, he was responsible for it.
verses.explainer: The prison keeper puts Joseph in charge of all the prisoners. Whatever is done there, Joseph is responsible.

verses.text: The keeper of the prison didn’t look after anything that was under his hand, because Yahweh was with him; and that which he did, Yahweh made it prosper.
verses.explainer: The keeper of the prison pays no attention to anything under Joseph's care because the LORD is with him. Whatever Joseph does, the LORD makes it prosper. The chapter ends exactly where it began: the LORD is with Joseph.

explainer.summary: Two of Pharaoh's officials -- the chief cupbearer and the chief baker -- are imprisoned with Joseph. Each has a dream on the same night. Joseph notices their distress and offers to interpret, saying interpretations belong to God. The cupbearer's dream means he will be restored to his position in three days; the baker's dream means he will be executed in three days. Both interpretations come true exactly. Joseph asks the cupbearer to mention him to Pharaoh, but the cupbearer forgets him.
themes: Joseph as interpreter through God's power
The contrast between restoration and judgment
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter sets up Joseph's eventual rise to power. His ability to interpret dreams will eventually bring him before Pharaoh, but not yet. Two more years of waiting remain.

verses.text: After these things, the butler of the king of Egypt and his baker offended their lord, the king of Egypt.
verses.explainer: Some time later, the cupbearer and baker of the king of Egypt offend their master. These are high-ranking officials -- the cupbearer tasted the king's wine (a trusted role because of the threat of poisoning), and the baker prepared his food.

verses.text: Pharaoh was angry with his two officers, the chief cup bearer and the chief baker.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh is angry with his two officers.

verses.text: He put them in custody in the house of the captain of the guard, into the prison, the place where Joseph was bound.
verses.explainer: He puts them in custody in the house of the captain of the guard, in the prison where Joseph is confined. Providence arranges the meeting.

verses.text: The captain of the guard assigned them to Joseph, and he took care of them. They stayed in prison many days.
verses.explainer: The captain of the guard assigns Joseph to attend them. They are in custody for some time.

verses.text: They both dreamed a dream, each man his dream, in one night, each man according to the interpretation of his dream, the cup bearer and the baker of the king of Egypt, who were bound in the prison.
verses.explainer: One night, both the cupbearer and baker dream -- each his own dream with its own interpretation. Dreams in pairs suggest divine origin.

verses.text: Joseph came in to them in the morning, and saw them, and saw that they were sad.
verses.explainer: Joseph comes to them in the morning and sees they are troubled.

verses.text: He asked Pharaoh’s officers who were with him in custody in his master’s house, saying, “Why do you look so sad today?”
verses.explainer: He asks: 'Why do you look so sad today?' Even in prison, Joseph notices others' distress and responds with compassion.

verses.text:
They said to him, “We have dreamed a dream, and there is no one who can interpret it.”
Joseph said to them, “Don’t interpretations belong to God? Please tell it to me.”
verses.explainer: They say: 'We have had dreams, and there is no one to interpret them.' Joseph says: 'Do not interpretations belong to God? Please tell them to me.' Joseph claims no personal ability -- God is the interpreter. He is merely the vessel.

verses.text: The chief cup bearer told his dream to Joseph, and said to him, “In my dream, behold, a vine was in front of me,
verses.explainer: The chief cupbearer tells his dream: a vine before him with three branches.

verses.text: and in the vine were three branches. It was as though it budded, it blossomed, and its clusters produced ripe grapes.
verses.explainer: The vine buds, blossoms, and produces clusters of ripe grapes.

verses.text: Pharaoh’s cup was in my hand; and I took the grapes, and pressed them into Pharaoh’s cup, and I gave the cup into Pharaoh’s hand.”
verses.explainer: Pharaoh's cup is in his hand. He takes the grapes, squeezes them into Pharaoh's cup, and places the cup in Pharaoh's hand. The dream replays his job.

verses.text: Joseph said to him, “This is its interpretation: the three branches are three days.
verses.explainer: Joseph interprets: 'The three branches are three days.'

verses.text: Within three more days, Pharaoh will lift up your head, and restore you to your office. You will give Pharaoh’s cup into his hand, the way you did when you were his cup bearer.
verses.explainer: In three days, Pharaoh will lift up the cupbearer's head and restore him to his position. He will place Pharaoh's cup in his hand as before. Good news.

verses.text: But remember me when it will be well with you, and please show kindness to me, and make mention of me to Pharaoh, and bring me out of this house.
verses.explainer: Joseph asks: 'When it goes well with you, remember me. Mention me to Pharaoh and get me out of this prison.' A reasonable request from an unjustly imprisoned man.

verses.text: For indeed, I was stolen away out of the land of the Hebrews, and here also I have done nothing that they should put me into the dungeon.”
verses.explainer: Joseph explains: 'I was stolen out of the land of the Hebrews, and here also I have done nothing that they should put me into the pit.' He uses the word 'pit' -- the same word used for the cistern his brothers threw him into.

verses.text: When the chief baker saw that the interpretation was good, he said to Joseph, “I also was in my dream, and behold, three baskets of white bread were on my head.
verses.explainer: The chief baker sees the interpretation is favorable and tells his dream: three cake baskets on his head.

verses.text: In the uppermost basket there were all kinds of baked food for Pharaoh, and the birds ate them out of the basket on my head.”
verses.explainer: The top basket has all kinds of baked food for Pharaoh, but birds are eating from it.

verses.text: Joseph answered, “This is its interpretation. The three baskets are three days.
verses.explainer: Joseph interprets: 'The three baskets are three days.'

verses.text: Within three more days, Pharaoh will lift up your head from off you, and will hang you on a tree; and the birds will eat your flesh from off you.”
verses.explainer: In three days, Pharaoh will lift up the baker's head -- from him. He will hang the baker on a tree, and birds will eat his flesh. 'Lift up your head' has a double meaning: restoration for the cupbearer, execution for the baker.

verses.text: On the third day, which was Pharaoh’s birthday, he made a feast for all his servants, and he lifted up the head of the chief cup bearer and the head of the chief baker among his servants.
verses.explainer: On the third day -- Pharaoh's birthday -- he gives a feast for all his servants. He lifts up the heads of the cupbearer and baker.

verses.text: He restored the chief cup bearer to his position again, and he gave the cup into Pharaoh’s hand;
verses.explainer: He restores the cupbearer to his position.

verses.text: but he hanged the chief baker, as Joseph had interpreted to them.
verses.explainer: He hangs the baker, just as Joseph had interpreted.

verses.text: Yet the chief cup bearer didn’t remember Joseph, but forgot him.
verses.explainer: Yet the chief cupbearer does not remember Joseph but forgets him. The chapter ends on a note of abandonment. Joseph, who was faithful and accurate, is left in prison by the man who benefited from his gift. Two more years will pass (41:1).

explainer.summary: Two years after the cupbearer's release, Pharaoh has two dreams that no one can interpret. The cupbearer finally remembers Joseph. Pharaoh summons Joseph from prison, and Joseph interprets the dreams: seven years of abundance followed by seven years of devastating famine. He advises Pharaoh to appoint someone wise to store grain during the good years. Pharaoh appoints Joseph himself, making the Hebrew slave the second most powerful man in Egypt. Joseph marries an Egyptian woman and has two sons: Manasseh and Ephraim.
explainer.contextInBook: This is the turning point of the Joseph story. Everything that went wrong -- the pit, the slavery, the false accusation, the prison -- turns out to have been preparation for this moment. Joseph is positioned to save not only Egypt but his own family.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Joseph's Egyptian name, given by Pharaoh
wordStudies.meaning: Joseph's Egyptian name, given by Pharaoh. Its meaning is debated: possibly 'God speaks and lives' or 'the one who furnishes the nourishment of life.' It marks Joseph's official integration into Egyptian court culture.

verses.text: At the end of two full years, Pharaoh dreamed: and behold, he stood by the river.
verses.explainer: After two whole years, Pharaoh dreams. Two years of waiting after the cupbearer was restored. God's timing is precise but not fast.

verses.text: Behold, there came up out of the river seven cattle, sleek and fat, and they fed in the marsh grass.
verses.explainer: In the dream, seven healthy, fat cows come up from the Nile and graze in the reed grass. The Nile was the source of Egypt's life.

verses.text: Behold, seven other cattle came up after them out of the river, ugly and thin, and stood by the other cattle on the brink of the river.
verses.explainer: Seven other cows, ugly and thin, come up after them and stand beside the fat cows on the bank of the Nile.

verses.text: The ugly and thin cattle ate up the seven sleek and fat cattle. So Pharaoh awoke.
verses.explainer: The ugly, thin cows eat the seven fat, healthy cows. Pharaoh wakes up.

verses.text: He slept and dreamed a second time: and behold, seven heads of grain came up on one stalk, healthy and good.
verses.explainer: He falls asleep again and dreams a second time: seven ears of grain, plump and good, grow on one stalk.

verses.text: Behold, seven heads of grain, thin and blasted with the east wind, sprung up after them.
verses.explainer: Seven thin ears, blighted by the east wind, sprout after them.

verses.text: The thin heads of grain swallowed up the seven healthy and full ears. Pharaoh awoke, and behold, it was a dream.
verses.explainer: The thin ears swallow the seven plump ears. Pharaoh wakes up. The doubled dream indicates divine urgency.

verses.text: In the morning, his spirit was troubled, and he sent and called for all of Egypt’s magicians and wise men. Pharaoh told them his dreams, but there was no one who could interpret them to Pharaoh.
verses.explainer: In the morning, Pharaoh's spirit is troubled. He summons all the magicians and wise men of Egypt and tells them the dreams. None can interpret them.

verses.text: Then the chief cup bearer spoke to Pharaoh, saying, “I remember my faults today.
verses.explainer: The chief cupbearer says: 'I remember my offenses today.' Two years too late, he remembers Joseph.

verses.text: Pharaoh was angry with his servants, and put me in custody in the house of the captain of the guard, with the chief baker.
verses.explainer: He recounts being imprisoned with the baker in the captain of the guard's house.

verses.text: We dreamed a dream in one night, he and I. We dreamed each man according to the interpretation of his dream.
verses.explainer: He tells of the dreams he and the baker had in one night, each with its own meaning.

verses.text: There was with us there a young man, a Hebrew, servant to the captain of the guard, and we told him, and he interpreted to us our dreams. To each man according to his dream he interpreted.
verses.explainer: A young Hebrew was with them, a servant of the captain of the guard. They told him their dreams, and he interpreted each correctly.

verses.text: As he interpreted to us, so it was. He restored me to my office, and he hanged him.”
verses.explainer: Everything happened as he interpreted: the cupbearer was restored, the baker was hanged.

verses.text: Then Pharaoh sent and called Joseph, and they brought him hastily out of the dungeon. He shaved himself, changed his clothing, and came in to Pharaoh.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh sends for Joseph. They bring him quickly from the dungeon. He shaves, changes clothes, and comes before Pharaoh. The transition from dungeon to throne room is abrupt and dramatic.

verses.text: Pharaoh said to Joseph, “I have dreamed a dream, and there is no one who can interpret it. I have heard it said of you, that when you hear a dream you can interpret it.”
verses.explainer: Pharaoh says: 'I have had a dream, and no one can interpret it. I have heard you can interpret dreams.' Joseph says: 'It is not in me; God will give Pharaoh a favorable answer.' Joseph deflects credit immediately.

verses.text: Joseph answered Pharaoh, saying, “It isn’t in me. God will give Pharaoh an answer of peace.”
verses.explainer: Joseph's humility is strategic and genuine: he will not claim a power that belongs to God.

verses.text: Pharaoh spoke to Joseph, “In my dream, behold, I stood on the brink of the river:
verses.explainer: Pharaoh recounts the first dream: the cows by the Nile.

verses.text: and behold, there came up out of the river seven cattle, fat and sleek. They fed in the marsh grass,
verses.explainer: Seven fat, attractive cows come up and feed in the reed grass.

verses.text: and behold, seven other cattle came up after them, poor and very ugly and thin, such as I never saw in all the land of Egypt for ugliness.
verses.explainer: Seven other cows, poor, ugly, and thin -- worse than any he has seen in all Egypt -- come up after them.

verses.text: The thin and ugly cattle ate up the first seven fat cattle,
verses.explainer: The thin cows eat the fat cows.

verses.text: and when they had eaten them up, it couldn’t be known that they had eaten them, but they were still ugly, as at the beginning. So I awoke.
verses.explainer: After eating, they look just as bad as before. No one would know they had eaten them. Pharaoh wakes up.

verses.text: I saw in my dream, and behold, seven heads of grain came up on one stalk, full and good:
verses.explainer: He tells the second dream: seven full, good ears on one stalk.

verses.text: and behold, seven heads of grain, withered, thin, and blasted with the east wind, sprung up after them.
verses.explainer: Seven withered, thin, blighted ears sprout after them.

verses.text: The thin heads of grain swallowed up the seven good heads of grain. I told it to the magicians, but there was no one who could explain it to me.”
verses.explainer: The thin ears swallow the good ears. Pharaoh has told the magicians, but none can explain it.

verses.text: Joseph said to Pharaoh, “The dream of Pharaoh is one. What God is about to do he has declared to Pharaoh.
verses.explainer: Joseph says: 'The dreams of Pharaoh are one. God has revealed to Pharaoh what he is about to do.' Joseph makes God the subject, not himself.

verses.text: The seven good cattle are seven years; and the seven good heads of grain are seven years. The dream is one.
verses.explainer: The seven good cows are seven years, and the seven good ears are seven years. The dreams are the same.

verses.text: The seven thin and ugly cattle that came up after them are seven years, and also the seven empty heads of grain blasted with the east wind; they will be seven years of famine.
verses.explainer: The seven thin cows and seven thin ears are seven years of famine.

verses.text: That is the thing which I spoke to Pharaoh. What God is about to do he has shown to Pharaoh.
verses.explainer: It is as Joseph told Pharaoh: God has shown Pharaoh what he is about to do.

verses.text: Behold, there come seven years of great plenty throughout all the land of Egypt.
verses.explainer: Seven years of great abundance are coming throughout all Egypt.

verses.text: There will arise after them seven years of famine, and all the plenty will be forgotten in the land of Egypt. The famine will consume the land,
verses.explainer: After them, seven years of famine will arise, and all the abundance will be forgotten. The famine will devastate the land.

verses.text: and the plenty will not be known in the land by reason of that famine which follows; for it will be very grievous.
verses.explainer: The abundance will not be remembered because the famine will be so severe.

verses.text: The dream was doubled to Pharaoh, because the thing is established by God, and God will shortly bring it to pass.
verses.explainer: The dream was repeated twice because the matter is fixed by God, and God will shortly bring it about. Doubled dreams indicate certainty and urgency.

verses.text: “Now therefore let Pharaoh look for a discreet and wise man, and set him over the land of Egypt.
verses.explainer: Joseph offers unsolicited advice: 'Let Pharaoh select a discerning and wise man and set him over the land of Egypt.' He transitions from interpreter to consultant.

verses.text: Let Pharaoh do this, and let him appoint overseers over the land, and take up the fifth part of the land of Egypt’s produce in the seven plenteous years.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh should appoint overseers to take a fifth of the harvest during the seven good years.

verses.text: Let them gather all the food of these good years that come, and lay up grain under the hand of Pharaoh for food in the cities, and let them keep it.
verses.explainer: Store the grain under Pharaoh's authority, keeping it in the cities for food.

verses.text: The food will be for a store to the land against the seven years of famine, which will be in the land of Egypt; that the land not perish through the famine.”
verses.explainer: The stored food will be a reserve for the seven years of famine, so the land will not perish. Joseph has interpreted the problem and proposed the solution.

verses.text: The thing was good in the eyes of Pharaoh, and in the eyes of all his servants.
verses.explainer: The proposal pleases Pharaoh and all his servants.

verses.text: Pharaoh said to his servants, “Can we find such a one as this, a man in whom is the Spirit of God?”
verses.explainer: Pharaoh asks: 'Can we find a man like this, in whom is the Spirit of God?' He recognizes the divine dimension.

verses.text: Pharaoh said to Joseph, “Because God has shown you all of this, there is no one so discreet and wise as you.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh tells Joseph: 'Since God has shown you all this, there is none so discerning and wise as you.'

verses.text: You shall be over my house, and according to your word will all my people be ruled. Only in the throne I will be greater than you.”
verses.explainer: Pharaoh says: 'You shall be over my house, and all my people shall order themselves as you command. Only in regard to the throne will I be greater than you.' From prisoner to prime minister in a single conversation.

thenVsNow.then: The rise of a foreigner to high position in Egypt has parallels in Egyptian literature and history. However, a Hebrew slave becoming second in command was extraordinary and demonstrated God's sovereign ability to work within foreign power structures.
thenVsNow.now: Joseph's story has inspired centuries of reflection on divine providence. What humans intended for evil, God intended for good. The worst chapters of Joseph's life were preparation for the best.

verses.text: Pharaoh said to Joseph, “Behold, I have set you over all the land of Egypt.”
verses.explainer: Pharaoh says: 'I have set you over all the land of Egypt.'

verses.text: Pharaoh took off his signet ring from his hand, and put it on Joseph’s hand, and arrayed him in robes of fine linen, and put a gold chain about his neck,
verses.explainer: Pharaoh gives Joseph his signet ring, dresses him in fine linen, and puts a gold chain around his neck. Each item is a symbol of authority and status.

verses.text: and he made him to ride in the second chariot which he had. They cried before him, “Bow the knee!” He set him over all the land of Egypt.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh has Joseph ride in his second chariot, with runners crying 'Bow the knee!' before him. Joseph is publicly installed.

verses.text: Pharaoh said to Joseph, “I am Pharaoh, and without you shall no man lift up his hand or his foot in all the land of Egypt.”
verses.explainer: Pharaoh says: 'I am Pharaoh, but without your consent no one shall lift hand or foot in all the land of Egypt.' Total administrative authority.

verses.text: Pharaoh called Joseph’s name Zaphenath-Paneah; and he gave him Asenath, the daughter of Potiphera priest of On as a wife. Joseph went out over the land of Egypt.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh gives Joseph the Egyptian name Zaphenath-paneah and gives him Asenath, daughter of Potiphera priest of On, as his wife. Joseph is thoroughly integrated into Egyptian society.

verses.text: Joseph was thirty years old when he stood before Pharaoh king of Egypt. Joseph went out from the presence of Pharaoh, and went throughout all the land of Egypt.
verses.explainer: Joseph is thirty years old when he enters Pharaoh's service. He was sold at seventeen; thirteen years have passed in slavery and prison.

verses.text: In the seven plenteous years the earth produced abundantly.
verses.explainer: During the seven plentiful years, the earth produces abundantly.

verses.text: He gathered up all the food of the seven years which were in the land of Egypt, and laid up the food in the cities: the food of the field, which was around every city, he laid up in the same.
verses.explainer: Joseph gathers all the food of the seven years and stores it in the cities -- food from the fields around each city.

verses.text: Joseph laid up grain as the sand of the sea, very much, until he stopped counting, for it was without number.
verses.explainer: Joseph stores grain in such abundance that he stops measuring -- it is beyond counting, like the sand of the sea.

verses.text: To Joseph were born two sons before the year of famine came, whom Asenath, the daughter of Potiphera priest of On, bore to him.
verses.explainer: Before the famine comes, Joseph has two sons by Asenath.

verses.text: Joseph called the name of the firstborn Manasseh, “For”, he said, “God has made me forget all my toil, and all my father’s house.”
verses.explainer: He names the firstborn Manasseh ('making to forget'): 'God has made me forget all my hardship and all my father's house.' The name reveals both healing and loss.

verses.text: The name of the second, he called Ephraim: “For God has made me fruitful in the land of my affliction.”
verses.explainer: He names the second Ephraim ('fruitful'): 'God has made me fruitful in the land of my affliction.' Egypt is still the land of affliction, even though he rules it.

verses.text: The seven years of plenty, that were in the land of Egypt, came to an end.
verses.explainer: The seven years of abundance come to an end.

verses.text: The seven years of famine began to come, just as Joseph had said. There was famine in all lands, but in all the land of Egypt there was bread.
verses.explainer: The seven years of famine begin, as Joseph predicted. Famine affects all lands, but there is bread in Egypt.

verses.text: When all the land of Egypt was famished, the people cried to Pharaoh for bread, and Pharaoh said to all the Egyptians, “Go to Joseph. What he says to you, do.”
verses.explainer: When all Egypt is hungry, the people cry to Pharaoh. Pharaoh says: 'Go to Joseph. What he says to you, do.'

verses.text: The famine was over all the surface of the earth. Joseph opened all the store houses, and sold to the Egyptians. The famine was severe in the land of Egypt.
verses.explainer: The famine is over all the face of the earth. Joseph opens the storehouses and sells grain to the Egyptians.

verses.text: All countries came into Egypt, to Joseph, to buy grain, because the famine was severe in all the earth.
verses.explainer: All the earth comes to Egypt to buy grain because the famine is severe everywhere. This global famine will draw Joseph's brothers to Egypt and fulfill his dreams.

explainer.summary: Jacob sends ten of his sons to Egypt to buy grain, keeping Benjamin at home. Joseph recognizes his brothers, but they do not recognize him. He accuses them of being spies and puts them in prison for three days. He then releases them but keeps Simeon as hostage, demanding they return with Benjamin to prove their story. He secretly returns their money in their sacks, terrifying them. They return to Jacob and report everything. Jacob refuses to let Benjamin go, mourning that everything is against him.
themes: The dreams begin to be fulfilled
explainer.contextInBook: The brothers who bowed to Joseph's sheaf in his dream now bow to the ruler of Egypt without knowing he is their brother. The testing will reveal whether they have changed.

verses.text: Now Jacob saw that there was grain in Egypt, and Jacob said to his sons, “Why do you look at one another?”
verses.explainer: Jacob hears there is grain in Egypt and asks his sons: 'Why do you look at one another?' Hunger demands action.

verses.text: He said, “Behold, I have heard that there is grain in Egypt. Go down there, and buy for us from there, so that we may live, and not die.”
verses.explainer: Jacob says: 'I have heard there is grain in Egypt. Go down and buy so that we may live and not die.'

verses.text: Joseph’s ten brothers went down to buy grain from Egypt.
verses.explainer: Ten of Joseph's brothers go to buy grain in Egypt.

verses.text: But Jacob didn’t send Benjamin, Joseph’s brother, with his brothers; for he said, “Lest perhaps harm happen to him.”
verses.explainer: Jacob does not send Benjamin, Joseph's full brother, fearing harm might come to him. Benjamin is now the favored son, the last link to Rachel.

verses.text: The sons of Israel came to buy among those who came, for the famine was in the land of Canaan.
verses.explainer: The sons of Israel are among those who come to buy grain, for the famine is in Canaan.

verses.text: Joseph was the governor over the land. It was he who sold to all the people of the land. Joseph’s brothers came, and bowed themselves down to him with their faces to the earth.
verses.explainer: Joseph is the governor of the land, the one who sells to all people. His brothers come and bow down to him with their faces to the ground. The dream of 37:7 is being fulfilled -- the sheaves bow to Joseph's sheaf.

verses.text:
Joseph saw his brothers, and he recognized them, but acted like a stranger to them, and spoke roughly with them. He said to them, “Where did you come from?”
They said, “From the land of Canaan to buy food.”
verses.explainer: Joseph sees his brothers and recognizes them, but he disguises himself and speaks harshly: 'Where do you come from?' He knows who they are.

verses.text: Joseph recognized his brothers, but they didn’t recognize him.
verses.explainer: Joseph recognizes his brothers, but they do not recognize him. He was seventeen when they sold him; he is now almost forty, Egyptian in dress, language, and authority.

verses.text: Joseph remembered the dreams which he dreamed about them, and said to them, “You are spies! You have come to see the nakedness of the land.”
verses.explainer: Joseph remembers the dreams he had about them. He accuses them: 'You are spies! You have come to see the nakedness of the land.'

verses.text: They said to him, “No, my lord, but your servants have come to buy food.
verses.explainer: They protest: 'No, my lord, your servants have come to buy food.'

verses.text: We are all one man’s sons; we are honest men. Your servants are not spies.”
verses.explainer: They say: 'We are all sons of one man. We are honest men. Your servants are not spies.'

verses.text: He said to them, “No, but you have come to see the nakedness of the land!”
verses.explainer: Joseph insists: 'No, you have come to see the nakedness of the land.'

verses.text: They said, “We, your servants, are twelve brothers, the sons of one man in the land of Canaan; and behold, the youngest is today with our father, and one is no more.”
verses.explainer: They explain: 'We, your servants, are twelve brothers, sons of one man in Canaan. The youngest is with our father, and one is no more.' They mention Joseph without knowing they are talking to him.

verses.text: Joseph said to them, “It is like I told you, saying, ‘You are spies!’
verses.explainer: Joseph says: 'It is as I said: you are spies.'

verses.text: By this you shall be tested. By the life of Pharaoh, you shall not go out from here, unless your youngest brother comes here.
verses.explainer: He gives them a test: 'By the life of Pharaoh, you shall not leave this place unless your youngest brother comes here.'

verses.text: Send one of you, and let him get your brother, and you shall be bound, that your words may be tested, whether there is truth in you, or else by the life of Pharaoh surely you are spies.”
verses.explainer: He says: 'Send one of you to get your brother. The rest of you will remain in prison.' This is the test: will they sacrifice Benjamin to save themselves?

verses.text: He put them all together into custody for three days.
verses.explainer: He puts them all in custody for three days. Three days in a pit -- echoing Joseph's own experience.

verses.text: Joseph said to them the third day, “Do this, and live, for I fear God.
verses.explainer: On the third day, Joseph changes the terms: 'Do this and you will live, for I fear God.'

verses.text: If you are honest men, then let one of your brothers be bound in your prison; but you go, carry grain for the famine of your houses.
verses.explainer: He says: 'If you are honest men, let one of your brothers remain confined while the rest carry grain back for your families.'

verses.text:
Bring your youngest brother to me; so will your words be verified, and you won’t die.”
They did so.
verses.explainer: He demands: 'Bring your youngest brother to me. Then your words will be verified, and you will not die.'

verses.text: They said to one another, “We are certainly guilty concerning our brother, in that we saw the distress of his soul, when he begged us, and we wouldn’t listen. Therefore this distress has come upon us.”
verses.explainer: The brothers say to one another: 'We are paying for what we did to Joseph. We saw his distress when he pleaded with us, but we would not listen. That is why this distress has come upon us.' After twenty years, guilt surfaces.

verses.text: Reuben answered them, saying, “Didn’t I tell you, saying, ‘Don’t sin against the child,’ and you wouldn’t listen? Therefore also, behold, his blood is required.”
verses.explainer: Reuben says: 'Did I not tell you not to sin against the boy? But you would not listen. Now comes the reckoning for his blood.' Reuben reminds them of his earlier protest (37:22).

verses.text: They didn’t know that Joseph understood them; for there was an interpreter between them.
verses.explainer: They do not know that Joseph understands them, because he has been using an interpreter. Joseph hears their guilt and remorse in their own language.

verses.text: He turned himself away from them, and wept. Then he returned to them, and spoke to them, and took Simeon from among them, and bound him before their eyes.
verses.explainer: Joseph turns away from them and weeps. Then he turns back and speaks to them. He takes Simeon and binds him before their eyes.

verses.text: Then Joseph gave a command to fill their bags with grain, and to restore each man’s money into his sack, and to give them food for the way. So it was done to them.
verses.explainer: Joseph orders their bags filled with grain, their money returned secretly in each sack, and provisions given for the journey. He shows generosity while maintaining the test.

verses.text: They loaded their donkeys with their grain, and departed from there.
verses.explainer: They load their donkeys and depart.

verses.text: As one of them opened his sack to give his donkey food in the lodging place, he saw his money. Behold, it was in the mouth of his sack.
verses.explainer: At the lodging place, one opens his sack for feed and discovers his money in the mouth of his sack.

verses.text: He said to his brothers, “My money is restored! Behold, it is in my sack!” Their hearts failed them, and they turned trembling to one another, saying, “What is this that God has done to us?”
verses.explainer: He tells his brothers: 'My money has been put back!' Their hearts sink. They tremble: 'What is this that God has done to us?' They interpret everything through the lens of guilt.

verses.text: They came to Jacob their father, to the land of Canaan, and told him all that had happened to them, saying,
verses.explainer: They return to Jacob and report everything.

verses.text: “The man, the lord of the land, spoke roughly with us, and took us for spies of the country.
verses.explainer: They tell Jacob: 'The man who is lord of the land spoke harshly to us and treated us as spies.'

verses.text: We said to him, ‘We are honest men. We are no spies.
verses.explainer: They said they were honest men, not spies.

verses.text: We are twelve brothers, sons of our father; one is no more, and the youngest is today with our father in the land of Canaan.’
verses.explainer: They explained they were twelve brothers, one is no more, and the youngest is with their father in Canaan.

verses.text: The man, the lord of the land, said to us, ‘By this I will know that you are honest men: leave one of your brothers with me, and take grain for the famine of your houses, and go your way.
verses.explainer: The lord of the land said: 'Leave one brother with me, take food for your households, and go.'

verses.text: Bring your youngest brother to me. Then I will know that you are not spies, but that you are honest men. So I will deliver your brother to you, and you shall trade in the land.’”
verses.explainer: 'Bring your youngest brother to me, and I will know you are honest. I will give you back your brother, and you may trade in the land.'

verses.text: As they emptied their sacks, behold, each man’s bundle of money was in his sack. When they and their father saw their bundles of money, they were afraid.
verses.explainer: As they empty their sacks, every man's bundle of money is in his sack. They and their father are afraid.

verses.text: Jacob, their father, said to them, “You have bereaved me of my children! Joseph is no more, Simeon is no more, and you want to take Benjamin away. All these things are against me.”
verses.explainer: Jacob says: 'You have bereaved me of my children. Joseph is no more, Simeon is no more, and now you would take Benjamin. Everything is against me.' Jacob's lament is heartbreaking -- he sees only loss.

verses.text: Reuben spoke to his father, saying, “Kill my two sons, if I don’t bring him to you. Entrust him to my care, and I will bring him to you again.”
verses.explainer: Reuben offers: 'Kill my two sons if I do not bring Benjamin back to you.' It is a desperate pledge that Jacob cannot take seriously -- killing his own grandchildren would add grief to grief.

verses.text: He said, “My son shall not go down with you; for his brother is dead, and he only is left. If harm happens to him along the way in which you go, then you will bring down my gray hairs with sorrow to Sheol.”
verses.explainer: Jacob refuses: 'My son shall not go down with you, for his brother is dead, and he alone is left. If harm should come to him, you would bring down my gray hairs with sorrow to Sheol.' Jacob clings to Benjamin.

explainer.summary: The famine worsens, and the family needs more grain. Jacob reluctantly agrees to send Benjamin after Judah personally guarantees his safety. The brothers return to Egypt with gifts and double money. Joseph sees Benjamin, is overwhelmed with emotion, and invites them to a meal at his house. The brothers are terrified, fearing the returned money was a trap. Joseph's steward reassures them and brings Simeon out. Joseph is moved to tears when he sees Benjamin but controls himself. At the feast, the brothers are seated in birth order (which astonishes them), and Benjamin receives five times as much food as the others.
explainer.contextInBook: Judah's guarantee of Benjamin marks his transformation from the brother who suggested selling Joseph (37:26) to the brother who will risk everything for Benjamin (44:33). Joseph watches to see if the brothers will resent Benjamin's special treatment as they resented his.

verses.text: The famine was severe in the land.
verses.explainer: The famine is severe in the land.

verses.text: When they had eaten up the grain which they had brought out of Egypt, their father said to them, “Go again, buy us a little more food.”
verses.explainer: When they have eaten all the grain from Egypt, Jacob says: 'Go again, buy us a little food.'

verses.text: Judah spoke to him, saying, “The man solemnly warned us, saying, ‘You shall not see my face, unless your brother is with you.’
verses.explainer: Judah warns: 'The man solemnly warned us: you shall not see my face unless your brother is with you.'

verses.text: If you’ll send our brother with us, we’ll go down and buy you food,
verses.explainer: If Jacob sends Benjamin, they will go. If not, they cannot.

verses.text: but if you’ll not send him, we’ll not go down, for the man said to us, ‘You shall not see my face, unless your brother is with you.’”
verses.explainer: Judah is firm: 'The man said to us, You shall not see my face unless your brother is with you.'

verses.text: Israel said, “Why did you treat me so badly, telling the man that you had another brother?”
verses.explainer: Jacob asks: 'Why did you treat me so badly by telling the man you had another brother?'

verses.text: They said, “The man asked directly concerning ourselves, and concerning our relatives, saying, ‘Is your father still alive? Have you another brother?’ We just answered his questions. Is there any way we could know that he would say, ‘Bring your brother down?’”
verses.explainer: They explain: 'The man questioned us directly: Is your father still alive? Do you have another brother? We answered his questions. How could we know he would say bring your brother?'

verses.text: Judah said to Israel, his father, “Send the boy with me, and we’ll get up and go, so that we may live, and not die, both we, and you, and also our little ones.
verses.explainer: Judah says: 'Send the boy with me, and we will arise and go, that we may live and not die -- both we and you and our little ones.'

verses.text: I’ll be collateral for him. From my hand will you require him. If I don’t bring him to you, and set him before you, then let me bear the blame forever,
verses.explainer: Judah pledges: 'I will be a guarantee for him. If I do not bring him back, let me bear the blame forever.' This is a personal guarantee that puts Judah's honor on the line.

thenVsNow.then: Judah is offering himself as a surety -- a legal commitment to bear the consequences of failure. This is the same man who sold Joseph for twenty shekels. His transformation is remarkable.
thenVsNow.now: Judah's role as guarantor for Benjamin foreshadows the concept of vicarious responsibility that runs through Scripture: one person bearing the consequences for another.

verses.text: for if we hadn’t delayed, surely we would have returned a second time by now.”
verses.explainer: Judah points out: 'If we had not delayed, we could have gone and returned twice already.'

verses.text: Their father, Israel, said to them, “If it must be so, then do this. Take from the choice fruits of the land in your bags, and carry down a present for the man, a little balm, a little honey, spices and myrrh, nuts, and almonds;
verses.explainer: Jacob relents: 'If it must be so, then take the best products of the land as a gift: balm, honey, gum, myrrh, pistachios, and almonds.'

verses.text: and take double money in your hand, and take back the money that was returned in the mouth of your sacks. Perhaps it was an oversight.
verses.explainer: He tells them to take double money -- the money that was returned plus money for new grain. Perhaps the return was a mistake.

verses.text: Take your brother also, get up, and return to the man.
verses.explainer: He says: 'Take your brother also, and arise, go to the man.'

verses.text: May God Almighty give you mercy before the man, that he may release to you your other brother and Benjamin. If I am bereaved of my children, I am bereaved.”
verses.explainer: Jacob prays: 'May God Almighty grant you mercy before the man, and may he send back your other brother and Benjamin. If I am bereaved, I am bereaved.' Resignation mixed with faith.

verses.text: The men took that present, and they took double money in their hand, and Benjamin; and got up, went down to Egypt, and stood before Joseph.
verses.explainer: The brothers take the gifts, double money, and Benjamin. They go down to Egypt and stand before Joseph.

verses.text: When Joseph saw Benjamin with them, he said to the steward of his house, “Bring the men into the house, and butcher an animal, and prepare; for the men will dine with me at noon.”
verses.explainer: When Joseph sees Benjamin with them, he tells his steward: 'Bring the men to my house, slaughter an animal, and prepare a meal. These men will dine with me at noon.'

verses.text: The man did as Joseph commanded, and the man brought the men to Joseph’s house.
verses.explainer: The steward does as Joseph says and brings the men to Joseph's house.

verses.text: The men were afraid, because they were brought to Joseph’s house; and they said, “Because of the money that was returned in our sacks the first time, we’re brought in; that he may seek occasion against us, attack us, and seize us as slaves, along with our donkeys.”
verses.explainer: The brothers are afraid: 'We are brought in because of the money returned in our sacks. He will attack us, seize us as slaves, and take our donkeys.'

verses.text: They came near to the steward of Joseph’s house, and they spoke to him at the door of the house,
verses.explainer: They approach the steward at the door and explain about the money.

verses.text: and said, “Oh, my lord, we indeed came down the first time to buy food.
verses.explainer: They say they have come to buy food.

verses.text: When we came to the lodging place, we opened our sacks, and behold, each man’s money was in the mouth of his sack, our money in full weight. We have brought it back in our hand.
verses.explainer: They recount finding the money in their sacks and have brought it back along with more money.

verses.text: We have brought down other money in our hand to buy food. We don’t know who put our money in our sacks.”
verses.explainer: They do not know who put the money in their sacks.

verses.text: He said, “Peace be to you. Don’t be afraid. Your God, and the God of your father, has given you treasure in your sacks. I received your money.” He brought Simeon out to them.
verses.explainer: The steward says: 'Peace to you. Do not be afraid. Your God, the God of your father, must have put treasure in your sacks. I received your money.' He brings Simeon out to them.

verses.text: The man brought the men into Joseph’s house, and gave them water, and they washed their feet. He gave their donkeys fodder.
