verses.explainer: The steward brings them into Joseph's house, gives them water to wash their feet, and feeds their donkeys.

verses.text: They prepared the present for Joseph’s coming at noon, for they heard that they should eat bread there.
verses.explainer: They prepare their gifts for Joseph's arrival at noon, having heard they would eat there.

verses.text: When Joseph came home, they brought him the present which was in their hand into the house, and bowed themselves down to the earth before him.
verses.explainer: When Joseph arrives, they present the gifts and bow to the ground before him. The dream continues to be fulfilled.

verses.text: He asked them of their welfare, and said, “Is your father well, the old man of whom you spoke? Is he yet alive?”
verses.explainer: Joseph asks: 'Is your father well, the old man of whom you spoke? Is he still alive?'

verses.text: They said, “Your servant, our father, is well. He is still alive.” They bowed down humbly.
verses.explainer: They answer: 'Your servant our father is well; he is still alive.' They bow down in homage.

verses.text: He lifted up his eyes, and saw Benjamin, his brother, his mother’s son, and said, “Is this your youngest brother, of whom you spoke to me?” He said, “God be gracious to you, my son.”
verses.explainer: Joseph looks up and sees Benjamin, his mother's son: 'Is this your youngest brother of whom you spoke to me?' He says: 'God be gracious to you, my son.'

verses.text: Joseph hurried, for his heart yearned over his brother; and he sought a place to weep. He entered into his room, and wept there.
verses.explainer: Joseph hurries out because his compassion for his brother overwhelms him. He seeks a place to weep. He weeps in his private chamber.

verses.text: He washed his face, and came out. He controlled himself, and said, “Serve the meal.”
verses.explainer: He washes his face, controls himself, and comes out. He says: 'Serve the food.'

verses.text: They served him by himself, and them by themselves, and the Egyptians, that ate with him, by themselves, because the Egyptians don’t eat bread with the Hebrews, for that is an abomination to the Egyptians.
verses.explainer: They serve Joseph by himself, the brothers by themselves, and the Egyptians by themselves, because Egyptians could not eat with Hebrews. Joseph maintains Egyptian customs.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Egyptians not eating with Hebrews', 'explanation': 'The Egyptians considered eating with foreigners an abomination, likely due to dietary and ritual purity concerns. This segregation reflects the deep cultural divisions Joseph navigates between his Egyptian position and his Hebrew identity.'}

verses.text: They sat before him, the firstborn according to his birthright, and the youngest according to his youth, and the men marveled one with another.
verses.explainer: The brothers are seated before Joseph in order from firstborn to youngest. They look at one another in astonishment. How does this Egyptian know their birth order?

verses.text: He sent portions to them from before him, but Benjamin’s portion was five times as much as any of theirs. They drank, and were merry with him.
verses.explainer: Portions are taken from Joseph's table to them, but Benjamin's portion is five times as much as any of theirs. This is a test: will they resent the preferential treatment of the youngest, as they resented Joseph's? They feast and drink freely with him. They pass the test.

explainer.summary: Joseph orchestrates a final test: he has his silver cup hidden in Benjamin's sack. After the brothers leave, Joseph sends his steward to overtake them and accuse them of theft. The cup is found in Benjamin's sack. The brothers tear their clothes and return to Joseph. Judah delivers one of the most moving speeches in all of Scripture, offering himself as a slave in Benjamin's place, rather than return to their father without him. This is the moment Joseph has been waiting for: his brothers have changed.
themes: The final test of the brothers
The silver cup as a test device
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter answers the question the entire testing sequence has been building toward: have the brothers changed? Will they abandon another favored son to save themselves? Judah's speech proves they will not. They are not the same men who sold Joseph.

verses.text: He commanded the steward of his house, saying, “Fill the men’s sacks with food, as much as they can carry, and put each man’s money in his sack’s mouth.
verses.explainer: Joseph commands his steward: fill the men's sacks with food, return each man's money, and put my silver cup in the youngest one's sack.

verses.text: Put my cup, the silver cup, in the sack’s mouth of the youngest, with his grain money.” He did according to the word that Joseph had spoken.
verses.explainer: The steward does exactly as Joseph says. The trap is set.

verses.text: As soon as the morning was light, the men were sent away, they and their donkeys.
verses.explainer: At daylight, the men are sent on their way with their donkeys.

verses.text: When they had gone out of the city, and were not yet far off, Joseph said to his steward, “Up, follow after the men. When you overtake them, ask them, ‘Why have you rewarded evil for good?
verses.explainer: When they have gone a short distance, Joseph tells his steward: 'Go after them. Ask: why have you repaid evil for good?'

verses.text: Isn’t this that from which my lord drinks, and by which he indeed divines? You have done evil in so doing.’”
verses.explainer: Joseph instructs: 'Is this not the cup from which my lord drinks and uses for divination? You have done evil.' The divination reference fits Joseph's Egyptian persona.

verses.text: He overtook them, and he spoke these words to them.
verses.explainer: The steward overtakes them and repeats the accusation.

verses.text: They said to him, “Why does my lord speak such words as these? Far be it from your servants that they should do such a thing!
verses.explainer: They protest: 'Why would my lord say such things? Far be it from your servants to do such a thing!'

verses.text: Behold, the money, which we found in our sacks’ mouths, we brought again to you out of the land of Canaan. How then should we steal silver or gold out of your lord’s house?
verses.explainer: They argue: 'We brought back the money we found in our sacks. Why would we steal silver or gold from your lord's house?'

verses.text: With whomever of your servants it is found, let him die, and we also will be my lord’s slaves.”
verses.explainer: They declare: 'Whichever of your servants is found with it shall die, and the rest of us will be your lord's slaves.' They are confident of their innocence.

verses.text: He said, “Now also let it be according to your words: he with whom it is found will be my slave; and you will be blameless.”
verses.explainer: The steward agrees but softens the penalty: only the one found with the cup will become a slave; the rest may go free.

verses.text: Then they hurried, and each man took his sack down to the ground, and each man opened his sack.
verses.explainer: Each man quickly lowers his sack and opens it.

verses.text: He searched, beginning with the oldest, and ending at the youngest. The cup was found in Benjamin’s sack.
verses.explainer: The steward searches from eldest to youngest. The cup is found in Benjamin's sack. The suspense builds as each sack proves empty.

verses.text: Then they tore their clothes, and each man loaded his donkey, and returned to the city.
verses.explainer: They tear their clothes. Every one of them loads his donkey and returns to the city. Note: they all return. No one abandons Benjamin. This is the first sign of change.

verses.text: Judah and his brothers came to Joseph’s house, and he was still there. They fell on the ground before him.
verses.explainer: Judah and his brothers come before Joseph. They fall to the ground before him.

verses.text: Joseph said to them, “What deed is this that you have done? Don’t you know that such a man as I can indeed divine?”
verses.explainer: Joseph says: 'What deed is this you have done? Do you not know that a man like me can practice divination?'

verses.text: Judah said, “What will we tell my lord? What will we speak? Or how will we clear ourselves? God has found out the iniquity of your servants. Behold, we are my lord’s slaves, both we, and he also in whose hand the cup is found.”
verses.explainer: Judah says: 'What shall we say to my lord? How shall we clear ourselves? God has found out the guilt of your servants. We are all my lord's slaves, both we and the one found with the cup.' Judah does not protest innocence; he accepts collective responsibility.

verses.text: He said, “Far be it from me that I should do so. The man in whose hand the cup is found, he will be my slave; but as for you, go up in peace to your father.”
verses.explainer: Joseph says: 'Far be it from me to do that. Only the man found with the cup shall be my slave. The rest of you, go in peace to your father.' This is the ultimate test: can they leave Benjamin behind?

verses.text: Then Judah came near to him, and said, “Oh, my lord, please let your servant speak a word in my lord’s ears, and don’t let your anger burn against your servant; for you are even as Pharaoh.
verses.explainer: Judah steps forward: 'Oh, my lord, please let your servant speak a word in my lord's ears. Do not let your anger burn against your servant, for you are like Pharaoh himself.' Judah begins the most powerful speech in Genesis.

verses.text: My lord asked his servants, saying, ‘Have you a father, or a brother?’
verses.explainer: He recounts: 'My lord asked his servants, Do you have a father or a brother?'

verses.text: We said to my lord, ‘We have a father, an old man, and a child of his old age, a little one; and his brother is dead, and he alone is left of his mother; and his father loves him.’
verses.explainer: Judah said: 'We have a father, an old man, and a young brother, the child of his old age. His brother is dead, and he alone is left of his mother's children, and his father loves him.'

verses.text: You said to your servants, ‘Bring him down to me, that I may set my eyes on him.’
verses.explainer: Judah recounts Joseph's demand: 'Bring him down to me that I may set my eyes on him.'

verses.text: We said to my lord, ‘The boy can’t leave his father: for if he should leave his father, his father would die.’
verses.explainer: They told the lord: 'The boy cannot leave his father, for if he leaves, his father will die.'

verses.text: You said to your servants, ‘Unless your youngest brother comes down with you, you will see my face no more.’
verses.explainer: The lord said: 'Unless your youngest brother comes down with you, you shall not see my face.'

verses.text: When we came up to your servant my father, we told him the words of my lord.
verses.explainer: Judah says: 'When we went back to your servant my father, we told him what you said.'

verses.text: Our father said, ‘Go again and buy us a little food.’
verses.explainer: Their father said: 'Go again, buy a little food.'

verses.text: We said, ‘We can’t go down. If our youngest brother is with us, then we will go down: for we may not see the man’s face, unless our youngest brother is with us.’
verses.explainer: They said: 'We cannot go down unless our youngest brother is with us.'

verses.text: Your servant, my father, said to us, ‘You know that my wife bore me two sons:
verses.explainer: Their father said: 'You know that my wife bore me two sons.' Jacob speaks of Rachel's sons as uniquely his -- a painful reminder of his favoritism.

verses.text: and the one went out from me, and I said, “Surely he is torn in pieces”; and I haven’t seen him since.
verses.explainer: 'One left me, and I said: Surely he has been torn to pieces. I have never seen him since.'

verses.text: If you take this one also from me, and harm happens to him, you will bring down my gray hairs with sorrow to Sheol.’
verses.explainer: 'If you take this one also from me, and harm comes to him, you will bring down my gray hairs with sorrow to Sheol.'

verses.text: Now therefore when I come to your servant my father, and the boy is not with us; since his life is bound up in the boy’s life;
verses.explainer: Judah says: 'If we come to your servant my father, and the boy is not with us -- since his life is bound up in the boy's life --'

verses.text: it will happen, when he sees that the boy is no more, that he will die. Your servants will bring down the gray hairs of your servant, our father, with sorrow to Sheol.
verses.explainer: 'When he sees that the boy is not with us, he will die, and your servants will bring down the gray hairs of your servant our father with sorrow to Sheol.'

verses.text: For your servant became collateral for the boy to my father, saying, ‘If I don’t bring him to you, then I will bear the blame to my father forever.’
verses.explainer: Judah reveals his pledge: 'Your servant became a guarantee for the boy to my father, saying: If I do not bring him back, I will bear the blame before my father all my life.'

verses.text: Now therefore, please let your servant stay instead of the boy, my lord’s slave; and let the boy go up with his brothers.
verses.explainer: The climax: 'Now therefore, please let your servant remain instead of the boy as a slave to my lord, and let the boy go back with his brothers.' Judah offers himself as a substitute. The man who sold Joseph into slavery now offers to become a slave to save Joseph's brother.

thenVsNow.then: Judah's offer of self-substitution is the most dramatic moment of character transformation in the Old Testament. The man who profited from selling one brother now sacrifices himself for another.
thenVsNow.now: Christians see in Judah's substitution a foreshadowing of Christ's atonement: the guilty one offering himself in place of the innocent. It is from Judah's line that Jesus -- 'the Lion of the tribe of Judah' -- will come.

verses.text: For how will I go up to my father, if the boy isn’t with me?—lest I see the evil that will come on my father.”
verses.explainer: Judah says: 'For how can I go back to my father if the boy is not with me? I could not bear to see the suffering that would come on my father.' Judah has learned to feel his father's pain. The test is over.

explainer.summary: Joseph can no longer control himself. He sends all the Egyptians out and reveals his identity to his brothers: 'I am Joseph! Is my father still alive?' The brothers are terrified, but Joseph reassures them with one of the greatest theological statements in the Bible: 'You meant it for evil, but God sent me before you to preserve life.' He weeps, embraces Benjamin, kisses all his brothers, and tells them to bring Jacob and the entire family to Egypt. Pharaoh generously supports the invitation. The brothers return to Canaan with the news that Joseph is alive.
explainer.contextInBook: This is the emotional climax of Genesis. Twenty-two years of separation, grief, and guilt end in a moment of recognition, weeping, and forgiveness. Joseph's theological interpretation of his suffering gives meaning to the entire narrative.

verses.text: Then Joseph couldn’t control himself before all those who stood before him, and he cried, “Cause everyone to go out from me!” No one else stood with him, while Joseph made himself known to his brothers.
verses.explainer: Joseph cannot control himself before all his attendants. He cries: 'Make everyone go out!' No one else is present when he reveals himself. This is a private family moment.

verses.text: He wept aloud. The Egyptians heard, and the house of Pharaoh heard.
verses.explainer: He weeps so loudly that the Egyptians hear it, and Pharaoh's household hears it. The emotional release after years of concealment.

verses.text:
Joseph said to his brothers, “I am Joseph! Does my father still live?”
His brothers couldn’t answer him; for they were terrified at his presence.
verses.explainer: Joseph says to his brothers: 'I am Joseph! Is my father still alive?' The brothers cannot answer because they are dismayed at his presence. Terror, not joy, is the initial response.

verses.text:
Joseph said to his brothers, “Come near to me, please.”
They came near. “He said, I am Joseph, your brother, whom you sold into Egypt.
verses.explainer: Joseph says: 'Come near to me.' They come near. He says: 'I am your brother Joseph, whom you sold into Egypt.' He names the truth directly.

verses.text: Now don’t be grieved, nor angry with yourselves, that you sold me here, for God sent me before you to preserve life.
verses.explainer: He says: 'Do not be distressed or angry with yourselves because you sold me here, for God sent me before you to preserve life.' This is the theological key to the entire narrative. Joseph does not deny their guilt but reframes it within God's larger purpose.

thenVsNow.then: In the ancient world, the idea that a god could use human evil for good purposes was not common. Gods were typically seen as either causing evil directly or being indifferent to it. Joseph's theology of providence -- that God works through human choices, even evil ones, without excusing them -- was groundbreaking.
thenVsNow.now: Joseph's statement 'God sent me before you to preserve life' has become one of the most frequently cited passages in discussions of divine providence. It holds two truths in tension: human responsibility and divine sovereignty.

verses.text: For these two years the famine has been in the land, and there are yet five years, in which there will be no plowing and no harvest.
verses.explainer: The famine has lasted two years; five more are coming. No plowing or harvesting. The practical urgency drives the invitation.

verses.text: God sent me before you to preserve for you a remnant in the earth, and to save you alive by a great deliverance.
verses.explainer: God sent Joseph ahead to preserve a remnant on earth and to save their lives by a great deliverance. Joseph sees his suffering as part of a rescue mission orchestrated by God.

verses.text: So now it wasn’t you who sent me here, but God, and he has made me a father to Pharaoh, lord of all his house, and ruler over all the land of Egypt.
verses.explainer: Joseph says: 'It was not you who sent me here, but God.' He does not excuse what they did; he transcends it. God's purpose does not eliminate human responsibility, but it does give the suffering meaning.

verses.text: Hurry, and go up to my father, and tell him, ‘This is what your son Joseph says, “God has made me lord of all Egypt. Come down to me. Don’t wait.
verses.explainer: Joseph urges them: 'Hurry back to my father and say: Your son Joseph says God has made me lord of all Egypt. Come down to me; do not delay.'

verses.text: You shall dwell in the land of Goshen, and you will be near to me, you, your children, your children’s children, your flocks, your herds, and all that you have.
verses.explainer: He offers them the land of Goshen, near him, with their families, flocks, herds, and everything.

verses.text: There I will nourish you; for there are yet five years of famine; lest you come to poverty, you, and your household, and all that you have.”’
verses.explainer: Joseph will provide for them during the remaining five years of famine.

verses.text: Behold, your eyes see, and the eyes of my brother Benjamin, that it is my mouth that speaks to you.
verses.explainer: He says: 'Your own eyes and Benjamin's eyes can see that it is my mouth speaking to you.' He speaks in Hebrew, confirming his identity.

verses.text: You shall tell my father of all my glory in Egypt, and of all that you have seen. You shall hurry and bring my father down here.”
verses.explainer: He says: 'Tell my father about all my honor in Egypt and everything you have seen. Bring my father down here quickly.'

verses.text: He fell on his brother Benjamin’s neck, and wept, and Benjamin wept on his neck.
verses.explainer: Joseph falls on Benjamin's neck and weeps. Benjamin weeps on his neck. The full brothers, Rachel's sons, reunite.

verses.text: He kissed all his brothers, and wept on them. After that his brothers talked with him.
verses.explainer: He kisses all his brothers and weeps on them. After this, his brothers talk with him. The ice of terror thaws into conversation.

verses.text: The report of it was heard in Pharaoh’s house, saying, “Joseph’s brothers have come.” It pleased Pharaoh well, and his servants.
verses.explainer: The report reaches Pharaoh's house: 'Joseph's brothers have come.' It pleases Pharaoh and his servants.

verses.text: Pharaoh said to Joseph, “Tell your brothers, ‘Do this. Load your animals, and go, travel to the land of Canaan.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh tells Joseph: 'Say to your brothers, load your animals and go to Canaan.'

verses.text: Take your father and your households, and come to me, and I will give you the good of the land of Egypt, and you will eat the fat of the land.’
verses.explainer: Pharaoh says: 'Get your father and your households and come to me. I will give you the best of the land of Egypt.' The invitation comes with royal authority.

verses.text: Now you are commanded: do this. Take wagons out of the land of Egypt for your little ones, and for your wives, and bring your father, and come.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh provides wagons for the children, wives, and for Jacob.

verses.text: Also, don’t concern yourselves about your belongings, for the good of all the land of Egypt is yours.”
verses.explainer: He says: 'Do not worry about your belongings, for the best of all the land of Egypt is yours.'

verses.text: The sons of Israel did so. Joseph gave them wagons, according to the commandment of Pharaoh, and gave them provision for the way.
verses.explainer: The sons of Israel do so. Joseph gives them wagons and provisions as Pharaoh commanded.

verses.text: He gave each one of them changes of clothing, but to Benjamin he gave three hundred pieces of silver and five changes of clothing.
verses.explainer: He gives each brother a change of clothes, but to Benjamin he gives three hundred shekels of silver and five changes of clothes. Benjamin gets special treatment -- will the brothers resent it this time?

verses.text: He sent the following to his father: ten donkeys loaded with the good things of Egypt, and ten female donkeys loaded with grain and bread and provision for his father by the way.
verses.explainer: He sends to his father ten donkeys loaded with the good things of Egypt and ten female donkeys loaded with grain, bread, and provisions.

verses.text: So he sent his brothers away, and they departed. He said to them, “See that you don’t quarrel on the way.”
verses.explainer: He sends his brothers off: 'Do not quarrel on the way.' He knows his brothers. The admonition is practical wisdom.

verses.text: They went up out of Egypt, and came into the land of Canaan, to Jacob their father.
verses.explainer: They go up from Egypt and come to Jacob in Canaan.

verses.text: They told him, saying, “Joseph is still alive, and he is ruler over all the land of Egypt.” His heart fainted, for he didn’t believe them.
verses.explainer: They tell Jacob: 'Joseph is still alive, and he is ruler over all the land of Egypt.' Jacob's heart grows numb; he does not believe them. The shock is too great.

verses.text: They told him all the words of Joseph, which he had said to them. When he saw the wagons which Joseph had sent to carry him, the spirit of Jacob, their father, revived.
verses.explainer: They tell him everything Joseph said. When Jacob sees the wagons Joseph sent, his spirit revives.

verses.text: Israel said, “It is enough. Joseph my son is still alive. I will go and see him before I die.”
verses.explainer: Israel says: 'It is enough. Joseph my son is still alive. I will go and see him before I die.' The father who refused to be comforted (37:35) is finally alive again.

explainer.summary: Jacob sets out for Egypt with everything he has. At Beersheba, God speaks to him in a night vision, reassuring him not to fear going to Egypt and promising to bring his descendants back. A detailed genealogy lists seventy members of Jacob's family who go to Egypt. Joseph meets Jacob in Goshen, and they embrace in one of the most tender reunion scenes in the Bible. Joseph coaches his brothers on what to tell Pharaoh to secure settlement in Goshen.
themes: God's reassurance for a frightening journey
The seventy who go to Egypt
The reunion of father and son
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter marks the transition from Canaan to Egypt that will define Israel's story for the next four hundred years. Jacob's family enters Egypt as honored guests; they will leave as liberated slaves.

verses.text: Israel traveled with all that he had, and came to Beersheba, and offered sacrifices to the God of his father, Isaac.
verses.explainer: Israel sets out with all he has and comes to Beersheba, where he offers sacrifices to the God of his father Isaac. Beersheba is on the border -- the last stop before leaving the Promised Land.

verses.text:
God spoke to Israel in the visions of the night, and said, “Jacob, Jacob!”
He said, “Here I am.”
verses.explainer: God speaks to Israel in visions of the night: 'Jacob! Jacob!' He answers: 'Here I am.' The same hinneni of Abraham and Joseph.

verses.text: He said, “I am God, the God of your father. Don’t be afraid to go down into Egypt, for there I will make of you a great nation.
verses.explainer: God says: 'I am God, the God of your father. Do not be afraid to go down to Egypt, for there I will make you into a great nation.' The fear is reasonable -- leaving the Promised Land feels like abandoning the promise. God says it is part of the plan.

verses.text: I will go down with you into Egypt. I will also surely bring you up again. Joseph will close your eyes.”
verses.explainer: God promises: 'I myself will go down with you to Egypt, and I will also bring you up again. Joseph's hand shall close your eyes.' Two assurances: God goes with Jacob, and Joseph will be at his deathbed.

verses.text: Jacob rose up from Beersheba, and the sons of Israel carried Jacob, their father, their little ones, and their wives, in the wagons which Pharaoh had sent to carry him.
verses.explainer: Jacob leaves Beersheba. His sons carry him, their little ones, and their wives in the wagons Pharaoh sent.

verses.text: They took their livestock, and their goods, which they had gotten in the land of Canaan, and came into Egypt—Jacob, and all his offspring with him,
verses.explainer: They take their livestock and possessions from Canaan and come to Egypt -- Jacob and all his offspring.

verses.text: his sons, and his sons’ sons with him, his daughters, and his sons’ daughters, and he brought all his offspring with him into Egypt.
verses.explainer: His sons and grandsons, daughters and granddaughters -- all his offspring he brings with him to Egypt.

verses.text: These are the names of the children of Israel, who came into Egypt, Jacob and his sons: Reuben, Jacob’s firstborn.
verses.explainer: These are the names of the Israelites who came to Egypt: Reuben, Jacob's firstborn.

verses.text: The sons of Reuben: Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi.
verses.explainer: Reuben's sons: Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi.

verses.text: The sons of Simeon: Jemuel, Jamin, Ohad, Jachin, Zohar, and Shaul the son of a Canaanite woman.
verses.explainer: Simeon's sons: Jemuel, Jamin, Ohad, Jachin, Zohar, and Shaul (son of a Canaanite woman).

verses.text: The sons of Levi: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari.
verses.explainer: Levi's sons: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. From these the Levitical priesthood will descend.

verses.text: The sons of Judah: Er, Onan, Shelah, Perez, and Zerah; but Er and Onan died in the land of Canaan. The sons of Perez were Hezron and Hamul.
verses.explainer: Judah's sons: Er, Onan, Shelah, Perez, and Zerah (Er and Onan died in Canaan). Perez's sons: Hezron and Hamul.

verses.text: The sons of Issachar: Tola, Puvah, Iob, and Shimron.
verses.explainer: Issachar's sons: Tola, Puvah, Jashub, and Shimron.

verses.text: The sons of Zebulun: Sered, Elon, and Jahleel.
verses.explainer: Zebulun's sons: Sered, Elon, and Jahleel.

verses.text: These are the sons of Leah, whom she bore to Jacob in Paddan Aram, with his daughter Dinah. All the souls of his sons and his daughters were thirty-three.
verses.explainer: These are Leah's sons, born to Jacob in Paddan-aram, plus Dinah. Thirty-three persons total.

verses.text: The sons of Gad: Ziphion, Haggi, Shuni, Ezbon, Eri, Arodi, and Areli.
verses.explainer: Gad's sons: Ziphion, Haggi, Shuni, Ezbon, Eri, Arodi, and Areli.

verses.text: The sons of Asher: Imnah, Ishvah, Ishvi, Beriah, and Serah their sister. The sons of Beriah: Heber and Malchiel.
verses.explainer: Asher's sons: Imnah, Ishvah, Ishvi, and Beriah, and their sister Serah. Beriah's sons: Heber and Malchiel.

verses.text: These are the sons of Zilpah, whom Laban gave to Leah, his daughter, and these she bore to Jacob, even sixteen souls.
verses.explainer: These are the sons of Zilpah, whom Laban gave to Leah. Sixteen persons.

verses.text: The sons of Rachel, Jacob’s wife: Joseph and Benjamin.
verses.explainer: Rachel's sons: Joseph and Benjamin.

verses.text: To Joseph in the land of Egypt were born Manasseh and Ephraim, whom Asenath, the daughter of Potiphera, priest of On, bore to him.
verses.explainer: In Egypt, Joseph's sons are Manasseh and Ephraim, born to him by Asenath.

verses.text: The sons of Benjamin: Bela, Becher, Ashbel, Gera, Naaman, Ehi, Rosh, Muppim, Huppim, and Ard.
verses.explainer: Benjamin's sons: Bela, Becher, Ashbel, Gera, Naaman, Ehi, Rosh, Muppim, Huppim, and Ard.

verses.text: These are the sons of Rachel, who were born to Jacob: all the souls were fourteen.
verses.explainer: These are Rachel's descendants: fourteen persons.

verses.text: The son of Dan: Hushim.
verses.explainer: Dan's son: Hushim.

verses.text: The sons of Naphtali: Jahzeel, Guni, Jezer, and Shillem.
verses.explainer: Naphtali's sons: Jahzeel, Guni, Jezer, and Shillem.

verses.text: These are the sons of Bilhah, whom Laban gave to Rachel, his daughter, and these she bore to Jacob: all the souls were seven.
verses.explainer: These are the sons of Bilhah, whom Laban gave to Rachel. Seven persons.

verses.text: All the souls who came with Jacob into Egypt, who were his direct offspring, besides Jacob’s sons’ wives, all the souls were sixty-six.
verses.explainer: All the persons belonging to Jacob who came into Egypt, his direct descendants (not counting his sons' wives), were sixty-six.

verses.text: The sons of Joseph, who were born to him in Egypt, were two souls. All the souls of the house of Jacob, who came into Egypt, were seventy.
verses.explainer: With Joseph's two sons born in Egypt, the total house of Jacob in Egypt is seventy. The number seventy has symbolic significance -- it matches the seventy nations of Genesis 10, suggesting that Israel is a microcosm of all humanity.

verses.text: He sent Judah before him to Joseph, to show the way before him to Goshen, and they came into the land of Goshen.
verses.explainer: Jacob sends Judah ahead to Joseph to prepare the way to Goshen.

verses.text: Joseph prepared his chariot, and went up to meet Israel, his father, in Goshen. He presented himself to him, and fell on his neck, and wept on his neck a good while.
verses.explainer: Joseph prepares his chariot and goes up to Goshen to meet his father Israel. He presents himself, falls on his neck, and weeps on his neck a long time. The reunion Jacob thought would never happen.

verses.text: Israel said to Joseph, “Now let me die, since I have seen your face, that you are still alive.”
verses.explainer: Israel says to Joseph: 'Now let me die, since I have seen your face and know that you are still alive.' The grief of twenty-two years is finally released.

verses.text: Joseph said to his brothers, and to his father’s house, “I will go up, and speak with Pharaoh, and will tell him, ‘My brothers, and my father’s house, who were in the land of Canaan, have come to me.
verses.explainer: Joseph tells his brothers: 'I will go up and tell Pharaoh that my family has come from Canaan.'

verses.text: These men are shepherds, for they have been keepers of livestock, and they have brought their flocks, and their herds, and all that they have.’
verses.explainer: He instructs them to tell Pharaoh they are keepers of livestock.

verses.text: It will happen, when Pharaoh summons you, and will say, ‘What is your occupation?’
verses.explainer: When Pharaoh asks their occupation, they should say they have been keepers of livestock from their youth.

verses.text: that you shall say, ‘Your servants have been keepers of livestock from our youth even until now, both we, and our fathers:’ that you may dwell in the land of Goshen; for every shepherd is an abomination to the Egyptians.”
verses.explainer: This will ensure they settle in Goshen, because shepherds are an abomination to the Egyptians. Joseph uses Egyptian prejudice strategically to secure the best land for his family while keeping them separate from Egyptian culture.

explainer.summary: Joseph presents his brothers and his father to Pharaoh. Jacob blesses Pharaoh -- the smaller blesses the greater, a quiet assertion of covenant dignity. The family settles in Goshen. Meanwhile, the famine devastates Egypt, and Joseph acquires all the money, livestock, and eventually the land itself for Pharaoh in exchange for grain. Only the priests' land is exempted. Jacob lives seventeen years in Egypt and, near death, makes Joseph swear to bury him in Canaan.
themes: Jacob's desire to be buried in Canaan
explainer.contextInBook: The family is established in Egypt, but the land promise remains. Jacob's insistence on burial in Canaan keeps the Promised Land in view even as they settle comfortably in a foreign country.

verses.text: Then Joseph went in and told Pharaoh, and said, “My father and my brothers, with their flocks, their herds, and all that they own, have come out of the land of Canaan; and behold, they are in the land of Goshen.”
verses.explainer: Joseph tells Pharaoh: 'My father and brothers, with their flocks, herds, and all they possess, have come from Canaan. They are in the land of Goshen.'

verses.text: From among his brothers he took five men, and presented them to Pharaoh.
verses.explainer: Joseph takes five of his brothers and presents them to Pharaoh.

verses.text:
Pharaoh said to his brothers, “What is your occupation?”
They said to Pharaoh, “Your servants are shepherds, both we, and our fathers.”
verses.explainer: Pharaoh asks: 'What is your occupation?' They answer: 'Your servants are shepherds, as our fathers were.'

verses.text: They said to Pharaoh, “We have come to live as foreigners in the land, for there is no pasture for your servants’ flocks. For the famine is severe in the land of Canaan. Now therefore, please let your servants dwell in the land of Goshen.”
verses.explainer: They ask to sojourn in the land, as there is no pasture in Canaan because of the famine. They request Goshen.

verses.text: Pharaoh spoke to Joseph, saying, “Your father and your brothers have come to you.
verses.explainer: Pharaoh tells Joseph: 'Your father and brothers have come to you. The land of Egypt is before you. Settle them in the best of the land.'

verses.text: The land of Egypt is before you. Make your father and your brothers dwell in the best of the land. Let them dwell in the land of Goshen. If you know any able men among them, then put them in charge of my livestock.”
verses.explainer: Pharaoh adds: 'If you know any capable men among them, put them in charge of my livestock.' The family is welcomed and employed.

verses.text: Joseph brought in Jacob, his father, and set him before Pharaoh, and Jacob blessed Pharaoh.
verses.explainer: Joseph brings Jacob before Pharaoh, and Jacob blesses Pharaoh. A wandering Hebrew nomad blesses the most powerful ruler in the world. The lesser does not bless the greater; the covenant-bearer blesses the monarch.

verses.text: Pharaoh said to Jacob, “How many are the days of the years of your life?”
verses.explainer: Pharaoh asks: 'How many are the years of your life?'

verses.text: Jacob said to Pharaoh, “The days of the years of my pilgrimage are one hundred thirty years. Few and evil have been the days of the years of my life, and they have not attained to the days of the years of the life of my fathers in the days of their pilgrimage.”
verses.explainer: Jacob answers: 'The years of my sojourning are 130. Few and evil have been the years of my life, and they have not attained to the years of my fathers.' Jacob sees his life as brief and difficult compared to Abraham (175) and Isaac (180). His honesty is disarming.

verses.text: Jacob blessed Pharaoh, and went out from the presence of Pharaoh.
verses.explainer: Jacob blesses Pharaoh and goes out. He blesses Pharaoh again -- bookending the encounter with blessing.

verses.text: Joseph placed his father and his brothers, and gave them a possession in the land of Egypt, in the best of the land, in the land of Rameses, as Pharaoh had commanded.
verses.explainer: Joseph settles his family in the land of Rameses (Goshen), giving them property in the best of the land.

verses.text: Joseph nourished his father, his brothers, and all of his father’s household, with bread, according to their families.
verses.explainer: Joseph provides food for his father, his brothers, and all his father's household, according to the number of their dependents.

verses.text: There was no bread in all the land; for the famine was very severe, so that the land of Egypt and the land of Canaan fainted by reason of the famine.
verses.explainer: There is no food in all the land, for the famine is very severe. Egypt and Canaan languish.

verses.text: Joseph gathered up all the money that was found in the land of Egypt, and in the land of Canaan, for the grain which they bought: and Joseph brought the money into Pharaoh’s house.
verses.explainer: Joseph gathers all the money in Egypt and Canaan in exchange for grain and brings it to Pharaoh's house.

verses.text: When the money was all spent in the land of Egypt, and in the land of Canaan, all the Egyptians came to Joseph, and said, “Give us bread, for why should we die in your presence? For our money fails.”
verses.explainer: When the money runs out, all Egypt comes to Joseph: 'Give us food! Why should we die?'

verses.text: Joseph said, “Give me your livestock; and I will give you food for your livestock, if your money is gone.”
verses.explainer: Joseph says: 'Give your livestock, and I will give you food in exchange.' A barter economy.

verses.text: They brought their livestock to Joseph, and Joseph gave them bread in exchange for the horses, and for the flocks, and for the herds, and for the donkeys: and he fed them with bread in exchange for all their livestock for that year.
verses.explainer: They bring their horses, flocks, herds, and donkeys. Joseph feeds them for their livestock that year.

verses.text: When that year was ended, they came to him the second year, and said to him, “We will not hide from my lord how our money is all spent, and the herds of livestock are my lord’s. There is nothing left in the sight of my lord, but our bodies, and our lands.
verses.explainer: The next year they come again: 'Our money is gone, our herds belong to my lord. We have nothing left but our bodies and our land.'

verses.text: Why should we die before your eyes, both we and our land? Buy us and our land for bread, and we and our land will be servants to Pharaoh. Give us seed, that we may live, and not die, and that the land won’t be desolate.”
verses.explainer: They offer: 'Buy us and our land in exchange for food. We will be servants to Pharaoh.' Famine drives people to surrender everything.

verses.text: So Joseph bought all the land of Egypt for Pharaoh, for every man of the Egyptians sold his field, because the famine was severe on them, and the land became Pharaoh’s.
verses.explainer: Joseph buys all the land of Egypt for Pharaoh. Every Egyptian sells his field because the famine is severe.

verses.text: As for the people, he moved them to the cities from one end of the border of Egypt even to the other end of it.
verses.explainer: Joseph moves the people into the cities from one end of Egypt to the other. A massive relocation program.

verses.text: Only he didn’t buy the land of the priests, for the priests had a portion from Pharaoh, and ate their portion which Pharaoh gave them. That is why they didn’t sell their land.
verses.explainer: Only the priests' land he does not buy, because priests have a fixed allotment from Pharaoh and eat from that allotment.

verses.text: Then Joseph said to the people, “Behold, I have bought you and your land today for Pharaoh. Behold, here is seed for you, and you shall sow the land.
verses.explainer: Joseph tells the people: 'I have bought you and your land for Pharaoh. Here is seed for you; sow the land.'

verses.text: It will happen at the harvests, that you shall give a fifth to Pharaoh, and four parts will be your own, for seed of the field, for your food, for them of your households, and for food for your little ones.”
verses.explainer: At harvest, a fifth goes to Pharaoh; four-fifths are for seed and food. A 20 percent tax -- relatively moderate by ancient standards.

verses.text: They said, “You have saved our lives! Let us find favor in the sight of my lord, and we will be Pharaoh’s servants.”
verses.explainer: The people say: 'You have saved our lives. We will be servants to Pharaoh.'

verses.text: Joseph made it a statute concerning the land of Egypt to this day, that Pharaoh should have the fifth. Only the land of the priests alone didn’t become Pharaoh’s.
verses.explainer: Joseph establishes a law: a fifth of the produce goes to Pharaoh. Only the priests' land is exempt. This law remains in effect.

verses.text: Israel lived in the land of Egypt, in the land of Goshen; and they got themselves possessions therein, and were fruitful, and multiplied exceedingly.
verses.explainer: Israel settles in Goshen, acquires property, and is fruitful and multiplies greatly. The creation mandate continues.

verses.text: Jacob lived in the land of Egypt seventeen years. So the days of Jacob, the years of his life, were one hundred forty-seven years.
verses.explainer: Jacob lives seventeen years in Egypt. He lives to be 147.

verses.text: The time came near that Israel must die, and he called his son Joseph, and said to him, “If now I have found favor in your sight, please put your hand under my thigh, and deal kindly and truly with me. Please don’t bury me in Egypt,
verses.explainer: As death approaches, Jacob calls Joseph and says: 'Put your hand under my thigh and promise me: do not bury me in Egypt.'

verses.text:
but when I sleep with my fathers, you shall carry me out of Egypt, and bury me in their burying place.”
He said, “I will do as you have said.”
verses.explainer: Jacob says: 'Carry me out of Egypt and bury me in my fathers' burial place.' Even in death, Jacob belongs to the Promised Land.

verses.text: He said, “Swear to me,” and he swore to him. Israel bowed himself on the bed’s head.
verses.explainer: Joseph swears. Israel bows in worship on the head of his bed. The old patriarch, near death, worships God and secures his connection to the land of promise.

explainer.summary: Jacob adopts Joseph's two sons, Ephraim and Manasseh, as his own -- giving Joseph a double portion through them. When Joseph brings them for Jacob's blessing, Jacob crosses his hands, placing his right hand (the hand of the greater blessing) on Ephraim, the younger. Joseph objects, but Jacob insists: the younger will be greater. The pattern of the younger over the older -- the defining pattern of Genesis -- continues to the end.
themes: The crossed hands and the reversal of primogeniture
explainer.contextInBook: Jacob's adoption of Ephraim and Manasseh gives Joseph two tribal portions in the Promised Land instead of one. The crossed-hands blessing is Jacob's final act of choosing the younger over the older -- the pattern of his own life.

verses.text: After these things, someone said to Joseph, “Behold, your father is sick.” He took with him his two sons, Manasseh and Ephraim.
verses.explainer: After these things, Joseph is told: 'Your father is ill.' He takes his two sons, Manasseh and Ephraim, and goes to Jacob.

verses.text: Someone told Jacob, and said, “Behold, your son Joseph comes to you,” and Israel strengthened himself, and sat on the bed.
verses.explainer: Jacob is told: 'Your son Joseph has come to you.' Israel strengthens himself and sits up in bed.

verses.text: Jacob said to Joseph, “God Almighty appeared to me at Luz in the land of Canaan, and blessed me,
verses.explainer: Jacob tells Joseph: 'God Almighty appeared to me at Luz (Bethel) in Canaan and blessed me.'

verses.text: and said to me, ‘Behold, I will make you fruitful, and multiply you, and I will make of you a company of peoples, and will give this land to your offspring after you for an everlasting possession.’
verses.explainer: He recounts the promise: 'I will make you fruitful, multiply you, make you a company of peoples, and give this land to your offspring as an everlasting possession.'

verses.text: Now your two sons, who were born to you in the land of Egypt before I came to you into Egypt, are mine; Ephraim and Manasseh, even as Reuben and Simeon, will be mine.
verses.explainer: Jacob says: 'Your two sons, born to you in Egypt before I came, are mine. Ephraim and Manasseh shall be mine, as Reuben and Simeon are.' He formally adopts them, elevating them to tribal status.

thenVsNow.then: Adoption in the ancient Near East was a legal act that gave the adopted children full inheritance rights. By adopting Ephraim and Manasseh, Jacob gives Joseph the double portion normally reserved for the firstborn.
thenVsNow.now: The tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh become two of the twelve tribes of Israel, replacing Joseph and Levi (who receive no territorial inheritance due to their priestly role) in the tribal allotments.

verses.text: Your issue, whom you become the father of after them, will be yours. They will be called after the name of their brothers in their inheritance.
verses.explainer: Any children born to Joseph after these two will be counted under Ephraim and Manasseh's names for inheritance purposes.

verses.text: As for me, when I came from Paddan, Rachel died by me in the land of Canaan on the way, when there was still some distance to come to Ephrath, and I buried her there on the way to Ephrath (also called Bethlehem).”
verses.explainer: Jacob mentions Rachel's death near Bethlehem with evident grief. Even at 147, the loss of Rachel still hurts.

verses.text: Israel saw Joseph’s sons, and said, “Who are these?”
verses.explainer: Jacob sees Joseph's sons and asks: 'Who are these?'

verses.text:
Joseph said to his father, “They are my sons, whom God has given me here.”
He said, “Please bring them to me, and I will bless them.”
verses.explainer: Joseph says: 'They are my sons, whom God has given me here.' Jacob says: 'Bring them to me that I may bless them.'

verses.text: Now the eyes of Israel were dim for age, so that he couldn’t see. He brought them near to him; and he kissed them, and embraced them.
verses.explainer: Jacob's eyes are dim with age. Joseph brings the boys near, and Jacob kisses and embraces them.

verses.text: Israel said to Joseph, “I didn’t think I would see your face, and behold, God has let me see your offspring also.”
verses.explainer: Jacob says: 'I never expected to see your face, and now God has let me see your children also.' Joy beyond imagination.

verses.text: Joseph brought them out from between his knees, and he bowed himself with his face to the earth.
verses.explainer: Joseph removes the boys from Jacob's knees and bows with his face to the earth.

verses.text: Joseph took them both, Ephraim in his right hand toward Israel’s left hand, and Manasseh in his left hand toward Israel’s right hand, and brought them near to him.
verses.explainer: Joseph positions the boys: Ephraim at Jacob's left, Manasseh at Jacob's right (so Jacob's right hand would naturally fall on the firstborn).

verses.text: Israel stretched out his right hand, and laid it on Ephraim’s head, who was the younger, and his left hand on Manasseh’s head, guiding his hands knowingly, for Manasseh was the firstborn.
verses.explainer: Jacob crosses his hands, putting his right hand on Ephraim's head (the younger) and his left on Manasseh's (the firstborn). He does this knowingly.

verses.text: He blessed Joseph, and said, “The God before whom my fathers Abraham and Isaac walked, the God who has fed me all my life long to this day,
verses.explainer: Jacob blesses Joseph: 'The God before whom my fathers Abraham and Isaac walked, the God who has been my shepherd all my life long to this day...'

verses.text: the angel who has redeemed me from all evil, bless the lads, and let my name be named on them, and the name of my fathers Abraham and Isaac. Let them grow into a multitude upon the earth.”
verses.explainer: '...the angel who has redeemed me from all evil, bless the boys.' Jacob calls God his shepherd and redeemer. After a life of struggle, he trusts the God who guided him.

verses.text: When Joseph saw that his father laid his right hand on the head of Ephraim, it displeased him. He held up his father’s hand, to remove it from Ephraim’s head to Manasseh’s head.
verses.explainer: Joseph sees that Jacob has placed his right hand on Ephraim and is displeased. He tries to move Jacob's hand to Manasseh.

verses.text: Joseph said to his father, “Not so, my father; for this is the firstborn; put your right hand on his head.”
verses.explainer: Joseph says: 'Not this way, my father; this one is the firstborn. Put your right hand on his head.'

verses.text: His father refused, and said, “I know, my son, I know. He also will become a people, and he also will be great. However, his younger brother will be greater than he, and his offspring will become a multitude of nations.”
verses.explainer: Jacob refuses: 'I know, my son, I know. Manasseh too shall become a people. But his younger brother shall be greater.' The pattern of Genesis: the younger is chosen over the older. Isaac over Ishmael, Jacob over Esau, now Ephraim over Manasseh.

verses.text: He blessed them that day, saying, “In you will Israel bless, saying, ‘God make you as Ephraim and as Manasseh’” He set Ephraim before Manasseh.
verses.explainer: Jacob blesses them: 'By you Israel will pronounce blessings, saying: God make you as Ephraim and as Manasseh.' He puts Ephraim before Manasseh.

verses.text: Israel said to Joseph, “Behold, I am dying, but God will be with you, and bring you again to the land of your fathers.
verses.explainer: Jacob tells Joseph: 'I am about to die, but God will be with you and bring you back to the land of your fathers.' The land promise is passed on.

verses.text: Moreover I have given to you one portion above your brothers, which I took out of the hand of the Amorite with my sword and with my bow.”
verses.explainer: Jacob gives Joseph one mountain slope (shechem) more than his brothers, which he took from the Amorites with sword and bow. This may refer to the land near Shechem (33:19) and establishes Joseph's special inheritance.

explainer.summary: On his deathbed, Jacob gathers all twelve sons and speaks over each of them -- some with blessing, some with rebuke, all with prophetic weight. Reuben loses his preeminence for sleeping with Bilhah. Simeon and Levi are scattered for the Shechem massacre. Judah receives the lion's share: the scepter will not depart from his tribe until one comes to whom it belongs. Joseph receives the longest and most lavish blessing. Jacob commands burial in Machpelah, draws up his feet, and dies.
themes: Prophetic blessings over the twelve tribes
Jacob's death and the land promise
explainer.contextInBook: Jacob's blessings shape the future of the twelve tribes. The Judah oracle (49:8-12) is one of the most important messianic passages in the Old Testament. These words echo through the rest of biblical history.

wordStudies.translatedAs: One of the most debated words in Genesis
wordStudies.meaning: One of the most debated words in Genesis. Possible meanings include 'the one to whom it belongs,' 'he whose it is,' or a reference to the town of Shiloh. Traditionally understood as a messianic reference: the scepter remains with Judah until the ultimate king arrives.

verses.text: Jacob called to his sons, and said: “Gather yourselves together, that I may tell you that which will happen to you in the days to come.
verses.explainer: Jacob calls his sons: 'Gather yourselves, that I may tell you what shall happen to you in days to come.' These are prophetic blessings that look far beyond the individuals to the tribes they will become.

verses.text:
Assemble yourselves, and hear, you sons of Jacob.
Listen to Israel, your father.
verses.explainer: 'Assemble and listen, sons of Jacob; listen to Israel your father.'

verses.text:
“Reuben, you are my firstborn, my might, and the beginning of my strength;
excelling in dignity, and excelling in power.
verses.explainer: Reuben: 'My firstborn, my might, the firstfruits of my strength.' The firstborn should have had preeminence.
But 'unstable as water, you shall not have preeminence, because you went up to your father's bed.' Reuben's sin with Bilhah (35:22) costs him the firstborn's rights.

verses.text:
“Simeon and Levi are brothers.
Their swords are weapons of violence.
verses.explainer: Simeon and Levi: 'Weapons of violence are their swords.' Jacob recalls the Shechem massacre.

verses.text:
My soul, don’t come into their council.
My glory, don’t be united to their assembly;
for in their anger they killed men.
In their self-will they hamstrung cattle.
verses.explainer: 'Let my soul not enter their council.' Jacob distances himself from their violence.

verses.text:
Cursed be their anger, for it was fierce;
and their wrath, for it was cruel.
I will divide them in Jacob,
and scatter them in Israel.
verses.explainer: 'I will divide them in Jacob and scatter them in Israel.' Simeon is eventually absorbed into Judah. Levi becomes the scattered priestly tribe -- a scattering transformed into a vocation.

verses.text:
“Judah, your brothers will praise you.
Your hand will be on the neck of your enemies.
Your father’s sons will bow down before you.
verses.explainer: Judah: 'Your brothers shall praise you.' The name Judah (yehudah) is related to 'praise' (yadah). His brothers will bow before him.

verses.text:
Judah is a lion’s cub.
From the prey, my son, you have gone up.
He stooped down, he crouched as a lion,
as a lioness.
Who will rouse him up?
verses.explainer: 'Judah is a lion's cub.' The lion becomes the symbol of Judah's tribe and, later, of David's dynasty and Jesus Christ ('the Lion of the tribe of Judah').

verses.text:
The scepter will not depart from Judah,
nor the ruler’s staff from between his feet,
until he comes to whom it belongs.
To him will the obedience of the peoples be.
verses.explainer: The scepter shall not depart from Judah, nor the ruler's staff from between his feet, until tribute comes to him; and to him shall be the obedience of the peoples. This is one of the most important messianic prophecies in the Old Testament. Royal authority belongs to Judah until the ultimate ruler arrives.

thenVsNow.then: This oracle establishes Judah as the royal tribe. David, a descendant of Judah, becomes Israel's greatest king. The promise extends beyond David to an ultimate figure to whom all peoples give obedience.
thenVsNow.now: Christians identify 'Shiloh' or 'the one to whom it belongs' with Jesus Christ, the descendant of Judah who receives the obedience of all nations. This verse is central to messianic theology.

verses.text:
Binding his foal to the vine,
his donkey’s colt to the choice vine;
he has washed his garments in wine,
his robes in the blood of grapes.
verses.explainer: He ties his donkey to the vine and his colt to the choice vine. He washes his garments in wine. The imagery is of such abundance that wine is used for laundry and vines for hitching posts.

verses.text:
His eyes will be red with wine,
his teeth white with milk.
verses.explainer: His eyes are darker than wine, his teeth whiter than milk. Judah's territory will be lavishly prosperous.

verses.text:
“Zebulun will dwell at the haven of the sea.
He will be for a haven of ships.
His border will be on Sidon.
verses.explainer: Zebulun: 'Shall dwell at the shore of the sea and be a haven for ships.' Zebulun's territory will be near the coast.

verses.text:
“Issachar is a strong donkey,
lying down between the saddlebags.
verses.explainer: Issachar: 'A strong donkey lying down between the sheepfolds.' Issachar is strong but passive.

verses.text:
He saw a resting place, that it was good,
the land, that it was pleasant.
He bows his shoulder to the burden,
and becomes a servant doing forced labor.
verses.explainer: He sees that rest is good and the land pleasant, so he bows his shoulder to bear and becomes a servant at forced labor.

verses.text:
“Dan will judge his people,
as one of the tribes of Israel.
verses.explainer: Dan: 'Shall judge his people as one of the tribes of Israel.' Dan's name means 'judge.'

verses.text:
Dan will be a serpent on the trail,
an adder in the path,
That bites the horse’s heels,
so that his rider falls backward.
verses.explainer: Dan shall be a serpent in the way, a viper by the path, that bites the horse's heels so the rider falls backward. Dan will be cunning and effective despite his small size.

verses.text: I have waited for your salvation, Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Jacob interjects: 'I wait for your salvation, O LORD.' A prayer amid the blessings.

verses.text:
“A troop will press on Gad,
but he will press on their heel.
verses.explainer: Gad: 'Raiders shall raid him, but he shall raid at their heels.' Gad's territory (east of the Jordan) will face constant border attacks.

verses.text:
“Asher’s food will be rich.
He will produce royal dainties.
verses.explainer: Asher: 'His food shall be rich, and he shall yield royal delicacies.' Asher's coastal territory will produce fine food.

verses.text:
“Naphtali is a doe set free,
who bears beautiful fawns.
verses.explainer: Naphtali: 'A doe let loose that bears beautiful fawns.' Freedom and beauty characterize Naphtali.

verses.text:
“Joseph is a fruitful vine,
a fruitful vine by a spring.
His branches run over the wall.
verses.explainer: Joseph: 'A fruitful bough by a spring, his branches run over the wall.' Joseph's blessing is the longest and most extravagant.

verses.text:
The archers have severely grieved him,
shot at him, and persecuted him:
verses.explainer: Archers fiercely attacked him, shot at him, and harassed him. Joseph's suffering is acknowledged.

verses.text:
But his bow remained strong.
The arms of his hands were made strong,
by the hands of the Mighty One of Jacob,
(from there is the shepherd, the stone of Israel),
verses.explainer: But his bow remained steady, his arms agile, by the hands of the Mighty One of Jacob, by the name of the Shepherd, the Rock of Israel. God sustained him through every trial.

verses.text:
even by the God of your father, who will help you;
by the Almighty, who will bless you,
with blessings of heaven above,
blessings of the deep that lies below,
blessings of the breasts, and of the womb.
verses.explainer: The God of your father who helps you, God Almighty who blesses you with blessings of heaven above, blessings of the deep below, blessings of the breasts and of the womb.

verses.text:
The blessings of your father have prevailed above the blessings of your ancestors,
above the boundaries of the ancient hills.
They will be on the head of Joseph,
on the crown of the head of him who is separated from his brothers.
verses.explainer: The blessings of your father surpass the blessings of my ancestors, up to the bounties of the everlasting hills. May they be on the head of Joseph, on the brow of the prince among his brothers.

verses.text:
“Benjamin is a ravenous wolf.
In the morning he will devour the prey.
At evening he will divide the plunder.”
verses.explainer: Benjamin: 'A ravenous wolf; in the morning devouring the prey, in the evening dividing the spoil.' Benjamin's tribe will be fierce warriors. King Saul and the apostle Paul both come from Benjamin.

verses.text: All these are the twelve tribes of Israel, and this is what their father spoke to them and blessed them. He blessed everyone according to his blessing.
verses.explainer: These are the twelve tribes of Israel, each blessed with a blessing suitable to him. The blessings are prophetic -- they describe not just individuals but the national destinies of their tribes.

verses.text: He instructed them, and said to them, “I am to be gathered to my people. Bury me with my fathers in the cave that is in the field of Ephron the Hittite,
verses.explainer: Jacob commands: 'Bury me with my fathers in the cave in the field of Ephron the Hittite.'

verses.text: in the cave that is in the field of Machpelah, which is before Mamre, in the land of Canaan, which Abraham bought with the field from Ephron the Hittite as a burial place.
verses.explainer: The cave of Machpelah, east of Mamre, in Canaan -- the field Abraham bought from Ephron.

verses.text: There they buried Abraham and Sarah, his wife. There they buried Isaac and Rebekah, his wife, and there I buried Leah:
verses.explainer: There they buried Abraham and Sarah, Isaac and Rebekah. There Jacob buried Leah. The family tomb.

verses.text: the field and the cave that is therein, which was purchased from the children of Heth.”
verses.explainer: The field and cave were purchased from the Hittites.

verses.text: When Jacob finished charging his sons, he gathered up his feet into the bed, and yielded up the spirit, and was gathered to his people.
verses.explainer: When Jacob finishes commanding his sons, he draws up his feet into the bed, breathes his last, and is gathered to his people. The third patriarch dies. His body will return to Canaan, as he requested.

explainer.summary: Joseph weeps over his father, has him embalmed according to Egyptian custom, and leads a massive funeral procession to Canaan to bury Jacob in the cave of Machpelah. After the burial, the brothers fear that Joseph will now take revenge. Joseph delivers the theological climax of the entire book: 'You meant evil against me, but God meant it for good, to bring it about that many people should be kept alive.' Joseph dies at 110, is embalmed in Egypt, and makes his family swear to carry his bones back to the Promised Land when God visits them.
themes: Mourning and burial in the Promised Land
The greatest statement of providence in the Bible
Joseph's death and the hope of return
explainer.contextInBook: Genesis ends not with arrival in the Promised Land but with a coffin in Egypt. The story is unfinished. The promise remains. The bones of Joseph point forward to the Exodus, when God will visit his people and bring them home.

verses.text: Joseph fell on his father’s face, wept on him, and kissed him.
verses.explainer: Joseph falls on his father's face, weeps over him, and kisses him. The physical grief is unreserved.

verses.text: Joseph commanded his servants, the physicians, to embalm his father; and the physicians embalmed Israel.
verses.explainer: Joseph commands his physicians to embalm Israel. The Egyptian embalming process takes forty days.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Embalming', 'explanation': "The Egyptians were master embalmers, using a seventy-day process involving removal of internal organs, natron salt drying, and wrapping in linen. Jacob receives this royal treatment, showing Joseph's status and his father's honor."}

verses.text: Forty days were fulfilled for him, for that is how many the days it takes to embalm. The Egyptians wept for him for seventy days.
verses.explainer: Egypt mourns Jacob for seventy days -- only two days less than the standard mourning period for a Pharaoh. Jacob is honored as near-royalty.

verses.text: When the days of weeping for him were past, Joseph spoke to the house of Pharaoh, saying, “If now I have found favor in your eyes, please speak in the ears of Pharaoh, saying,
verses.explainer: After the mourning period, Joseph asks Pharaoh's household to intercede: 'My father made me swear to bury him in Canaan.'

verses.text: ‘My father made me swear, saying, “Behold, I am dying. Bury me in my grave which I have dug for myself in the land of Canaan.” Now therefore, please let me go up and bury my father, and I will come again.’”
verses.explainer: Joseph relays Jacob's dying wish: 'In my tomb that I hewed out for myself in Canaan, there shall you bury me.'

verses.text: Pharaoh said, “Go up, and bury your father, just like he made you swear.”
verses.explainer: Pharaoh says: 'Go up and bury your father, as he made you swear.'

verses.text: Joseph went up to bury his father; and with him went up all the servants of Pharaoh, the elders of his house, all the elders of the land of Egypt,
verses.explainer: Joseph goes up with all the servants of Pharaoh, the elders of his household, and all the elders of Egypt.

verses.text: All the house of Joseph, his brothers, and his father’s house. Only their little ones, their flocks, and their herds, they left in the land of Goshen.
verses.explainer: All Joseph's household, his brothers, and his father's household go -- only the children and livestock remain in Goshen.

verses.text: There went up with him both chariots and horsemen. It was a very great company.
verses.explainer: Chariots and horsemen accompany them -- a very great company. It is a royal funeral procession.

verses.text: They came to the threshing floor of Atad, which is beyond the Jordan, and there they lamented with a very great and severe lamentation. He mourned for his father seven days.
verses.explainer: At the threshing floor of Atad beyond the Jordan, they mourn with great and grievous lamentation for seven days.

verses.text: When the inhabitants of the land, the Canaanites, saw the mourning in the floor of Atad, they said, “This is a grievous mourning by the Egyptians.” Therefore its name was called Abel Mizraim, which is beyond the Jordan.
verses.explainer: The Canaanites see the mourning and say: 'This is a grievous mourning by the Egyptians.' They name the place Abel-mizraim ('mourning of Egypt').

verses.text: His sons did to him just as he commanded them,
verses.explainer: Jacob's sons do as he commanded them.

verses.text: for his sons carried him into the land of Canaan, and buried him in the cave of the field of Machpelah, which Abraham bought with the field, as a possession for a burial site, from Ephron the Hittite, near Mamre.
verses.explainer: They carry him to Canaan and bury him in the cave of Machpelah, the field east of Mamre that Abraham bought from Ephron the Hittite. Three generations of patriarchs now rest in the one piece of the Promised Land they owned.

verses.text: Joseph returned into Egypt—he, and his brothers, and all that went up with him to bury his father, after he had buried his father.
verses.explainer: After the burial, Joseph returns to Egypt with his brothers and everyone who went with him.

verses.text: When Joseph’s brothers saw that their father was dead, they said, “It may be that Joseph will hate us, and will fully pay us back for all the evil which we did to him.”
verses.explainer: Joseph's brothers fear: 'What if Joseph holds a grudge and pays us back for all the wrong we did to him?' The brothers have not fully grasped the depth of Joseph's forgiveness.

verses.text: They sent a message to Joseph, saying, “Your father commanded before he died, saying,
verses.explainer: They send word to Joseph: 'Your father commanded before he died...'

verses.text: ‘You shall tell Joseph, “Now please forgive the disobedience of your brothers, and their sin, because they did evil to you.”’ Now, please forgive the disobedience of the servants of the God of your father.” Joseph wept when they spoke to him.
verses.explainer: 'Say to Joseph: Please forgive the transgression of your brothers and their sin, because they did evil to you.' They add: 'Please forgive the transgression of the servants of the God of your father.' Joseph weeps when they speak to him.

verses.text: His brothers also went and fell down before his face; and they said, “Behold, we are your servants.”
verses.explainer: His brothers come and fall before him: 'We are your servants.' The dream of 37:7 is fulfilled completely.

verses.text: Joseph said to them, “Don’t be afraid, for am I in the place of God?
verses.explainer: Joseph says: 'Do not fear, for am I in the place of God?' Vengeance belongs to God, not to Joseph. He refuses to usurp God's prerogative.

verses.text: As for you, you meant evil against me, but God meant it for good, to bring to pass, as it is today, to save many people alive.
verses.explainer: Joseph delivers the theological summary of the entire book: 'As for you, you meant evil against me, but God meant it for good, to bring it about that many people should be kept alive, as they are today.' Two intentions existed simultaneously: human evil and divine good. God did not cause the evil but sovereignly directed it toward a redemptive outcome.

thenVsNow.then: Joseph's statement is the most sophisticated theology of providence in the Old Testament. It affirms three things simultaneously: the brothers' real guilt, God's real sovereignty, and the good that emerged from the evil. None cancels out the others.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is perhaps the most quoted passage from Genesis. It is cited in times of suffering as assurance that God can bring good from evil. It does not explain why evil happens but affirms that God is never defeated by it.

verses.text: Now therefore don’t be afraid. I will nourish you and your little ones.” He comforted them, and spoke kindly to them.
verses.explainer: Joseph reassures them: 'Do not fear. I will provide for you and your little ones.' He speaks kindly to them. The forgiveness is complete and practical.

verses.text: Joseph lived in Egypt, he, and his father’s house. Joseph lived one hundred ten years.
verses.explainer: Joseph lives in Egypt with his father's family. He lives 110 years -- the Egyptian ideal lifespan, considered a sign of divine favor.

verses.text: Joseph saw Ephraim’s children to the third generation. The children also of Machir, the son of Manasseh, were born on Joseph’s knees.
verses.explainer: Joseph sees Ephraim's children to the third generation. Machir, Manasseh's son, has children born on Joseph's knees (adopted by Joseph).

verses.text: Joseph said to his brothers, “I am dying, but God will surely visit you, and bring you up out of this land to the land which he swore to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob.”
verses.explainer: Joseph tells his brothers: 'I am about to die, but God will surely visit you and bring you up out of this land to the land he swore to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.' The deathbed prophecy points forward to the Exodus.

verses.text: Joseph took an oath from the children of Israel, saying, “God will surely visit you, and you shall carry up my bones from here.”
verses.explainer: Joseph makes the sons of Israel swear: 'God will surely visit you, and you shall carry up my bones from here.' His bones are a pledge -- a physical reminder that Egypt is not home. The Promised Land awaits.

verses.text: So Joseph died, being one hundred ten years old, and they embalmed him, and he was put in a coffin in Egypt.
verses.explainer: Joseph dies at 110 years old. He is embalmed and placed in a coffin in Egypt. Genesis ends not with a triumphant arrival in the Promised Land but with a coffin in Egypt and a promise unfulfilled. The story demands a sequel. That sequel is Exodus.
